title: Security: Add bounds checks to prevent integer underflow in packet size validation
body: Summary The `on_spawn_receive`, `on_delta_receive`, and `on_sync_receive` functions perform unsigned arithmetic (`p_buffer_len - ofs`) that can underflow if `ofs` exceeds `p_buffer_len`. A malicious client can craft packets that bypass size validation, leading to out-of-bounds memory access. Issue Validation Issue validated via code inspection. Impact A malicious client can send malformed spawn/sync/delta packets that bypass validation checks, potentially causing: - Out-of-bounds memory reads - Server crash (DoS) - Potential information disclosure Reproduction 1. Client connects to Godot multiplayer server 2. Client sends spawn/sync/delta packet with `p_buffer_len` smaller than expected `ofs` offset 3. Arithmetic `p_buffer_len - ofs` underflows to ~4 billion 4. Size checks pass when they should not 5. Subsequent buffer access reads out of bounds Fix Added explicit bounds checks (`ofs > p_buffer_len || ...`) before each unsigned subtraction to ensure the offset never exceeds the buffer length before performing safe arithmetic. Files Changed - `modules/multiplayer/scene_replication_interface.cpp`

title: Fix icons and code completion colors when following system theme
body: This change fixes the font color and some icons colors in the code completion dropdown menu when the theme is changed due to a change of the system theme. This also fixes 116697 Current behavior: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8d831517-5ede-432e-ab07-d055b2f179df With this change: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8d0d6be3-0a60-4d0e-ad68-4bf304b044d0 This fix include two changes : One as pointed out here https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116697issuecomment-3960993322 to clear and rebuild the icon cache on system theme change to fix the icon colors. Another change in `CodeTextEditor::_update_text_editor_theme()` to refresh the code completion font colors from the editor settings and resubmit the code completion suggestions with the correct font color if the dropdown is open when the theme change happens. (If the dropdown is not open, the correct font colors are still submitted the next time it is open anyway.)

title: Core: Restore legacy keyboard/mouse device IDs for old projects
body: Summary Restore device ID 0 for keyboard/mouse events when a legacy compatibility setting is enabled. Motivation Regression in 4.7-dev: keyboard/mouse events now default to device IDs 16/32, which breaks projects that relied on device=0. Fixes 118025. Technical overview - Add a project setting: input_devices/compatibility/legacy_keyboard_mouse_device_ids. - Auto-enable it for projects created before 4.7. - When enabled, normalize keyboard/mouse events to device=0 at input dispatch and in InputMap event storage. - Keep the new IDs as the default for new projects. Testing Not run (code change + unit test update only). AI disclosure This PR description was assisted by an AI tool. Breaking changes None. Default behavior stays the same for new projects.

title: Fix newly imported files not having existing UID assigned
body: Fixes 109636. Fixes 118038. This change fixes three issues with the `ItemAction::ACTION_FILE_ADD` case in `EditorFileSystem::_update_scan_actions`: 1. It currently does a `ResourceUID::get_id_path` call without checking if the UID actually exists first, causing needless `Unrecognized UID: [...]` errors. - To fix this, I've added a `ResourceUID::has_id` check. 2. It does not currently assign anything to `ia.new_file->uid` for the case where the UID exists on disk but isn't known to `ResourceUID`, which becomes a problem for `ResourceSaver::get_resource_id_for_path` when saving out any file that references this UID, since it relies on whatever is cached in `EditorFileSystemDirectory::files`, leading to the UID part of the reference being erroneously removed. - To fix this, I've added said assignments. 3. It does not currently store the UID in `ResourceUID` for the case where the UID exists on disk but isn't known to `ResourceUID`, which seemed weird/broken. - To fix this, I've added `ResourceUID::set_id`/`ResourceUID::add_id` calls for that case. --- _Disclaimer: AI tooling was used during the triage of these issues._

title: GDScript: Fix type deduction for functions without `return` statements
body: * Fixes 118011. * Resolves 118007. Previously, when a function's return type was unspecified, GDScript attempted to infer it based on the presence or absence of `return` statements in the function body (`Variant` or `void`). However, this is incorrect, since functions can be overridden in child classes, and abstract methods have no bodies at all. I think it's more correct to infer `Variant` in the case of an unspecified return type (or copy the return type from the parent method when overriding). There is one subtle point. If you explicitly specify the `Variant` type (or it's copied from the parent method), you must return some value. However, if the return type isn't specified, you aren't required to return a value. This internal flag (`GDScriptParser::DataType::type_source`) is implicitly copied, but it's not distinguishable in `MethodInfo` reflection and documentation (`Variant` is used regardless of the presence or absence of the flag). However, I don't think we need to distinguish between these two options (118007) - if a method returns a `Variant`, you can always use `return null` in code generation. In my opinion, the only problem is that reflection incorrectly reported the method as `void`. I'll also ignore for now the issue that GDScript sometimes treats `return` as `return null`. This can be somewhat complicated, which we can address later. See also https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/79363pullrequestreview-1598144070.

title: @abstract method metadata has incorrect return values
body: Tested versions Reproducible in Godot 4.5.2, 4.6.1, 4.7 System information Godot v4.7.dev3 - macOS Tahoe (26.3.1) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Metal (Forward+) - integrated Apple M1 Pro (Apple7) - Apple M1 Pro (10 threads) - 16.00 GiB memory Issue description The results of `get_method_list` contains incorrect return data for abstract methods. The `return.type` and `return.usage` fields are not set correctly. Although this is how the metadata has been historically, due to the changes in return type checks in 4.7 it has become an issue when dynamically generating code. When generating an implementation for the abstract method I must know whether or not I can include `return` clauses. Without correct return metadata I cannot reliably create wrappers. Workarounds It is possible for the user to give my tool information about how to create wrappers, but this has a number of drawbacks. The user could also create a local class that extends the abstract class, but this is tedious. title: Web Export: TARGET_NOT_SUPPORTED crash in Android Facebook in-app browser
body: Tested versions -4.6 stabil System information Android 16 - Samsung S23 Ultra - Godot 4.6 stabil Issue description OS: Android (Tested on Android 16) Browser: Android Facebook In-App Browser (WebView / Chrome 146) Issue description When trying to run a Godot 4 Web Export inside the Android Facebook App's built-in browser, the game crashes at loading. The console throws the following Emscripten-generated error at line 1 in `index.js`: `TARGET_NOT_SUPPORTED;if(currentSafariVersion<150200){throw new Error("This emscripten-generated code requires Safari v15.2.0 or newer...")}` **The underlying issue:** The Android Facebook In-App Browser has a very messy User-Agent string that includes both Chrome and Mobile Safari, along with Facebook's custom tags. Example User-Agent Mozilla/5.0 (Linux; Android 16; SM-S918B Build/BP2A.250605.031.A3; wv) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/4.0 Chrome/146.0.7680.168 Mobile Safari/537.36 [FB_IAB/FB4A;FBAV/554.0.0.57.70;IABMV/1;] Because of this UA string, Emscripten's browser detection gets confused. It detects the word "Safari", fails to parse the correct version due to the Facebook tags, assumes it's an outdated Safari version, and throws a fatal exception. **The Proof / Workaround:** The underlying Chromium WebView actually fully supports all required modern features (like Wasm exception handling). **If I manually open the exported `index.js` and delete the `if(currentSafariVersion<150200){...}` check, the game runs perfectly and flawlessly inside the Android Facebook browser!** *(Note: The game also runs perfectly in the iOS Facebook in-app browser out of the box, as the iOS WebKit User-Agent is parsed correctly by Emscripten. The issue is strictly isolated to Android's FB browser UA).* This means we are losing a massive amount of Android mobile players simply due to a false positive browser detection regex in the Emscripten glue code. Steps to reproduce 1. Export any Godot 4.6-stable project to Web. 2. Host it on a server. 3. Post it on Facebook. 4. Open the link on an Android device using the Facebook App. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A - This applies to any blank Godot 4 web export.

title: Rework copy-pasting section/category values
body: Another follow-up to 111469 (is there anything left from the original code? 🤔) Copying section/category values had a major flaw cause by how the properties were copied. The inspector relied on Object's `get_property_list()`, which could return list that does not exactly match what is displayed in the inspector (due to various special cases). The code to extract properties belonging to sections/categories was a bit hacky and reliable. This PR changes it to happen completely on the inspector side. The properties will now register themselves in the parent section/category, so when copying values, they are copied from the list of EditorProperties, instead of object's properties. They are pasted into the EditorProperties too. This allowed to simplify the code (no more special cases for MultiNodeEdit etc.). - Fixes 116889 - Fixes the above bug for shader parameters (idk if there is open issue) - Fixes being able to paste section values into read-only properties - Fixes errors and resetting values when property name is not in clipboard (regression from 117600)

title: Disable auto-translation of plugin names

It's useful if you include a custom translation in your plugin, but auto-translation is not always desirable if your plugin name happens to be a word that exists in editor translation. The option would need to be stored in the plugin config though.

title: GDScript: Fix inconsistent heap-use-after-free on exit
body: Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117975 We can access an already freed script when clearing dependencies between scripts. It can happen because sometimes the reference to the base script can be the last reference and at the same time the next one in the `script_list`. So we first advance list to the next node and then free the script in the `Ref<GDScript> scr` destructor. To fix it I choose to manually clear the base script before moving to the next node to allow it to remove itself from the `script_list` if it needs to. Tbh, not sure how often it happens, but it does happen in my project and in the mrp at least.

title: Fix color correction not applying in mobile renderer without glow
body: Fixes 117890 . Problem: color correction wasn't being applied in forward mobile backend unless glow was enabled Cause: "using_subpass_post_process" was only disabled when glow, auto exposure, DOF, or canvas background were active. Color correction was missing from this condition, so when only color correction was enabled, the post-process pass was skipped entirely. Fix: Added `environment_get_color_correction_enabled()` to the condition so the post-process pass runs correctly when color correction is active.

title: Fix property drag activating from inspector preview widgets
body: When clicking and dragging inside a 3D preview (mesh or material) in the inspector, `EditorProperty::get_drag_data()` unconditionally initiates a property name drag. This causes a "forbidden" cursor to appear and the property label to be visually selected, even though the user is interacting with the preview widget. This adds bounds checks in `get_drag_data()` to reject drags that originate inside `bottom_child_rect` (the preview/bottom editor area) or to the right of the property label text (the content/value area). **Steps to reproduce (before fix):** 1. Add a `MeshInstance3D` node and assign any mesh (e.g. `BoxMesh`) 2. In the inspector, click the mesh resource to expand it 3. Click and drag inside the 3D mesh preview 4. Observe: the "Mesh" property label gets selected and a forbidden cursor appears **After fix:** Dragging inside the preview only rotates the mesh, as expected. Dragging the property label still works normally.

title: GPU Particles 2D subemitters not inheriting the parent velocity
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: v4.7-dev3; v4.7.dev.gh-113509 [375321054]; v4.7.dev.gh-117909 [499756d28] - Not Reproducible in: v4.7-dev2; v4.6.2-RC1; and earlier versions. System information Godot v4.7.dev3 - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+, Mobile) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.9186) - AMD Ryzen 7 5700X 8-Core Processor (16 threads) - 31.88 GiB memory Issue description 2D sub emitter particles are not inheriting the parent's velocity, neither using their own, they just stay in their spawn location. - Disabling "Keep Velocity" makes the sub-particles fallback to their velocity, as expected. - Re-enabling keeps using their velocity settings instead of using the parent's. - Reopening the editor resets the sub-particles to staying in place. I tested with Velocity, Gravity and Accelerations, none worked. This issue seem to be a regression, as it happens on v4.7-Dev3, but not on v4.7-Dev2 and earlier. The problem should be somewhere from PR 113509 and earlier, as that was where I noticed it for the first time. I also tested the Artifacts from 117909, but the issue still persists. Expected behavior The sub-particles should inherit their parent's velocity at the moment they spawn. Steps to reproduce The reproduction project has the "main_2d" scene that shows the issue, even in the editor. To test with a new project: 1) Create a 2D GPU particle (A) with some velocity or acceleration. 2) Create a 2D GPU particle (B) with visibly different velocity/acceleration. 3) Make B a Sub Emitter of A. 4) Enable Process Material > Sub Emitter > Keep Velocity. 5) B should follow A's velocity, but instead keeps it's own or stays in place. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) GPUParticle2D.SubEmitter.Inherit.Velocity.zip

title: GDScript: Fix parameter hint highlighting for vararg annotations
body: Fixes 117927. When typing arguments for vararg annotations like `@export_enum`, the parameter hint highlighting was incorrect — it stopped highlighting after the last declared parameter index, even though the annotation accepts additional arguments. **Changes in `_make_arguments_hint`:** - For vararg annotations: the last declared parameter now displays with a `...` prefix (e.g., `...names: String`) and stays highlighted for all argument indices >= the last parameter index. - For non-annotation vararg methods (e.g., `print()`): behavior is unchanged, still using the existing `...args: Array` fallback. <details> <summary>Before</summary> For `@export_enum("A", "B", "C")`: - Typing 1st arg `"A"` → `names: String` highlighted ✅ - Typing 2nd arg `"B"` → no parameter highlighted ❌ - Typing 3rd arg `"C"` → no parameter highlighted ❌ </details> <details> <summary>After</summary> For `@export_enum("A", "B", "C")`: - Typing 1st arg `"A"` → `...names: String` highlighted ✅ - Typing 2nd arg `"B"` → `...names: String` highlighted ✅ - Typing 3rd arg `"C"` → `...names: String` highlighted ✅ </details>

title: GDScript: Improve error message for static/non-static function conflicts
body: Fixes 117912. When a child class defines a function with the same name as a parent's static function (or vice versa), the error messages were not specific enough about the static/non-static mismatch. **Changes:** 1. In `check_class_member_name_conflict`: When a member name conflicts with a parent class's static function, the error now says `The member "X" already exists in parent class Y as a static function.` instead of the generic `The member "X" already exists in parent class Y.` 2. In `resolve_function_signature`: Split the validation into two cases: - **Static mismatch**: Shows a specific error like `The function "X" is not static, but the parent function of the same name is static.` - **Signature mismatch**: Shows the existing signature comparison error (unchanged behavior). <details> <summary>Before</summary> </details> <details> <summary>After</summary> </details> **Tests added:** - `override_static_function_with_non_static.gd/.out` - `override_non_static_function_with_static.gd/.out` - `variable_overloads_superclass_static_function.gd/.out`

title: Fix frozen kinematic RigidBody3D notifying children of unchanged transform
body: Fixes 117852. When a `RigidBody3D` is frozen in kinematic mode, `_sync_body_state` is still called every physics frame. Previously, it unconditionally called `set_global_transform()`, which triggers `NOTIFICATION_TRANSFORM_CHANGED` on all children — even when the transform hasn't actually changed. This adds a guard using `is_equal_approx()` to skip the `set_global_transform()` call when the transform is unchanged, preventing spurious notifications to children. This follows the same pattern used in `CollisionObject3D::_on_transform_changed`.

title: Fix SplineIK crash cases
body: 1. An out-of-bounds access occurred when there was only one point and it was closer than the first segment. I added the case where there is only one point as an OR condition to the abort case in the calculation for the first segment. Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117724. 2. Because the first segment had a higher priority than the `ended` flag, there were cases where the first segment was incorrectly searched after `ended`, which caused an infinite loop (see MRP). I swapped the priority of the flags. Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/114564.

title: Fix AnimationTree AnimationPlayerEditor broken
body: - Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117941 I assumed that this code that used to prevent the AnimationPlayerEditor from showing over the AnimationTreeEditor was fine, but it seems that something has changed since it was last used and now there are issues. https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/220b0b2f74d8e089481b140c42a42992a76dd6fc/editor/animation/animation_player_editor_plugin.cppL2490-L2491 The logic in `AnimationPlayerEditorPlugin::make_visible` needs to run even if opening the AnimationTreeEditor over it, so I removed the check. This also makes the AnimationPlayerEditor show if it is not in the same dock as the AnimationTreeEditor, which is now possible with some of the bottom dock reworks. But then there needs to be a different way to make sure that the AnimationTreeEditor opens over it. (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115947) So I changed the order in `EditorData::get_handling_sub_editors`. The order of sub editor plugins (non main screen ones) was changed in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/37022 as an addition to the main editor plugins, in order to give user plugins priority. But all sub editor plugins are edited, so to give users priority they should actually be edited after the default ones so they can show over them. This change also makes plugins edit in the order they are added, the AnimationTreeEditor is added after the AnimationPlayerEditor, so it now shows over it. Generally, I think more general plugins are added before more specific ones, so it should make sense to edit the more specific ones after. I don't think any other sub editors edit the same object and care about order where this would be an issue. Here are all plugins in the order that they are added to EditorData's `editor_plugins` in a default build. I'm still looking through it to see if there would be any issues from changing it to be in reverse order to forward order for non-main screens, but I haven't found any issues. <details><summary>All EditorPlugins in order</summary> 1. title: Handle Tree's root and foreign item drops better
body: This PR fixes three minor problems that appeared after 112993, including one regression. - First, it closes 117895. - Second, the new reliance of Tree on `selected_item` for the improved rejection of recursive child drops as well as for the unchanged position drop indicator necessitated a way to distinguish a drop originating from a foreign Tree vs an internal one, since foreign drops could be negated by an unrelated selection in the receiving Tree. `dragging_within_self` accomplishes this, and is the most minimal way of doing so without a TreeItem-based refactor of drop data. - Third, dragging a node from SceneTreeDock "onto" a folder in FileSystemDock is supposed to request that folder as the drop location in the "Save As..." prompt. However, due to FileSystemDock implicitly "selecting" the hovered TreeItem when receiving this kind of drop, our prior negation of dropping onto self was preventing this, setting "res://" as the location, while sibling drops still worked fine. This has been fixed, and is a side-effect of the last change. In the future, it would most probably be best to change FileSystemDock's foreign drop behaviour into a superficial theme type for its drop indicator. Further follow ups entail a deeper dive into individual Tree implementations, and while that may lead to these changes being whittled down in the future, they are not hacky and should generally benefit all Trees.

title: Enum var, typing a period, ERROR Parameter "type" is null
body: Tested versions Reproducible in: v4.7.dev3.official [60fff00a6] Not reproducible in: v4.7.dev2.official [778cf54da] v4.6.2.rc2.official [638b2f1e9] v4.6.1.stable.official [14d19694e] System information Godot v4.7.dev3 - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - GeForce GTX 550 Ti - Intel(R) Core(TM) i3-2100 CPU @ 3.10GHz (4 threads) - 11.95 GiB memory Issue description Typing a period after an enum variable in Godot 4.7.dev3 shows: > ERROR: core/object/class_db.cpp:1210 - Parameter "type" is null. There's no error in the last three Godot versions before 4.7.dev3. title: Fix behavior of `window_is_hdr_output_supported` for Wayland and adjust warnings.
body: What problem(s) does this PR solve? From a look at the code it appears that `DisplayServerWayland::window_is_hdr_output_supported` returns `true` when the rendering device does not support HDR output. This behaviour is incorrect and does not match the behaviour of other display servers. What is the rationale for the approach used in this PR? The problem is resolved by simply adding a check to the `DisplayServerWayland::window_is_hdr_output_supported` function so it matches other display servers. In addition to this change, I have adjusted some errors to be warnings and improved some of the warning messages. Finally, because the functions that enable HDR output do not check if the rendering device supports HDR output, I have slightly changed the `window_request_hdr_output` logic to fail to request HDR output when `rendering_device` is `nullptr`. I don't believe this is ever possible, but this is safer logic that ensures its impossible for HDR output to be successfully requested when the rendering device does not support HDR output. Was generative AI (LLM AI) used to create a portion of this PR? No. Are there any parts of this PR that you are uncertain of or require special attention from reviewers? No, but please double check that my logic statements are correct.

title: Fix a bug where shared skeleton modification stacks caused errors and wierd behavior
body: Description This PR refactors the `SkeletonModificationStack2D` and its associated modifiers to decouple runtime state from the Resource configuration. The Problem: Currently, `SkeletonModificationStack2D` (a Resource) stores volatile runtime data—specifically the `skeleton` pointer, `is_setup` flag, and `editor_gizmo_dirty` status—as class member variables. Because Resources are shared by default in Godot, when multiple `Skeleton2D` instances share the same modification stack, they overwrite these variables every frame. This leads to: - "Drawing is only allowed inside _draw()" errors in the editor. - State corruption where one skeleton's logic is applied to another's bones. - Performance degradation and unpredictable behavior. The Fix: I implemented a state-passing pattern: - Created a `SkeletonModificationStackState2D` struct to hold all instance-specific runtime data. - Moved state ownership to the `Skeleton2D` node. Each node now owns its own unique `modification_stack_state`. - Updated the Stack and Modifier APIs to be stateless, accepting a reference to the node's state during `setup` and `execute` calls. This ensures that the logic remains shared (saving memory) while the data remains unique (ensuring instance safety). Users no longer need to manually set `resource_local_to_scene` to `true` to avoid these conflicts. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d2fc2b90-cd1e-4bb4-a063-c92eafe20f0c Tests performed: - Multi-Instance Test: Created a scene with 10 identical `Skeleton2D` instances sharing a single modification stack resource. Verified that no "Drawing" errors occur. - Ran all Unit-tests to ensure nothing was breaking because of the changes made. Notes: - This change is internal to the C++ engine logic - the GDScript-facing API remains compatible. - Documentation XMLs have been updated using doctool to reflect the architectural change. Fixes 117090

title: Fix unexpected node movements in `AnimationNodeBlendTree` after changing name
body: After renaming a node in the AnimationNodeBlendTree, the node could unexpectedly change its position and continue moving until the user interacted with the graph (clicking on the screen). This happens because the GraphNode state becomes desynchronized from the underlying BlendTree data after a rename operation, causing stale position updates to be applied. Fix this by preventing the node from updating while in this state, avoiding unintended position changes and ensuring stable behavior after renaming.

title: Fix infinite loop in debugger when script has parse error
body: If using Godot in a way that doesn't support stdin input (e.g. headless mode, or without the console wrapper on Windows), the interactive debugger gets stuck in an infinite loop when the --debug flag is used and a script has a parse error Fixes 117123 When a parse error occurs during script loading, debug_break_parse() invokes the local interactive debugger. The debugger's main loop calls get_stdin_string() which returns empty immediately when there is no interactive console attached, and feof(stdin) never evaluates to true in this scenario, preventing the quit branch from being reached and causing an infinite loop. The fix adds a check before entering the debugger loop in LocalDebugger::debug() that checks the stdin type, if it is not STD_HANDLE_CONSOLE then the error information is printed and the function returns early. Tested on Windows with and without --headless, with and without the console wrapper executable. The interactive debugger still works correctly when launched via the console wrapper. Claude was used to help me navigate and understand the codebase. All investigation, testing, and final decisions were made by me with help from the godot contributors chat (https://chat.godotengine.org/channel/new-contributors/thread/rgiMeto2TytKbxksu)

body: Tested versions 4.6.1 System information Godot v4.6.1.rc (1ae4d3b23) - Freedesktop SDK 24.08 (Flatpak runtime) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5080 (nvidia) - AMD Ryzen 7 9800X3D 8-Core Processor (16 threads) - 30.39 GiB memory Issue description The documentation claim that higher values of `OS.low_processor_usage_mode_sleep_usec` make the CPU usage lower, but the inverse seems to be true. A value of 100 makes the CPU usage drop to like 1%, and a value of 1,000,000 makes it spike to 100%. Even the default of 6900 seems like too much. Looking at CPU usage in htop running a linux export, however, at a value of 100 it's 20% CPU usage and 1,000,000 it drops to near 0, which is what I'd also expect of the web app. So at least for desktop it seems to be fine. It seems that changing `Engine.max_fps` will also spike the CPU to extreme levels. E.g. `Engine.max_fps = 60`, likely because they both contribute to `get_frame_delay()`. Steps to reproduce - Export the app for web or use the pre-exported one - Run the app (e.g. python -m http.server and open localhost:8000) - Open developer tools to monitor CPU usage - Tick the checkbox. CPU usage should spike dramatically. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) cpu_spike_project.zip The code being used for reference

title: OpenXR: Add eye-tracked foveation setting, and enable subsampled images by default
body: In theory, enabling eye-tracked foveated rendering (when it's available on the hardware) should always be a win, which is why we didn't originally add a setting for it. However, there is currently a bug in Pico's OpenXR runtime, where enabling it will cause a crash. It's Pico's bug and they'll fix it eventually, but in order to allow our users to work around it, this PR adds a setting for it which is enabled by default. This can allow developers to use feature tags to disable it on specific platforms, and then remove the workaround later after Pico fixes the underlying bug. Similarly, foveation using Vulkan subsampled images should almost always be a win too, but we already added a setting for it, just in case. :-) This PR changes that setting to also be enabled by default

title: Using CharacterBody3D move_and_collide() from _process() with Jolt physics sometimes fails to generate collision responses.
body: Tested versions v4.6.stable.official [89cea1439] v4.6.1.rc.custom_build [785d85b00] System information Godot v4.6.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Direct3D 12 (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3080 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.8115) - AMD Ryzen 7 3700X 8-Core Processor (16 threads) - 31.95 GiB memory Issue description When using CharacterBody3D's move_and_collide() called from _process(), sometimes the collision info returned will be null, even though there is a collision, particularly if there are multiple collisions and iterations within 1 frame. This doesn't seem to happen when done from _physics_process(), but due to performance issues (see https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/93184), I'm trying to stagger the character movement updates across multiple frames, and since there isn't a per-frame physics update (see https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/10015), I'm forced to use _process(). The bug only seems to happen with Jolt, not Godot physics. Image Red debug lines indicate collision. Note how when it starts going crazy, there's no collisions reported. Steps to reproduce Create a CharacterBody3D and handle its movement in _process() using multiple move_and_collide() calls in a frame if there is a collision (in order to handle sliding and redirecting). See MRP for code that does that. Create a level where the ceiling angles down into the floor so the player can wedge and collide with both ceiling and floor at the same time. Note that the move_and_collide returns null so the movement logic for handling collision fails to work properly and the character builds up crazy velocity and/or starts moving through the ceiling or floor. body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.6.1.stable - Not reproducible in: 4.5.stable System information Manjaro Linux amd64, Godot v4.6.1.stable.official [14d19694e], no sandboxing Issue description Normally Godot remembers custom editor colour theme settings when upgrading to a new minor version. When going from 4.5 to 4.6.1, however, it seems to forget, or force-reset them. I had 4.5 set to a "custom" interface theme/colour preset, with custom base and accent colours, and theme contrast. title: `FTI` - Fix property release updates
body: Ensures properties are correctly updated to the server when being removed from the property update list. Fixes 116567. Forward port of 117823. The update when removing a node from the property update list was being called (`fti_update_servers_property`), _however_ it was not actually updating the server thanks to an optimization that only updates when `needs_interpolating` is true. This PR fixes 2 bugs: * By using a stale flag, it ensures there is an update to the server following a pump when no new data is coming in. It either does this on the following frame, or the following tick, whichever comes first. * I noticed there was a subtle bug in the existing `||` condition for calling `interpolated()` - if the first statement returned true, the later ones never ran, and thus only the first property would be interpolated. * The solution here was to force each condition to run longhand, store in a temporary variable, then evaluate the `||` condition afterwards. * I'm actually evaluating _all_ the interpolated properties each time, ,even though usually only 3 are needed for each camera mode. This is slightly wasteful but there is usually only one camera at a time, so code clarity and brevity should be more important. Notes * This was a slight oversight, and hadn't appeared in testing because it is only obvious at high tick rates and most testing was done with low tick rates. * This is now more elegant than the original approach of forcing an update on releasing nodes from the property update list, and ensures server updates are kept to a minimum (1 in all cases). <!-- Please target the `master` branch. We will take care of backporting relevant fixes to older versions. Before submitting, please read our checklist for new contributors: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/introduction.htmlchecklist-for-new-contributors -->

title: Fix bottom dock always opening a tab on startup
body: When a dock doesn't have a tab open, the `dock__selected_tab_idx` key is omitted from the config file on save. However, when that key is fetched, a value of `0` is used as a fallback if it's not found, causing the first tab to always be opened. This PR changes the fallback value to `-1`, and skips the tab opening if the value is below `0`.

title: Add support for saving HDR screenshots to `Image.save_exr` functions.
body: What problem(s) does this PR solve? - Fixes 117795 What is the rationale for the approach used in this PR? `Image.save_exr` functions have been modified to add two new parameters: 1. `color_image: bool` - Set `color_image` to `true` when saving a color image, such as a screenshot. Negative values will be included when `color_image` is `false`, which may be useful for saving raw floating point data such as a lightmap that includes negative light information. - When `true`, negative values are clipped to `0.0` to give correct colour rendering for screenshots. 3. `max_linear_value` - Color component values in the resulting EXR file will not exceed `max_linear_value` if `max_linear_value` is not negative. - When saving screenshots of a project that uses HDR output, use `Window.get_output_max_linear_value` for `max_linear_value`. **Clipping negative values (`color_image == true`)** Internal to Godot, negative color values represent negative radiant energy for the purposes of lighting simulation. But for the purposes of CIE colorimetry, which is used for HDR image formats such as EXR, negative RGB values represent colors that are outside of the current RGB gamut and are positive RGB values in other color gamuts. For this reason, negative values should never be saved to EXR files that are intended to represent a color image such as a screenshot. This PR adds parameters to the existing `save_exr` and `save_exr_to_buffer` functions to allow saving of HDR screenshots. **New `max_linear_value` parameter** The new `max_linear_value` parameter matches `Window.get_output_max_linear_value()`. This means that producing an EXR image that is identical to the rendering presented by Godot is as simple as: With these changes, it is now possible to save an image that is identical to the image presented by HDR output: In the above renderings: the red, green, and blue rectangles shown on the bottom left corner contain negative values, such as those that may be created by negative lights. The colour sweep that comprises the majority of the render exceeds values of `1.0` on the right side. Was generative AI (LLM AI) used to create a portion of this PR? No. Are there any parts of this PR that you are uncertain of or require special attention from reviewers? - I haven't yet tested `SRC_FLOAT`. I don't know how to do this, but if it's possible to test this code path it should be done before merging. Anyone know how to?

title: HDR screenshots via `save_exr` do not match HDR output
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in Godot 4.7 dev 1 onward Underlying bug reproducible in all versions of Godot with `Image.save_exr`. System information Godot v4.7.dev2 - macOS Tahoe (26.3.1) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Metal (Forward+) - integrated Apple M4 Pro (Apple9) - Apple M4 Pro (14 threads) - 24.00 GiB memory Issue description When I use `get_window().get_texture().get_image().save_exr("screenshot.exr")` with a project that uses HDR output, the resulting EXR screenshot sometimes does not match what I see in my game window. There are two issues here: 1. The result of negative lights (meant to represent non-physical negative radiance for lighting simulations) are saved as negative colours (which represent out-of-gamut colours in the EXR file format rather than representing negative radiance). 2. Values above `Window.get_output_max_linear_value()` are saved to the EXR file, causing a different appearance than rendered through Godot. Steps to reproduce 1. Create a project with Mobile or Forward+ 2. Enable HDR output in the project using the steps found in the tutorial 3. Create a scene that has colour component values that are below `0.0` or higher than the Window's output max linear value 4. Save an EXR screenshot using: `get_window().get_texture().get_image().save_exr("screenshot.exr")` 5. View the EXR and the game window and notice that they appear different **Note:** This is best done on a Macbook because macOS has good native support of EXR and HDR output, but the problem can be seen on other operating systems with the correct image viewer. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) save-hdr-exr-bug.zip

title: GDExtension crash on macOS 26 (Tahoe): NSBundle bundleWithURL throws for non-bundle paths
body: Godot version 4.6.1-stable System information macOS 26.3.1 (Tahoe), Apple M4 Pro, ARM64 Issue description Godot crashes on startup when loading any GDExtension on macOS 26 (Tahoe). The crash occurs in `OS_MacOS::get_framework_executable()` (os_macos.mm line 371). The function calls `[NSBundle bundleWithURL:url]` for every library path, including `.dylib` files and non-bundle directories. On macOS 26, Apple changed `NSBundle` to throw an `NSException` instead of returning `nil` for invalid bundle paths. This causes an unhandled Objective-C exception that terminates Godot. Stack trace Root cause In `platform/macos/os_macos.mm`, `get_framework_executable()` unconditionally calls `[NSBundle bundleWithURL:]` at line 371 without checking if the path is actually a `.framework` bundle first. Suggested fix Wrap the `NSBundle` call in `@try/@catch`, or check if the path ends in `.framework` before calling `bundleWithURL:`: Steps to reproduce 1. Build any GDExtension (framework or dylib) for macOS 2. Install it in a Godot project 3. Open the project in Godot 4.6.1 on macOS 26 4. Godot crashes immediately Minimal reproduction project Any GDExtension will trigger this on macOS 26. The issue is in Godot's library loading code, not in the extension itself.

title: Only print to stdio before setup and during/after cleanup
body: Fixes 117789. This introduces a new `CoreGlobals::print_ready` global, which when disabled will force anything that goes through `_err_print_error`, `__print_line`, `__print_line_rich` or `print_error` to only print to the stdio streams (`stdout`/`stderr`). This lets us enable this flag when entering `Main::setup`, and disable it when entering `Main::cleanup`, allowing us to avoid all the problematic `ERR_*` calls during shutdown that would otherwise try to interact with `Object`/`Variant` machinery and whatever else that could have already been torn down at that point.

title: texture_sdf_normal can't be used in CanvasItem shaders without texture_sdf
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: v4.6.1.stable.steam [14d19694e] System information OS: Windows 11 - Godot: v4.6.1.stable (Compatibility renderer) - CPU: AMD Ryzen 7 7800X3D - GPU: GeForce RTX 5070 Ti Issue description If you want to read the SDF normals generated by LightOccluder2D nodes in a shader using texture_sdf_normal(), texture_sdf() needs to be explicitly used in the final shader output. title: Fix a crash in `Viewport` in the wild while running a game
body: I believe the crash is a stale-lifetime bug in 3D picking for bu 117775 Viewport::_process_picking() caches last_object / last_id for multiple queued input events at the same pointer position. If the first _input_event callback removes the picked CollisionObject3D or current Camera3D from the tree, the next queued event can still reuse that cached target and call _collision_object_3d_input_event(), which immediately does get_global_transform on an object that is no longer safe to query. The patch does four things: 1. It rejects captured 3D pick targets that are no longer is_inside_tree() before reuse at viewport.cpp (similar changes exist in the code): 2. It re-fetches the cached same-position object from ObjectDB, verifies it still matches the cached pointer, and verifies it is still in the tree before dispatching. 3. It skips _mouse_exit() for a hovered 3D object that has already left the tree at viewport.cpp. 4. It adds a final guard inside _collision_object_3d_input_event() so even a future caller cannot query transforms on removed nodes or cameras at viewport.cpp. - *Bugsquad edit:* This PR fixes 117775 <!-- Please target the `master` branch. We will take care of backporting relevant fixes to older versions. Before submitting, please read our checklist for new contributors: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/introduction.htmlchecklist-for-new-contributors -->

title: Godot ignores file permissions, overwrites read-only/protected files.
body: Tested versions Tested on: v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7b6], v4.5.stable.official [876b29033], v4.6.1.stable.official [14d19694e], System information Godot v4.6.1.stable - Fedora Linux 43 (KDE Plasma Desktop Edition) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 3 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - Mesa Intel(R) Iris(R) Xe Graphics (TGL GT2) - 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-11370H @ 3.30GHz (8 threads) - 62.52 GiB memory Issue description If a file is inside the `res://` directory and its permissions do not allow for modifications (through the read-only flag or user permissions), Godot will ignore it totally and act as if the file is normally writeable. This is a problem for versioning of binary files (.scn, .res, as well as any imported ones like .fbx or .png) using Perforce or Git with LFS locking. Making it difficult if not precarious to work in teams on a project. I'm not sure if the Godot Git plugin (https://github.com/godotengine/godot-git-plugin) addresses it. The last time I used it, it butchered my LFS setup. But regardless, it seems like the engine should respect read-only settings with no plugins. Additional info: - v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7b6] and v4.5.stable.official [876b29033] seem to change the permissions of the file. - v4.6.1.stable.official [14d19694e] seems to leave the file permissions as is, but still overwrites it. Steps to reproduce 1. Create any file in the godot res:// folder (for example a material). 2. Change it to be read-only either through the file system or a VCS. 3. Try to modify it in Godot. 4. The file will be modified, regardless of its permissions. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) read-only.zip

title: HTTPClient: Wait for available data in blocking mode
body: (Hopefully) Fixes 33479, fixes 35868 and high CPU usage when performing HTTP requests with `HTTPRequest` using threads. When using `HTTPRequest.request` with `use_threads` setting to true, which configures its `HTTPClient` to enable blocking mode internally, most of CPU time is wasted on reading zero byte from `StreamPeer::get_partial_data` loop in `HTTPClientTCP::_get_http_data`[[1]](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/65e73b3a5eec19e3269809b0f00e73a646723067/core/io/http_client_tcp.cppL747). Since `StreamPeer::get_data` cannot be used (reason already stated in the comment of `HTTPClientTCP::_get_http_data`), and `StreamPeer::get_partial_data` is a non-blocking method, meaning it returns immediately regardless of the bytes actually read, most of the time there are no data available even with very fast internet connection in a tight loop. This PR makes `HTTPClientTCP::_get_http_data` waiting for available bytes with `StreamPeerTCP::wait` before trying to read data from socket in blocking mode. title: Fix breakpoints resetting on external editor save
body: Currently, Godot-defined breakpoints disappear when a file is saved from an external editor. This fixes it by restoring the previous editor state upon reloading a file. Therefore, it also applies to bookmarks and folded sections, as they had the same issue and it was easier to fix all of them at once this way. This new behavior has better consistency, since previously the breakpoints were cleared, but only if the file was opened in Godot's script editor. Now, breakpoints are never cleared, which is also more similar to what external editors such as vscode do when they receive external changes. It also fixes a related error, which was triggered if you: 1. Had a file with breakpoint at line n 2. Closed its editor in Godot 3. Edited the file externally to have m < n lines 4. Reopened the editor. You would expect this error: `Index p_line = (n-1) is out of bounds (get_line_count() = m)` The fix is included in this PR as it was necessary for the other changes and is harmless. Fixes 116672.

title: [Accessibility] Fix `update_set_name` setting a space character as the node label when name and `name_extra_info` is empty
body: When both name and name_extra_info are empty, the resulting name is a single space character. Screen readers may then speak out the word 'Space' because it is one character (occurs with ORCA v46). Reproduction Set everything on empty on a control node. Then using espeak-ng and ORCA (tested with v46), focus on the node. The read-out will be "Space". ORCA will be silent when you set the name as a space ' '. Then ORCA recognizes 2 or more characters and treats it differently. Here is that branching code for ORCA: https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/orca/-/blob/main/src/orca/speechdispatcherfactory.pyL545 AI Use Claude helped me identify the problem and point me to the relevant code files in Godot, Accesskit, and ORCA. Claude's fix was naive and left trailing spaces. strip_edges() should take care of that. Background I need elements to be ignored by the screen reader because I have voice actors speaking the text. For nodes that are accounted for with a voice from a different source, a screen reader must remain silent. Currently the only way to do this is to set everything to an empty string and set role to ROLE_UNKNOWN. ROLE_CONTAINER would be better but this will not work with some screen readers (ORCA), it will read out as 'Section'. But that is a different bug. P.s. this is my first contribution and I'm figuring out how to do this. I just read that making a PR through github is generally not accepted unless it is a small fix. I deemed this pretty small and confined. But if desired I'll fork and fix it locally. <!-- Please target the `master` branch. We will take care of backporting relevant fixes to older versions. Before submitting, please read our checklist for new contributors: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/introduction.htmlchecklist-for-new-contributors -->

title: Significant performance cost to updating a shape position with JoltPhysics
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in 4.6.1 System information Godot v4.6.1.rc (c90bb52b3) - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7216) - 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-13900K (32 threads) - 63.78 GiB memory Issue description I have a rope physics library. Internally it generates N collision shapes for each N rope segment. The collision shape positions are updated on every frame as the rope moves. The performance for updating Jolt Shapes is approximately 100x worse than GodotPhysics3D Shapes. I have some logging code that traces the call times for different sections of my update callback: (see repro case below): Updating with GodotPhysics3D: Updating with JoltPhysics3D: _update_collision_shapes is a simple update loop that calls `body_set_shape_transform` on each shape in the physics body. In jolt, each call to set_shape_transform will perform an O(N) update of the entire shape set in `commit_shapes` via `build_shapes` -> `_try_build_shape`. This has a significant negative impact on updating shape performance. Steps to reproduce 1. Checkout my rope library at this hash directly into the addons folder of a godot 4.6.1 project. https://github.com/mikest/halyard/commit/4fd539823aff31eb1a4493f474dfa505b238b249 2. Build library with scons. 3. Open `addons/halyard/tests/lod_test.tscn` (or any of the test files with Rope in them.) 4. Select the Rope object in the file and verify that the Collision Layer & Mask under Simulation are both set to "1": 5. Open console, you should see performance data printed every 2-3 seconds. 6. Switch between GodotPhysics and JoltPhysics and compare the results. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) see steps to reproduce.

title: Fix GLES3 batching skipping rendering all items on specific buffer sizes
body: Fixes 117602. As far as I've tested, also: Fixes 105645. Fixes 107297. When adding last instance to the batch, it instantly starts another batch and resets the index to zero. So when the batched instance count was a multiple of the `max_instances_per_buffer` then because of the `if (index == 0)` check it would skip rendering all the batched instances. AFAICT a correct check is to ensure we're still at the first batch and that its instance count is zero.

title: LSP: Calculate simple string insertions on the server-side
body: Requires and includes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/114598 Supersedes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/87508 Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/86488 Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-vscode-plugin/issues/961 Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-vscode-plugin/issues/885 Fixes https://github.com/GDQuest/zed-gdscript/issues/31 Does NOT fix - https://github.com/godotengine/godot-vscode-plugin/issues/644 -> can be solved using the same approach but will require an extra PR if this one goes through If the language server only provides an `insertText` it is up to the client how this text is matched with existing code when inserting. The builtin editor does match quoted options with existing quotes, however external editors don't seem to generally do this. For such cases the LSP allows servers to calculate the insertion on the server side. They only need to provide the client with the desired range where the text should be placed and clients have to follow this range without doing any matching. This PR makes GDScript calculate the insertion of strings on the server side. It is not possible (or much more complex) to do this only in the LSP code, since it has very limited access to the AST. So instead this PR introduces the `TextEdit` concept into our own completion API and implements it directly in `gdscript_editor.cpp`. TODO: - [x] Use `_calculate_string_insertion` in all places that insert strings - [x] Fallback for old clients -> client capabilities (Nvmd: only `InsertReplaceEdit` is optional) - [x] Finalize internal representation on `ScriptLanguage::CodeCompletionOption`

title: BONE_INDICES values in shader incorrect when mesh has ARRAY_FLAG_USE_8_BONE_WEIGHTS set
body: Tested versions v4.6.stable.official [89cea1439] v4.3.stable.official [77dcf97d8] Seems to be the same in 4.3 and 4.6 System information Godot v4.6.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7602) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-7600 CPU @ 3.50GHz (4 threads) - 15.94 GiB memory Issue description With some meshes the values of BONE_INDICES and BONE_WEIGHTS (spatial shader built-ins) are not what I would expect. This seems to happen with meshes with 8 bone weights instead of 4. Here's an example of where I first encountered the problem. This shader is trying to use BONE_INDICES to highlight specific bones on the mesh. The shoes mesh works as expected, highlighting faces on one specific bone, but the suit mesh has faces all over the place. And I found the shoe mesh has four bone weights per vertex while the suit has eight. The eyeballs also worked correctly and had four bone weights, while the human skin mesh had eight and showed the same problems as the suit. Here's a screenshot from the minimal reproduction, where the cube is supposed to map one face of the cube to one bone. The one on the right uses four bone weights and displays correctly, the one on the left uses 8 bone weights and is striped. Because of the stripes that appear on two faces when they should be solid on one face, I suspect the values in BONE_INDICES are getting indexed as though there are still only four per vertex, so vertex0 gets the first four values, but vertex1 gets vertex0's next four values, and vertex2 gets vertex1's first four values, and so on. Further testing indicates the extra BONE_INDICES values aren't just the extras values passed, but includes 65,535 so for some reason a max value or a negative one gets inserted in there? And the BONE_WEIGHTS end up being all 0.0 For the purposes of my shader, I would be happy with getting the first four BONE_INDICES and BONE_WEIGHTS even when the mesh has eight, but getting all these wrong values breaks the shader. Steps to reproduce It comes up if you try to use the BONE_INDICES in a shader with a mesh that has ARRAY_FLAG_USE_8_BONE_WEIGHTS flagged. The minimal reproduction has two generated cubes that should be identical except for the number of bone weights. You can change the values of some instanced uniforms to show how Minimal reproduction project (MRP) bone_indices_test.zip

title: Change Tracy intern table to be thread-local
body: Fixes 117640. This simply makes the members of `TracyInternTable` non-static, and instead makes `TracyInternTable` itself a `static thread_local`, which allows us to remove the `TracyInternTable::mutex` that's causing so much contention with multi-threaded GDScript workloads. Having `TracyInternTable` itself be a static thread-local means we can also guarantee that proper cleanup happens before the program exits. This will of course multiply the memory normally used by this interning by the number of threads, but given how memory-heavy Tracy seems to be in its profiling anyway, this is likely a drop in the ocean. Here are the numbers I'm now seeing in the MRP from 117640, again with `target=template_release` and `production=yes`: Interestingly this even made the single-threaded case faster. I haven't looked into why exactly, but I have to assume it's related to the mutex somehow. `tracy::intern_source_location` still comes at a fairly heavy cost in this particular MRP though, as it's what's responsible for much of the remaining performance loss, but I'm unsure whether it's as much of a problem in real-world scenarios. --- *Disclaimer: AI tooling was used in the making of this change. Any code not physically typed out by myself has been carefully reviewed to the best of my ability.*

title: Contention within Tracy integration has severe effects on multi-threaded performance
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.7.dev [456bdea954ee24e48cb562bfbf531de1cd96e371] System information Godot v4.7.dev (456bdea95) - Fedora Linux 43 (Forty Three) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Single-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT (RADV GFX1201) - AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D 16-Core Processor (32 threads) - 60.39 GiB memory Issue description To put it simply, `TracyInternTable::mutex` (as seen here) causes some severe contention in function call-heavy multi-threaded GDScript. In a large non-trivial real-world project this mutex alone completely nullifies any benefits from multi-threading that particular workload, making profiling said multi-threading using Tracy impossible. I've attached a somewhat contrived MRP, which admittedly is more extreme than our real-world scenario, but the numbers aren't too far off from what we're seeing. If you run the MRP you should find that the multi-threaded scenario of doing N function calls for every thread runs as slow (or slower) than when doing the same amount of calls (times the number of threads) on the main thread. Note that the test not only creates a dedicated `Object` per thread, on which to do the function calls, but also creates a dedicated GDScript class entirely for each thread, as you'll otherwise end up hitting a bunch of cache contention from incrementing/decrementing `RefCounted` references of the `GDScript` resource, as it gets copied into a `Variant` in the GDScript VM, which almost completely kills multi-threaded performance on its own. Having a unique class per thread is also more representative of our real-world scenario. Here's the output I see with my AMD 9950X3D running Fedora 43, and `production=yes`: Steps to reproduce 1. Build Godot with `target=template_release` and `profiler=tracy`. 2. Export the MRP project with said release template. 3. Run the exported executable from command-line (with `--headless`). 4. Note how the reported scaling is less than 1x. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) tracy-function-overhead.zip --- *Disclaimer: AI tooling was utilized in the making of this MRP. Any code not physically typed out by myself has been carefully reviewed to the best of my ability.*

title: `Camera2D.position_smoothing_speed`: correct description and fix overshoot bug
body: In `Camera2D`, the property `position_smoothing_speed` has the following description: > Speed in pixels per second of the camera's smoothing effect when [member position_smoothing_enabled] is [code]true[/code]. This doesn't really make sense if we look at the observed behavior and looking at the code we can confirm it's indeed not a velocity at all. From Camera2D::get_camera_transform(): As you can see what we're doing is a simple lerp: `smoothed_pos = lerp(smoothed_pos, target_pos, position_smoothing_speed * delta)` So `position_smoothing_speed` is not in px/s but 1/s and does not determine speed or acceleration, it's just part of a ratio calculation. It determines what proportion (not pixels) of the distance between the smoothed_position and the target_position the camera will cover every tick (process or physics depending on configuration). Besides the incorrect description, the approach can cause different behavior, even big glitches depending on framerate: Any time `position_smoothing_speed * delta` ends up being higher than 1.0 we are extrapolating rather than interpolating. This is easy to trigger by `position_smoothing_speed` to a value higher than the max fps but it can also happen with reasonable values if time delta becomes high enough. For example, if the camera is set to update on process, and `position_smoothing_speed` is set to 60 then when delta is 1/120 the lerping factor is 0.5 so the camera moves half way to the target position each frame. If delta increases to 1/60 then the camera moves all the way to the target position, as if with no smoothing. So far so good. If the framerate drops to 30 a delta of 1/30 will result in a lerping factor of 2. That means the camera actually overshoots across the target position and ends at the same distance it was before, causing a constant oscillation. Any consistent drops in framerate that result in a lerping factor above 2 will completely break smoothing since the camera will overshoot more and more each frame as can be observed here: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c3112b61-c1b6-412a-88e0-fd3b5ee3e67c **What this PR does** This PR corrects the description of `position_smoothing_speed` to give users a more accurate intuition on what different values do and fixes the overshoot by clamping the calculated lerp factor to 1. This fixes the overshoot bug in a compatible manner. A more correct solution would be to use frame-independent interpolation such as exponential decay, as explained here: https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/tutorials/math/interpolation.htmlsmoothing-motion (see Note)

title: Fix timeline cursor following mouse during marker selection
body: When right-clicking an existing marker and then left-clicking an empty space on the timeline, and click another place on the timeline, the timeline cursor follow mouse without holding mouse. Reproducible in: 4.6.1.stable Not reproducible in: 4.5.1.stable Before fix: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2fe84dc3-50f4-420c-93a4-a0d5289d6aec After fix: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/baaa50f1-8ebf-442e-a86b-8de184f25f29 Similar to 117276, The bug is reproducible in 4.6.1.stable while not reproducible in 4.5.1.stable. The bug was introduced in commit 2a10291. I confirmed that 2a10291~1 doesn't have this problem. The cause of the bug is that `moving_selection_attempt` is set as true in `_try_select_at_ui_pos`, and `AnimationMarkerEdit` accept the mouse releasing event when `moving_selection_attempt` is true, so the timeline doesn't know left-mouse has been released. To solve this problem we can add a `moving_selection_attempt = false`. MRP mrp-timecursor-follow-jump.zip <!-- Please target the `master` branch. title: Revert "[.NET] Remove EFS update on reloading assemblies" but with deferred call
body: - Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116169 It turns out this call was needed after all, it ensures new scripts are included in the global class list by updating EFS for them. In my previous PR (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/108487) I must've only tested scripts that have been opened or loaded by the editor at some point so I didn't encounter the bug. This reverts the https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/108487 but with a deferred call to avoid reintroducing the bug that the PR fixed (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/104052). Updating EFS here is still too early, so we defer the call to ensure the type info is available.

title: SkeletonModification2DJiggle incorrect look_at direction when the parent Node2D is mirrored
body: Tested versions 4.6.0, 4.6.1dev and latest 456bdea954ee24e48cb562bfbf531de1cd96e371 System information Windows11 Issue description Bug description When using 2D skeleton animation, I mirror-flip the character via Node2D scale to play the opposite-direction animation. However, SkeletonModification2DJiggle does not handle Node2D mirroring correctly: the jiggle bones still point to the pre-mirror direction. The issue appears to come from look_at being calculated purely from global positions, ignoring mirrored transforms. Additionally, during sudden changes like mirror flip or teleport, the jiggle simulation becomes numerically unstable for a moment. A manual reset API would allow developers to reset the jiggle cache when needed, without changing default behavior. Expected behavior After mirroring, jiggle bones should correctly orient toward the target in the mirrored space. Actual behavior Jiggle bones keep pointing toward the pre-mirror direction. Screenshots Before mirror: After mirror (bug): Expected: Steps to reproduce 1. Create a 2D character with Skeleton2D/Bone2D and add a SkeletonModification2DJiggle to a bone chain. 2. Mirror the character by setting the parent Node2D scale to (-1, 1) (or any negative scale). Minimal reproduction project (MRP) test2dskeleton.zip

title: Dynamically check for 3D editor snapping state
body: This change simplifies the check for snapping when we're editing the transform of a `Node3D`. It should address godotengine/godot117497. *Bugsquad edit:* Fixes 117497. As of writing, we only update `Node3DEditorViewport::EditData::snap` in `Node3DEditorViewport::begin_transform`. This works well when the user toggles snapping before modifying a `Node3D`'s transform. That said, if the user is pressing and releasing the Ctrl/Cmd key while adjusting a transform, snapping won't necessarily enable/disable. ~~To address this, we update the state of `snap` in `Node3DEditorViewport::update_transform`. This allows the user to toggle snapping while adjusting a `Node3D`'s transform.~~ We now check the snapping state dynamically. See below. Notes No generative AI was used in the research, development, or testing of this PR's changes.

title: Crash or blank screen when TileMapLayer has exactly rendering/gl_compatibility/item_buffer_size tiles
body: Tested versions Reproduced in 4.6.0 mono and 1eb0374e5f3efa05d8a9914f482b894b5b1f6b9c mono. System information Godot v4.7.dev.mono (1eb0374e5) - Fedora Linux 43 (Workstation Edition) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 3 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce GTX 970 (nvidia; 580.126.18) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-6700 CPU @ 3.40GHz (8 threads) - 23.36 GiB memory Issue description A TileMapLayer with exactly as many tiles as the rendering/gl_compatibility/item_buffer_size setting can disappear, or in some cases crash. On default settings, a 128x128 grid triggers the bug. The MRP below causes the disappearing tiles. I haven't been able to reduce the crash to an MRP but I strongly suspect it's the same issue. Backtrace: It appears to hit a boundary condition in drivers/gles3/rasterizer_canvas_gles3.cpp, in `_render_items`. Specifically, the `index == 0` case is triggered when the batch size limit is hit exactly. In that case, `_add_to_batch` resets `index` to 0. Then the function skips rendering even though 16864 items should be rendered. It also skips some cleanup at the end of `_render_items`, which I suspect (but haven't confirmed) is the cause of the crash. Steps to reproduce There's also a sprite in the scene. If you toggle its visibility on and off, the tilemap's visibility is also toggled, demonstrating how the bug only occurs when the batch limit is exactly hit. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) tilemap-bug.zip

title: GDScript: Add warning for shadowing global constants
body: Description This PR adds a missing warning in GDScript when a local variable or property shadows a global constant (such as `PI`, `OK`, or `INF`). Previously, shadowing these global constants would happen silently, which could lead to confusing logic errors for users who expect to be using the built-in global values. Fixes Changes - In `gdscript_analyzer.cpp`, added a check against `GDScriptLanguage::get_singleton()->get_global_map()` within the `is_shadowing` validation logic. - Trigger a `SHADOWED_VARIABLE` warning if a match is found in the global scope. Validation Tested with the following script: ```gdscript var PI = 3.14 Now triggers a warning var OK = 0 Now triggers a warning

title: Rework TileSet editor proxy objects
body: Fixes 117406 This changes proxy objects used in TileSet editor from singular Object to RefCounted. Every time something is edited, a new proxy object is created specifically for this item, so undo actions are created properly for this specific item. The lifetime of proxy objects is managed by UndoRedo, so this shouldn't cause memory problems.

title: SCons: Fix support for custom.py and get_flags
body: Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/97436 Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/103860 The approach I picked is to rely on a native scons parsing, and to make it work some options were loaded first to configure the defaults for other options that will be loaded later. The options that needed to be loaded were the "platform" and alias options. The first downside is that the system is opt-in and you need to write the name of the option twice, for example here `opts.Add(BoolVariable("metal", "Enable...", env.get_default("metal", False)))` `"metal"` was written again. Another one is that you need to explicitly ask for a custom configured default. And if you add another alias or flags to platforms the options that were affected will need to be updated. As for other changes both platform's and module's `get_opts` now accepts `env` object and they can get default with overrides from it. I used module `inspect` to add backwards support and it needs Python 3.5... I think, it was hard to find the info about it, but it is lower than minimum supported Python version for Godot, so I used it. The final order that options override each other is: Normal defaults < Platform defaults < Alias defaults < custom.py arguments < Cmdline arguments Platform defaults were gotten from `get_flags()` and to keep the old behaviour platform defaults can still override the final option with `"auto"` value. The order of the calls to modules and platforms should be the same, except for the platform's `get_opts` that now gets called later. And editor's `_helper_module` now also gets loaded slightly later. Everything else that is not touched by options directly should be loaded in the same order as before. Maybe there is a better way than explicit opt-in with calling `env.get_default("name", False)`, but I kind of can't think of any that is actually nice to use. Edit: About what's wrong with current behaviour. Setting alias `"dev_mode"` or `"production"` will make it ignore the values from `custom.py` that are set by them. `"debug_symbols"` if used in `custom.py` will be always overriden by `"dev_build"` even the default `"dev_build"`, unless `"production"` is used then it is true. And platform specific flags always override `custom.py` if they exist. Here is a crude test of using `"dev_mode"`. Top command that is not visible was `scons` and the bottom is `scons dev_mode=yes` Also what I wrote about in the edit is what is this PR is fixing

title: Fix BlendTree appearing empty
body: An early return in `update_graph_immediately` can lock `graph_update_queued` as `true` forever, rendering `update_graph_immediately` impossible for `update_graph` to call again, due to its own early return when `graph_update_queued` is true, causing all BlendTrees to appear empty until the engine is restarted. This bug is very difficult to reproduce, but I'm positive I've encountered it twice since the optimisation PR. Hopefully a glance at the file makes this make sense.

title: Wayland: Only report HDR request failed warning if HDR was actually requested

title: Fix hint string parsing for nested dictionaries
body: Fixes 104259 Helps 107556 Changes the inspector's hint string parsing to only split on the first `;` instead of all instances which caused the nested hint strings to get decimated and not passed down correctly. This allows typed dictionaries to be nested as the value via property list manipulation. For easier to define nested collection support, see 107556 . This would also be fixed with the hint string parsing used in 112632, but I went with the minimal change while the other PR is in review. **Note**: This does not help defining a nested dictionary as a key, only a value. Supporting that kind of hint string would need some kind of grouping syntax to know which `;` belongs to which level of the nesting. This PR is only trying to improve the more common use case. title: Import: when file headers/metadata is inaccurate, Godot will freeze infinitely trying to import the file
body: Tested versions 4.6.1 System information Godot v4.6.1.stable - Windows 10 (build 19044) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Direct3D 12 (Forward+) - dedicated Intel(R) Iris(R) Xe Graphics (Intel Corporation; 27.20.100.9664) - 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-1165G7 @ 2.80GHz (8 threads) - 39.84 GiB memory Issue description When importing files which have headers (e.g., WAV files), Godot will loop forever trying to import the file if the internal data size is incorrect, while simultaneously blasting the console with things like: I think the proper thing to do here is to simply abort the import if a seek turns out to be invalid. Maybe display an alert dialog to make it quite clear to the user that a file is damaged/broken and as such is not available for use until repaired. An alternative is to repair the file and re-save on disk, but I have no idea how scalable that solution is because Godot supports a lot of import formats and trying to fix every possible problem that we may encounter sounds really difficult (although for all I know it could be easier than I realize). Although I encountered this on audio files, I imagine this could happen with any file format which has offsets or other size indications within it. Steps to reproduce 1. Create a new project or open an existing one. 2. Drop a broken .WAV file into the project directory. (I had about 500 of them, so that probably didn't help...). For broken files, the best way is to generate one and modify one of the chunks to report a size beyond the length of the file. 3. Godot will hang as it tries to import the file forever until forcefully terminated. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) I'm not entirely certain if this requires an MRP or not. It happened with a blank project for me. The most I did was drop a (disabled) add-on into it, but that add-on does not, as far as I know, have format importers. And even if it did, I hadn't enabled it when this happened.

title: Improve thread-safety of `Object` signals
body: Fixes 117396. > [!NOTE] > I would recommend looking at these changes with whitespace changes hidden, as there's a fair bit of indentation changes. As mentioned in 117396, interacting with signal connections across multiple threads is not currently thread-safe, despite claims to the contrary. `Object::signal_mutex` is mostly covering `this->signal_map`, but does not cover `target->connections` at all. This fixes that by replacing the `OBJ_SIGNAL_LOCK` macro and `_ObjectSignalLock` struct with a new `ObjectSignalLock` struct that optionally takes two objects instead of just one. This lets us lock the `signal_mutex` of both objects at the same time, and (more importantly) in the same order every time. The latter is done by simply "sorting" the mutexes by their memory address, since that should be stable for the lifetime of the object. Most of these changes were fairly simple and mechanical in places were we already had the two objects readily available, but a few were less obvious, so are worth elaborating: 1. `Object::~Object` - The scope of the lock when erasing incoming connections has been shrunk to only cover `target->connections`, since we need to lock the `target` mutex. This is technically less thread-safe, since `signal_map` isn't being covered properly, but frankly anything reading/mutating an `Object` currently being destroyed is problematic on so many more levels that this is a non-issue in practice. - There was a null check of `c.callable.get_object()` happening during the erasing of outgoing connections, which will always be `this`, so was removed. 2. `Object::_disconnect` - There was a null check of `slot->cE` happening before, which is already being done inside of `target_object->connections.erase`, so was removed. 3. `Object::emit_signalp` - The scope of the lock has been shrunk to only cover `signal_map` and not the one-shot disconnections. This would otherwise break the lock ordering, since `_disconnect` will internally try to lock both mutexes, but one of them would have (arbitrarily) already been locked. 4. `Object::_remove_user_signal` - Since we can't iterate our signal slots while also doing the double-locking, this method needs to be a two-phase thing. We do this by first (under a `this` lock) finding the `slot_map` of the signal, moving it out into a temporary, then erasing it. Then as a second phase we iterate over our now erased `slot_map` and double-lock as per usual. --- *Disclaimer: AI tooling was used in the planning and review of this change, as well as some minor edits. Any code not physically typed out by myself has been carefully reviewed to the best of my ability.*

title: Fix blend point setter can't restore the name in deprecated=no
body: - Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117490 The cause of the issue is that `name` is always empty in the following line: Ideally, `name` should be the primary key, but since it is more critical that a point cannot be created if `node` is null, we have no choice but to make `node` the primary key. For now, we will keep `node` as the primary key and insert the `itos` name key regardless of whether it is deprecated. If `name` is set, it will be immediately reset right after restoration, so the `itos` name will not be displayed. Conflicts may occur if blend_points without a `name` and blend_points with a `name` coexist within a single BlendSpace, but since such cases never happen normally (unless you edit the .tscn directly), this should not be a problem.

title: GDScript: Fix `find_function` failing when script has overindented blocks
body: Fixes 117416 When a GDScript source contains an overindented dictionary like: the tokenizer produces more DEDENT tokens than INDENT tokens at the top level. The `find_function` method tracks indentation with a simple counter that increments on INDENT and decrements on DEDENT, looking for `func` tokens at indent level 0. Because of the extra DEDENTs from the overindented block, the counter goes negative and never returns to 0, so no function declarations past that point are found. This causes the signal connection dialog to think the target function doesn't exist and attempt to add a duplicate, which breaks the script. The fix clamps the indent counter to never go below 0, so that top-level `func` declarations are always found regardless of indentation anomalies earlier in the source. This is safe because `find_function` only cares about finding top-level functions — a negative indent level is never meaningful in that context. **Steps to test:** 1. Create a scene with a script containing an overindented dictionary body as shown above, followed by a function definition. 2. Use the Signals dock to connect any signal to the existing function. 3. Verify that the editor correctly recognizes the existing function instead of creating a duplicate.
