title: Fix "Custom" anchor preset being ignored if the parent isn't a `Control`
body: A check was introduced in 70882 to fix `layout_mode` being inconsistently saved in scene files. Down the line, it caused the "Custom" anchor preset to be ignored if the child's parent is not a `Control` too, since `layout_mode` is not saved in those cases. This PR lowers the check's position to allow the custom mode for anchors to still be set regardless. Hopefully without bring back the original bug. Should fix 112472.

title: Build profile: Only allow classes derived from `Resource` and `Node` to be disabled
body: The editor profile builder dialog shows only classes derived from `Resource` and `Node` in the tree to be toggled. However, the option to generate a profile from the project adds classes that don't derive from those. This PR makes so that the behavior from project generation now matches with the dialog options. This PR also makes that the check to see if a class is editor-only uses the `APIType` instead of just checking if the name starts with "Editor". Fixes 117414.

title: [Apple Embedded] Fix static .a/.xcframework library loading in `open_dynamic_library`
body: Problem When building iOS with vulkan=no, GDExtension loading fails with ERR_CANT_OPEN. The static library fallback in open_dynamic_library sets path = String() and calls dlopen(""), which doesn't search the statically linked symbol table. The fix returns RTLD_SELF early instead, consistent with the existing empty-path case already handled in the function. Tested on iOS with vulkan=no.

title: Add UTF-8 encoding to svg file open in `platform_builders.py`
body: This PR fixes the build failure on Windows by explicitly specifying UTF-8 encoding when reading SVG files in platform/platform_builders.py, preventing the UnicodeDecodeError caused by the system default GBK encoding. When building Godot on Windows, the build process fails with a UnicodeDecodeError during the generation of SVG icon headers for platform exporters. This happens because platform/platform_builders.py reads SVG files without explicitly specifying UTF-8 encoding, causing Python to fall back to the system default encoding — which is GBK on Chinese Windows installations. Windows systems configured for Chinese locales use GBK as the system default encoding, while SVG files are universally stored in UTF-8 and often contain multi-byte special characters that cannot be decoded correctly by GBK. This encoding mismatch results in an immediate decoding failure and halts the build. The specific error message is as follows:

title: Silently stop all script debuggers if no debugging server is active
body: Different approach from 114457 (cc @kbieganski) Closes 111175 (cannot reproduce myself, but has received feedback from @tmilker it works) Instead of the original solution of always propagating the `stopped` signal even when no debugger session was active (breaks DAP since it expects the signal to be emitted once and when a session is active), this approach does so only if the debug server is valid or the debugger session is active. If neither of those happen, then we still stop the debugging session but "internally", without propagating the `stopped` signal. (cc @m4gr3d due to f713a20c94478bef3a8bc0e9300c24d37987b984 / 108518, just to double check this doesn't reintroduce an issue you fixed)

title: RAW input alpha above 1.0 is dropped from expression upon color picker close and clamped to 1.0 upon value refresh
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in Godot v4.6.1.stable System information Godot v4.6.1.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Single-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.8142) - AMD Ryzen 5 5600X 6-Core Processor (12 threads) - 31.90 GiB memory Issue description Currently, alpha can only be modulated above 1.0 using code or the color picker's RAW input, but the latter is rather unfriendly with how it currently handles values above 1.0. In the example clip I've uploaded, I have a simple scene with a Node2D with a Sprite2D as its child. I decrease the Sprite2D's alpha to make it transparent, then increase the Node2D's alpha to 2.0 to make the Sprite2D fully opaque, then hit enter to confirm my changes. This closes the color picker window, and upon opening it, you can see that the alpha is missing from the expression but still applied. Clicking out of the color picker in this state will preserve the increased alpha, but clicking into any field besides the RAW input field will cause it to reset. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6c8dfa1-dd64-40df-8451-28b66fab465e Lowering alpha below 1.0 while the RAW input field is open will add alpha as the fourth field, and editing from there works as expected; changing it to any positive value below 1.0 won't cause any issues when closing the color picker or clicking into its fields. One solution could be always making alpha visible in the RAW input field, or just hiding it if it's not equal to 1.0 or if the user hasn't modified it. Setting alpha above 1.0 has a clear use case in the editor since it can help you visualize how things will look if you're modulating a chain of nodes. There's an additional issue I considered opening a separate report for that would be largely mitigated if alpha were always available in the RAW input field; that being that attempting to input a space into the field immediately closes the color picker and discards your input. The expression present in the field follows Godot's style guide and has spaces between its values, but anyone trying to follow suit by putting a space before their alpha value might come to the mistaken conclusion that editing alpha with the field isn't possible to begin with. Steps to reproduce 1. Open color picker on any node 2. Switch to RAW input mode 3. Drag the alpha slider below to make an alpha value appear or type in your own and make it higher than 1.0 4. Press enter to confirm 5. Re-open color picker and check RAW input field to see that your value is no longer visible 6. Click into any field besides RAW input to see your change reverted Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

title: Lightmap loads slower and uses more RAM in 4.6 compared to 4.5
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in v4.6.1.stable.mono.official [14d19694e] - Not reproducible in v4.5.1.stable.mono.official [f62fdbde1] Also tested some of the 4.6 dev versions, but I'm not sure which ones i could actually reproduce it in, since it was a bit inconsistent. At one point it seemed like it started in 4.6 dev 4 and another time it seemed like it started in dev 5 System information Godot v4.6.1.stable.mono - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.9174) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-8700 CPU @ 3.20GHz (12 threads) - 15.92 GiB memory Issue description After upgrading my game from 4.5.1 to 4.6.1, I noticed it the RAM usage changed from about 500mb to 1150mb, and loading a save in my game went from about 1 second to 3-5 seconds. The editor also seems to use a lot more RAM, from around 1300mb to around 2000mb. I measured the RAM usage with task manager in windows. I'm not sure if these are one or two different issues, but they seem related, since having my LightMapGI node hidden makes both issues disappear. When showing the LightMapGI node for the first time the memory usage increases and there is a lagspike. After it has been show initially the issue doesn't happen again when i return to the main menu and load the save/game world again in my game, but running the project again it always happens the first time. I also noticed switching to directx12 doesn't have the memory issue, but the longer loading time still seems to happen, maybe even a bit longer. But my project uses vulkan. Steps to reproduce The minimal reproduction project reproduces the issue for me, but a bit less than in my actual project, here it goes from around 500-600mb to 800mb, and the loading time is maybe from 1 second to 2 seconds. How to reproduce: - Open the project in godot 4.6.1 and bake the light map in test_scene.tscn - Run the project - Open task manager and expand the godot process to see the memory usage for the game, note how much it uses - Press 'ui_left' to change from the empty main scene to the test scene with the lightmap - Notice that the scene change takes around 2 seconds - Notice that the memory usage in the task manager has increased by around 200mb - Run the project and test again with godot 4.5.1 - Notice that the scene change is almost instant - Notice that the memory usage only increased with 30-50mb My numbers are from memory so they might be slightly wrong, but there should at least be a pretty noticable difference between godot 4.5.1 and godot 4.6.1. Let me know if you need me to help test anything. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) light-map-load-speed-and-memory-bug.zip

title: Exported variable set to null in scene file, when saving script with error
body: Tested versions Reproduced in: v4.7.dev2.official [778cf54da] v4.6.2.rc1.official [257ac3532] v4.6.1.stable.official [14d19694e] System information Godot v4.7.dev2 - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - GeForce GTX 550 Ti - Intel(R) Core(TM) i3-2100 CPU @ 3.10GHz (4 threads) - 11.95 GiB memory Issue description This issue was encountered when copying code from a tutorial, that uses spaces as indentation. Issue: The scene file saves an exported variable as `null`, ex: `a = null` - when the scene with the attached script is the focused tab - the attached script is open - the exported variable has the default value - and the script has an error. Even if the error is corrected on save, ex: Editor Settings > General > Text Editor > Behavior > Files > Convert Indent on Save Expected: Don't save the exported variable in the scene file as `null` Steps to reproduce - open the project: exported-var-set-to-null.zip - in the scene panel, select `Node2D` - in the inspector, note that the exported var `A` is set to `1` - open the attached script: `node_2d.gd` - note the variable `a`'s default value is set to: `1` - run the project - the output panel shows `1` (the expected value) - open the scene file `node_2d.tscn`, in a text editor that shows when the file is updated on disk, ex: vscode - the scene file `node_2d.tscn` looks like this: - copy the code below, the second line is indented with four spaces - back in Godot, paste it at the bottom of the script - the script editor shows: > Error at (6, 37): Used space character for indentation instead of tab as used before in the file. - save the script: `ctrl + s` or `file > save` - the scene file has a new entry: `a = null` - run the scene Issue: the output panel shows: `<null>` Expected: the scene file shouldn't store the exported variable as `null`, since the exported variable hasn't changed. Workaround: The `null` assignment is removed from the scene file: - when a scene with an attached script is the focused tab - the attached script is open - the script doesn't have an error - the script has been modified, so that it has something to save (see note below) - and the scene is saved: `ctrl + s` Note: The script only needs to be modified to remove the `null` assignment, if the scene and the script are closed and reopened. The `null` assignment can be removed without modifying the script, by resaving the script right after it was saved with the error. In this state the `null` assignment can be toggled with: - create a script error (ex: four spaces instead of a tab, when the script already has a tab indent) - save the script (`ctrl + s`) - `null` is assigned to the exported variable in the scene file - save the script (`ctrl + s`) - the `null` assignment is removed from the scene file - undo (`ctrl + u`) to recreate the error Minimal reproduction project (MRP) exported-var-set-to-null.zip

title: In-editor signal connector always creates a new function if the script contains an overindented dictionary
body: Tested versions - Found in v4.6.1.stable.official [14d19694e] - Reproduced in v4.1.3.stable.official [f06b6836a] I have not tested it with other versions. System information Windows 11 - Godot v4.6.1.stable.official [14d19694e] Issue description Defining a dictionary like this in a script: Is valid syntax, despite the overindented body and closing brace. It causes no errors or warnings, the script functions just fine, and the dictionary can be used normally. However, if you try to use the signals dock to connect a signal to one of the functions currently in the script, the editor will attempt to add a new function with the same name, which makes the script invalid. It is possible to delete the added function. However, if the connection binds extra arguments, the signal connection will fail altogether, displaying an error like this: `ERROR: Cannot connect to 'pressed': the provided callable is not valid: 'Control::button_pressed'.` making it impossible altogether to connect certain signals in a scene without editing the scene file manually or fixing the indentation mistake. While I do not know if the dictionary definition above should be valid syntax or not, the fact that it doesn't cause any errors or warnings other than this one made it really hard to track down why the signal couldn't connect properly. Steps to reproduce - Create a new scene - Attach a script to the root node of the scene - Add this code to the script (note the overindented body and closing brace in the dictionary): - Save the script and scene. - Go to the signals dock and try to connect any signal, from this node or a new one, to the `button_pressed` function. - When you click "Connect", a new function with the same name will be added to the script, making it invalid. title: Duplicating child node of scene instance or inherited scene copies signal binding locked state (yellow), preventing disconnecting until editor restart
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: v4.6.stable.official [89cea1439] System information Godot v4.6.stable - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6636) - 12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-12900K (24 threads) - 63.75 GiB memory Issue description In those two cases: 1. Creating an inherited scene from a base scene with signals on children and duplicating a child with signals 2. Instantiating a scene in another scene, enabling Editable Children and duplicating a child with signals The resulting child will have the pre-existing signals marked in yellow (not editable), preventing disconnection. This is only a transient bug: restarting the editor will clear the yellow mark and allow edition/disconnection. Steps to reproduce Setting up the project (already done in MRP, only need to do this once) 1. Create menu.tscn with Menu > Button 2. Add menu.gd script to Menu 3. Connect Button's pressed signal to menu.gd > `_on_button_pressed` method Scene instance 1. Create demo.tscn and add instance of menu.tscn 2. Enable Editable Children on that instance Inherited scene 1. Create New Inherited Scene from menu.tscn, named menu_inherited.tscn Bug repro (you must repeat these steps after every editor restart) Scene instance 1. In demo.tscn, duplicate the Button node 2. Check the Signals panel Actual: non-editable, non-disconnectable (yellow) signal binding to `_on_button_pressed` Expected: editable (white) signal binding to `_on_button_pressed` 3. Restart the editor The signal is now editable. Inherited scene 1. Open menu_inherited.tscn and duplicate the Button node 2. Check the Signals panel Actual: non-editable, non-disconnectable (yellow) signal binding to `_on_button_pressed` Expected: editable (white) signal binding to `_on_button_pressed` 3. Restart the editor The signal is now editable. Notes When copy-pasting a node instead of duplicating it, the signals are not copied at all, so the bug is not visible (not sure if intended or not, I suppose copy-pasting could be done to another location where the signal callback is not reachable and that's the reason why signal bindings are not copied) Workaround Same, copy the node and paste it into its parent for a duplicate without the signals at all (you'll have to manually re-add signals you need though) Minimal reproduction project (MRP) mrp-duplicate-inherited-or-instantiated-scene-child-cannot-disconnect-signal.zip

title: Error while compiling export template with build profile disabling Geometry3D
body: Tested versions It is not Godot's error, but rather the build system's thing. title: Instance shader parameters don't update in inspector when material changes
body: Tested versions Reproducible in 4.7-dev1, 4.6.1-stable, 4.5.1-stable System information Godot v4.6.1.stable - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.9579) - AMD Ryzen 5 7600X 6-Core Processor (12 threads) - 31.08 GiB memory Issue description When adding or removing a shader material that uses a shader with instance uniforms to a node, the Instance Shader Parameters section of the inspector doesn't immediately update. The instance uniforms don't appear when adding the material, and don't disappear when removing it if they were already there. Instead, you need to force the inspector to update by for example deselecting and then reselecting the node (so that the inspector is recreated) If the material is set in `material_override`, then adding or removing the _shader_ in the material resource does correctly update the instance uniform parameters, and they also update as expected when editing the shader code. This doesn't work if the material is set in `material_overlay`, in surface material overlay on a mesh instance, or in the mesh itself. Steps to reproduce * Create a shader resource (can be textual or visual) that contains at least one `instance uniform` * Create a material using the shader * Create a geometry instance, such as a MeshInstance3D * Assign the material to the node. Observe how the Instance Shader Parameters section of GeometryInstance3D doesn't appear * Deselect the node and then select it again. Observe how Instance Shader Parameters has appeared now * Remove the material from the node. Observe how the Instance Shader Parameters is still there * Deselect the node and then select it again. Observe how Instance Shader Parameters has disappeared now Minimal reproduction project (MRP) instance_shader_parameters_bug_2026-03-13_22-46-01.zip

title: Fix tangent decoding detection when computing vertex skinning
body: Fixes 81446 When decoding veretx tangent in the shader, there is a special case to detect which encoding system to use (Octahedral or axis-angle coordinates). See scene.glsl, line 618. In the issue mentioned above, some octahedral encoded tangents are exactly (0, 1) which is the value used to detect the encoding. When this special value is detected, we switch to axis-angle decoding, instead of Octahedral, resulting is undefined results. This only happened for skinned objects. The fix is the same as the c++ code (rendering_server.cpp line ~650) : detect this special case when encoding the tangent in the skeleton compute shader, and apply a different but equivalent encoding instead. I did not modify the gles3 skeleton shader. I could be wrong, but the compatibility mode does not seem to suffer from this. For the fix, we could also check the uint value directly for slightly fewer instructions, but I feel this would make the code harder to maintain in the future.

title: Signal disconnections are not thread-safe
body:
Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.7.dev [a8e37fc01048823f639c4011af645d2e8e2b79d3] System information Godot v4.7.dev (a8e37fc01) - Fedora Linux 43 (Forty Three) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Single-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT (RADV GFX1201) - AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D 16-Core Processor (32 threads) - 60.39 GiB memory Issue description Despite the efforts made in 105453 and the claim in the `Object` documentation about everything related to signals being thread-safe, they are unfortunately not so. This script should crash the process, especially if you run it with a `use_asan=yes` build: ... with the following AddressSanitizer report:
[Click to expand/collapse]

The reason for this seems to be that `Object::signal_mutex` only covers the object's own `signal_map` but not things like `target->connections`, which is mutated in `Object::~Object` and `Object::_remove_user_signal`. Steps to reproduce 1. Run the MRP main scene, optionally in a `use_asan=yes` build. 2. Note the crash. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) signal-thread-safety.zip

title: Cannot properly use Web builds with Address/Leak Sanitizer enabled (despite claimed as supported in the docs)
body: Tested versions Tested when building web export templates on Godot 4.5.1. System information Windows 10, Godot v4.5.1-stable Issue description I'm trying to debug a bizzare crash that I'm facing on iPhone Safari Web builds launched inside Youtube Playables. I'm testing on an iPhone 12 (but seems to crash on modern models as well) using Godot 4.5.1. For context: the game seems to crash without much symptoms: first it starts to stutter after some time, then crashes. Even an empty-ish project crashes (rotating sprite and some Control nodes, minimal load). The crash happens regardless of user tapping the screen (stutters start around 40 mins in an idle project, crashes around 60-90 minutes in). Tapping the screen somehow accelerates the proccess (stutters at 10-15 mins in, crash at 15-20). I've exhausted most other ways of finding the problem and wanted to detect a memory leak using Sanitizers (and I'm pretty sure it's a memory leak of some sort, as Safari tab receives a memory pressure warning fromthe iOS system, but Godot Debug Monitors do not report anything wrong). Does anyone know how to properly trigger logging from sanitizers (e.g. from `use_asan=true`/`use_lsan=true` compile flags) in a web build? I've tried `get_tree.quit(`) but that doesn't seem to do anything... the app just freezes. Same happens trying to call `__lsan_do_leak_check()`/`__lsan_do_recoverable_leak_check()` manually. In the browser console (trying to test it first on PC using Firefox/Chrome) I sometimes get errors like: When building with asan/lsan. Sometimes it's `wasm validation error: at offset unused values not explicitly dropped by end of block...` Having some sort of example project/guide for properly making sanitizers work on the Web would be really appreciated. Steps to reproduce 1. Build a custom web export template with `use_asan=true` or `use_lsan=true` . 2. Launch it on a server. 3. Load from a PC browser, open the console. 4. Trigger the leak information by calling `get_tree.quit(`) from GDScipt or `__lsan_do_leak_check()`/`__lsan_do_recoverable_leak_check()` from C++. 5. Notice a freeze that doesn't resolve itself. 6. Some web build configurations fail to load (possibly due to WASM size?) and show errors (mentioned above). Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Seems somewhat irrelevant? An empty project works fine, but web export templates need to be compiled manually.

title: Unexported variable overridden by last exported value
body: Tested versions Reproducible in: v4.7.dev2.official [778cf54da] v4.6.2.rc1.official [257ac3532] v4.6.1.stable.official [14d19694e] System information Godot v4.7.dev2 - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - GeForce GTX 550 Ti - Intel(R) Core(TM) i3-2100 CPU @ 3.10GHz (4 threads) - 11.95 GiB memory Issue description Changing a script's variable, has no effect when running a scene, after unexporting the variable, by removing `@export `. Expected: Unexporting a variable should start checking the script variable's value. Cause: The last exported variable change in the inspector, remains in the scene file (.tscn), and it overrides any script variable changes. The scene doesn't save and remove the unexported variable, when: - running the scene - or closing and reopening the project Workaround: Saving the scene manually before or after reopening the project, removes the last exported variable change from the scene file. Steps to reproduce - open the project: unexported-variable-overridden-by-last-exported-value.zip - open the script: `node_2d.gd` - in the script, note the variable's value `1`: `@export var a = 1` - select the node: `Node2D` - in the inspector, note the exported variable `A`'s value: `2` - run the scene - stop the scene - the output panel shows the exported value: `2` - in the script, unexport the variable, by removing: `@export ` Before: `@export var a = 1` After: `var a = 1` - the exported variable `A` disappears from the inspector - run the scene - stop the scene Issue: The output panel still shows the last exported value: `2` Expected: The output panel should show the script variable's current value: `1` Try again: - in the script, change the variable's value, ex: `var a = 3` - run the scene - stop the scene Issue: the output panel still shows the last exported value: `2` Expected: The output panel should show the script variable's current value: `3` Cause: The scene file: `node_2d.tscn` still has the last exported variable change: `a = 2` Workaround: Saving the scene manually, before or after reopening the project, removes the last exported variable: - `ctrl + s` - `Scene > Save Scene ` Now the output panel shows the script variable's current value, when the scene runs. Notes: The scene file doesn't seem to recognize that the variable has been unexported. The scene should auto save when the scene runs. According to the mouse over popup, on the setting: Editor Settings > General > Run > Auto Save > Save Before Running > Setting run/auto_save/save_before_running: bool If true, saves all scenes and scripts automatically before running the project. Setting this to false prevents the editor from saving if there are no changes which can speed up the project startup slightly, but it makes it possible to run a project that has unsaved changes. (Unsaved changes will not be visible in the running project.) 'Save Before Running' has not been changed from its default value: enabled Minimal reproduction project (MRP) unexported-variable-overridden-by-last-exported-value.zip

title: `RingBuffer`: Fix `T read()` method reading empty buffer
body: `T read()` method of the `RingBuffer` checks the `space_left()` instead of `data_left()`, causing it to read empty buffers and not reading full ones. I couldn't find any instances where this method is used in the engine, but I commonly use it for my tests and benchmarks.

title: [Accessibility] Add viewport flag to ensure keyboard focus can't be trapped by non Control tree.
body: Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117245 - Grab inspector focus when `edit(object)` is called and nothing else is selected. - Adds `deep_focus_lookup` flag to ensure Tab/Shift+Tab can always find some focusable control after focus is released, and set it to `true` for the editor root viewport. Mostly a workaround, since the issue is a result of releasing keyboard focus completely, which is not a normal state for GUI apps.

title: Wayland: Improve mapping robustness and synchronization
body: Fixes 115739. Should fix 104752. (Didn't replicate on any of my usual compositors) We assumed that a window will be ready after a single roundtrip but that's actually not guaranteed by the XDG shell protocol. This patch waits for an arbitrary timeout (10s) before erroring out and forcefully closing the window. Since this exercised quite some code paths, it also reworks some window handling logic and fixes a bunch of synchronization issues. --- Tested with Pinnacle 0.2.2 (built from commit `b6c40da68024fb7fbb1db1f0890a87b08da013c9`) on my KISS Linux laptop by following the steps from the linked issue. Couldn't notice any obvious regression on my KISS Linux laptop with sway 1.11 and my Fedora 43 VM with KDE 6.5.4, GNOME 49.2.2, and COSMIC 1.0.1. Note that this solution isn't ideal as the world stops while waiting. We should obviously make everything asynchronous but I'm not entirely sure how much that is possible. If the timed out window is the main window the engine will fall back to X11 properly, although for some reason *only in this case* `DetectPrimeX11` (if invoked) stalls. I'd really postpone this issue to another PR if possible, given how specific it is. (Does it even count as a regression? :P)

title: Fix undoing terrain set move duplicates terrain in second set
body: Fixes: 116636 Bug Preview https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4b831ab7-a54d-4aeb-95d6-5b948c5990c2 Cause The issue was caused by the undo system restoring terrain properties without clearing the existing terrain container first. Because of this, the previous terrain state was effectively applied on top of the current one, leading to duplicated or mismatched terrain data. Fix This PR fixes the problem by clearing the terrains in the affected terrain sets before restoring their properties during undo. This ensures the terrain containers are rebuilt from the saved undo state instead of being merged with the current state. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b3c86048-a57b-47e3-be3d-8d36ebdea7c4

title: GDScript: Fix crash when DEV_ENABLED
body: I discovered this whilst testing the MR) for 117339, which was crashing with DEV_ENABLED. Fix double-free of GDScript coroutine stack when re-awaiting Fixes a crash caused by a double-free of stack `Variant`s in the GDScript VM when a resumed coroutine hits another `await`. Root Cause When `GDScriptFunction::call()` is invoked with a saved state (`p_state != nullptr`) and the function suspends again (`awaited = true`), the cleanup code at the end of `call()` destroys the stack Variants in the old state's buffer: However, `p_state->stack_size` is never reset to `0`. Later, when the old `GDScriptFunctionState` is destroyed, `~GDScriptFunctionState()` calls `_clear_stack()`, which sees `stack_size != 0` and destroys the same Variants again — a double-free. Crash Details GDScript backtrace: C++ backtrace: The chain: timer `timeout` → destroys one-shot slot callable → releases `CallableCustomBind` holding a `Ref<GDScriptFunctionState>` → `~GDScriptFunctionState` calls `_clear_stack()` → attempts to destroy already-freed stack Variants. Fix Set `p_state->stack_size = 0` after the stack is freed, preventing the redundant destruction in `_clear_stack()`. > [!NOTE] > > 🤖 AI disclosure: I used API to assist with troubleshooting and identifying the fix

title: Irregular spacing between characters at small font sizes
body: Tested versions - Issue does not exist in Godot_v4.4.1-stable_mono_win64 - Issue exists in Godot_v4.5-stable_mono_win64 and Godot_v4.6.1-stable_mono_win64 (Issue introduced between 4.4.1 and 4.5) System information Godot v4.6.1.stable.mono - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - integrated Intel(R) Iris(R) Xe Graphics (Intel Corporation; 31.0.101.5186) - 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-1165G7 @ 2.80GHz (8 threads) - 15.65 GiB memory Issue description My game includes pixel art, along with UI rendered at native resolution (using the Canvas Items stretch mode). The window resolution is small (640 x 360), meaning that a normal, non-heading font ends up being relatively small as well (at 8 pixels). With nearest filtering, I've noticed that irregular horizontal spacing can occasionally be seen between text characters in Labels. In v4.4.1, the way I found to remedy this was to set the GUI > Theme > Default Font Subpixel Positioning setting to Disabled. That resulted in a crisp font with regular spacing. However, since Godot v4.5, that change no longer works; the text renders with irregular spacings regardless of what is specified for that setting (or any setting that I can find). Here is an example from 4.4.1, rendering without the irregular spacing: And here is an example from 4.6: Here's a zoomed in comparison to hopefully make it clearer. 4.4.1 is on the top; 4.6 on the bottom. The irregular spacings are underlined in red. Some alternatives I've looked into to try to remedy this issue include: * Using Linear filtering for fonts. This fixes the irregular spacing issues, but makes the text blurry. * Enabling Multichannel Signed Distance Field (MSDF) for the font. The text renders fairly crisply, but text outlines are no longer rendered properly. They all seem to be limited to around 1 pixel or so. A warning explaining this appears on Labels with outlines and the 8 pixel font size: `MSDF font pixel range is too small, some outlines/shadows will not render. Set MSDF pixel range to be at least 32 to render all outlines/shadows.` (32 pixels is way too large for my project, so isn't an option.) Neither of these ends up with parity to the original 4.4.1 text. If nothing changes, I will probably use MSDF, but ideally, I would like it if disabling subpixel positioning for fonts worked like in 4.4.1. That approach combined two things I cannot replicate with current settings: crisp font rendering at small font sizes, along with correct outline rendering. Was this change intentional in order to fix something else? Steps to reproduce The MRP includes the following setup steps: 1. Setup a project for pixel art with native resolution UI: * Window Resolution: 640 x 360 * Stretch Mode: Canvas Items * Scale Mode: Integer * Texture Filter: Nearest 2. Under Project Settings GUI > Theme, set Default Font Subpixel Positioning to Disabled. (Advanced settings must be enabled in order to view.) 3. Add a label with a small font size to a scene (8 pixels in this case.) In the MRP, simply run the project to view the sample text. In 4.4.1, the text renders without irregular horizontal spacings between characters. In 4.5 and 4.6, the text has irregular spacings. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) TextBug_4.4.1.zip

title: Fix `EditorPlugin::remove_control_from_docks` freeing passed control
body: Fixes 117331. Fixes a regression from 106503 which made `remove_control_from_docks(docked_control)` result in freeing the passed `docked_control`. After 106503 when a Control is added to a dock with `add_control_from_dock` it is wrapped into an EditorDock node by adding it as its child. On `remove_control_from_docks` such EditorDock is freed, but the wrapped control was never being removed from it, hence it was freed together with its parent. This is a regresion / compatibilty breakage, as `remove_control_from_docks` is documented to not free the passed control and tells clearly it's the user responsibility. https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/9095a76ff54ff96778c75260ac629cd4d1f1535f/doc/classes/EditorPlugin.xmlL681-L686

title: Core: Fix VariantCallable comparison using identity instead of hash only
body: Fixes 116141. Also related to 97044, which was closed as not-a-bug but had the same underlying cause. `VariantCallable::compare_equal` compared only hashes, so two different Variant objects with the same contents (e.g. two distinct arrays `[]` and `[]`) would be treated as the same callable. This caused a spurious "already connected" error when connecting signals to methods on different objects that happened to have equal values. The fix uses `Variant::identity_compare` after the hash check, so that only callables referencing the exact same object in memory are considered equal. `compare_less` had the same hash-only issue and is also fixed. Includes a unit test covering arrays, empty arrays, dictionaries, same-object identity, and different-method cases.

title: Fix macOS Steam time tracking lost when opening a project
body: Fixes 112281. On macOS, when the Project Manager opens a project, it spawns the Editor via `NSWorkspace`. The `NSWorkspaceOpenConfiguration` object doesn't carry over the parent's environment by default — it gets a clean one from `launchd`. This causes `SteamAppId` (set by the Steam client) to be missing in the Editor process, so `SteamAPI_Init()` silently fails and Steam shows "no longer running." This passes the parent process environment through to the spawned app instance, matching the behavior on Windows (`CreateProcessW` inherits by default) and Linux (`fork`/`execvp` inherits by default). Both the modern `NSWorkspaceOpenConfiguration` path and the legacy `launchApplicationAtURL:` path (x86_64 pre-10.15) are fixed.

title: Add fast path to Polygon2D
body: This saves a huge amount of time when animating vertices of a Polygon2D manually Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115476 Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/74540 The fundamental problem is that previously we recreate the internal mesh every frame. With this change we simply update the mesh each frame instead. This new approach is still not optimal, but it gets us most of the potential performance while being very simple and safe. What makes it not optimal is that we are recalculating all the surface data each time, and updating the entire buffer even if only a portion of the buffer needs to be updated. An optimal approach would be to go back to the Godot 3.x way of doing things and introduce a new rendering command to render polygons dynamically. The problem with this is it adds a lot of complexity to Polygon2D and would require a new rendering codepath in the renderer. In practice, it probably isn't worth the added complexity when we get most of the benefit with this simple change So far I have tested this on an M2 MBP. MRP from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115476: 33 FPS -> 101 FPS MRP from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/74540: 80 objects -> 220 objects

body: - Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117243 Issue Description When we changed layout of editor, e.g. move `EditorDebuggerPlugin` from default position to bottom. It will first remove from original position, then add to target position. The remove child operation will trigger a `tree_exited` signal, which connected to `_debugger_gone_away()`. https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/d103efb6ea076ec52b4deca358e83f1fd177d163/editor/debugger/editor_debugger_plugin.cppL118 It will set debugger to nullptr in `_debugger_gone_away()`, then cause `Plugin is not attached to debugger` at the next operation. title: Incorrect auto-completion and tooltip for named lambda
body: Tested versions Reproducible in 4.7.dev [220b0b2f7], 4.6.stable, 4.4.1.stable. title: [3.x] Fix sync issue in separate-thread physics

title: Fix `RenderingDevice::texture_clear()` behaving unexpectedly when using the D3D12 driver
body: Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117210 Using `RenderingDevice::texture_clear()` led to different results between the Vulkan and D3D12 driver, depending on the `DataFormat` of the texture. This PR changes the D3D12 driver to use `ID3D12GraphicsCommandList::ClearUnorderedAccessViewFloat()` for clearing color textures instead of `ID3D12GraphicsCommandList::ClearUnorderedAccessViewUint()`. As far as my testing goes, this should make the result of `RenderingDevice::texture_clear()` be on par between the D3D12 and Vulkan driver. The following example clears textures with different data formats to `Color.HOT_PINK` (left to right: `R8G8B8A8_UNORM`, `R32G32B32A32_SFLOAT`, `R8_UNORM`, `R32_SFLOAT`). **Before:** **This PR:** Example project: texture-clear-example.zip From what I've read about the behavior of those two clearing functions, I think that `ClearUnorderedAccessViewFloat()` would be more appropriate for clearing color textures. However, I am not sure whether I am missing something here that might cause issues, as I don't know whether there was a reason for using `ClearUnorderedAccessViewUint()` in the first place.

title: Fix "Send Key(s) to RESET" not appearing on first right-click in Animation Editor
body: Fixes 117100. The cause of issue is that `selection`(the selected keys) is updated next frame due to a deferred signal connection (`CONNECT_DEFERRED`), but `AnimationTrackEditor::can_add_reset_key` relis on its value at this frame. This commit extracts the menu pop up logic as a single function and uses `call_deferred` to call it to use the updated `selection`. For context, `selection` is updated by `_try_select_at_ui_pos` -> `emit_signal(SNAME("select_key"), key_idx, true)` -> `AnimationTrackEditor::_key_selected` and is used in `AnimationTrackEditor::can_add_reset_key`.

title: Bypass physics command queue during physics processing
body: Relates to 109591. Fixes 109213. Fixes 113270. Supersedes 116897. > [!NOTE] > While this is labeled as a regression fix, this is not strictly a regression. 109591 just made much more apparent the race conditions that have been present for years, likely since `physics/*d/run_on_separate_thread` was first introduced. This makes it so that physics server commands are not queued up at all during physics processing when `physics/*d/run_on_separate_thread` is enabled, and will instead be invoked immediately/synchronously. The only things that will be queued up to run asynchronously now is `PhysicsServer*D::step()` (which is still significant) or stuff done during idle processing. This can potentially have a non-trivial performance impact for projects that rely on `physics/*d/run_on_separate_thread` and are doing many costly physics-related state changes in things like `_physics_process`. This is unfortunately somewhat necessary because physics queries do not currently force a flush of the command queue, which leads to physics queries potentially operating on stale physics data, leading to issues like the ones seen in 109213 and 113270. While there could be a reasonable argument for exposing some way to forcefully flush the physics command queue instead, it would need to be called in unintuitive places and would lead to weird/drastic/unintuitive performance pitfalls, so I've opted to go with this simpler (albeit much more aggressive) approach instead.

title: Clearing `@export` variable from an instantiated scene does not update parent scene save state
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in 4.5.1-stable - Reproducible in 4.6.1-stable - Reproducible in 4.7-dev2 System information NixOS, Godot 4.6.1-stable Issue description When clearing an `@export` variable from an instantiated child scene, the editor (rightfully) recognizes that the scene state has changed. Saving the scene does not update the scene on disk, however. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c7bdad81-b798-46a9-8ab2-32113aa5c6cc Setting the export to an external Node, then deleting that external node, will properly update the `@export` variable to not be assigned on disk. Steps to reproduce 1. Create a scene with a script at the root that has an `@export` variable 2. Set it's `@export` variables to one of it's children. 3. Save the scene. 4. Instantiate this scene as a child scene in a parent scene. 5. git add or git commit the state. 6. Clear the `@export` variable 7. Save the parent scene. 8. You'll notice no change in the scene save state. 9. Reboot Godot 10. The `@export` variable will be reset to the value set in the child scene. Optional to confirm that you _can_ clear `@export` nodes; do this after running above steps. 1. Create a node in the parent scene of the same type of the `@export` variable 2. Assign it as the `@export` variable. 3. Save the scene. 4. Delete the assigned node. 5. Save the scene. 6. Notice how the save state now correctly indicates that the `@export` variable is cleared. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) scene-save-bug-demo.zip Above project is the same as in the demo gif, for 4.6.1-stable

title: Fix editor resources importing
body: Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/111008 Scenario - The current editor scale is 100%. - A new resource is created, flagged as **scale with editor scale** and reimported. - File `.godot/imported/[resource].editor.meta` is created. - The user change editor scale to 200% and restarts editor. - The imported editor resource how still original scale, because reimport was not applied. - Timestamp of resource (or its `.import` file) is still the same, so `_is_test_for_reimport_needed` returns `false`. Note: Same goes for **convert colors with editor theme**. Proposal - A new file `.godot/imported/.editor.meta` is introduced. - This file contains editor scale of the last import of editor resources. - The file is loaded during `EditorFileSystem::_scan_filesystem` call and used to determine if editor requests different scale. - This allows to keep timestamp optimization and flag only editor resources for testing for reimport if needed. - *Bugsquad edit: supersedes* https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111009

title: VirtualJoystick does not update visibility mode until touched by the user
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.7-dev1, 4.7-dev2. - Not applicable prior to 4.7 (new feature). System information Godot v4.7.dev2 - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4080 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.9571) - AMD Ryzen 7 5700X 8-Core Processor (16 threads) - 15.92 GiB memory Issue description Changing the `visibility_mode` of a `VirtualJoystick` does not actually take effect until the next time the user interacts with the joystick. Changing it from `ALWAYS` to `WHEN_TOUCHED` doesn't hide the joystick until the user touches it once, then it gets hidden properly. And the other way around. See this video of the MRP: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/23b346ef-f5f1-4b49-853c-c27aaee6eb17 Note about why this is important: I have a game where I want to have the joystick set to `WHEN_TOUCHED` usually, so the joystick does not clutter up the screen. But for the tutorial that teaches the player how to move, I want to briefly set it to `ALWAYS` so they can see the joystick on screen, then set it to `WHEN_TOUCHED` again after the tutorial is complete. I currently have no way to dynamically change the mode, and requiring the user to touch it before it appears defeats the point of the tutorial which is to show the user that it exists. Steps to reproduce 1. Turn on project setting `input_devices/pointing/emulate_touch_from_mouse` (or just use a touch screen). 2. Create a `VirtualJoystick`. 3. Optionally change `joystick_mode` to dynamic (not needed but makes it easier to use). 4. Create a checkbox/button that, when toggled, switches the joystick's `visibility_mode`. 5. Run the project and play with the checkbox and joystick. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) virtualjoystick-visibility-demo.zip

title: Fix VehicleBody3D sliding on slope.
body: This pull request fixes the VehicleBody3D from sliding on slopes. The sliding was caused by suspension force components not perpendicular to gravity. This solution sums these components and applies them in the opposite direction. Canceling out the force. The amount of force canceled out relies on 2 new exposed variables: Hold Angle: The vehicle will not start sliding below this contact slope angle Slide Angle: The value at which full sliding is applied

title: Fix TabContainer set current tab not immediately updating visibility
body: Fix TabContainer set current tab not immediately updating visibility - Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/99065 - Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115947 - Supersedes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116119 `_on_tab_changed` repaint was made deferred back in 58687, probably because repaint uses `theme_cache` which will crash if used too early. But now `TabContainer::set_current_tab` doesn't immediately set the current tab, it waits for `NOTIFICATION_ENTER_TREE`, since 83893. This means it should be safe to repaint immediately when the tab changes. I tested and didn't find any issues with it. This also fixes the AnimationTree dock issue, because the TabContainer not being visible immediately caused this check to not work: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/220b0b2f74d8e089481b140c42a42992a76dd6fc/editor/animation/animation_player_editor_plugin.cppL2490-L2491 Previously it was only possible to have the AnimationTree dock open automatically if the AnimationPlayer dock was already open. This was because this check in the dock manger never changed the tab to the AnimationPlayer dock because it detected that it was already visible since it didn't update yet to show the tree: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/220b0b2f74d8e089481b140c42a42992a76dd6fc/editor/docks/editor_dock_manager.cppL663 Before that was a lot of dock manager changes, I haven't checked what made it work in 4.5. `EditorData::get_handling_sub_editors` should probably be not in reverse order too, that way user plugins (and more specific plugins like AnimationTree) take priority over editor plugins, since they are opened in order and the last opened will override the others. This was sort of even the goal with the PR that changed it 37022

title: Fix new resource locked when created via editable children in inspector
body: Fixes 112211 Creating a new Resource via the inspector picker on a node that is an editable child of another scene, _get_owner_path() would return the owner node's scene file path (the external/child scene), causing the newly created resource to get a path like 'child.tscn::...' instead of 'parent.tscn::...'. The resource would then appear as foreign to the currently edited scene, locking it as read-only in the inspector until the scene was saved and reopened. The fix: after resolving the owner via get_owner(), check if that owner belongs to a different scene than the currently edited one. If so, use the edited scene root's path instead, consistent with the existing guard for nodes that have their own scene_file_path set.

title: Fix `are_spans_equal` for `p_size = 0` when pointers may be invalid
body: - Should fix https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117103 Should fix crashes in some configurations when comparing strings. Explanation `memcmp` (and other string comparison methods) apparently require non-nullptr arguments, even when size = 0. This requirement is specified in the C11 reference §7.24.1(2): https://port70.net/~nsz/c/c11/n1570.pdf It makes no sense for implementations to dereference the pointer on size = 0, so this does not usually cause problems. However, there is a specific gcc optimization `-fdelete-null-pointer-checks` that deletes nullptr checks if it is confident that they are not required. It may see the `memcmp` call, and according to §7.24.1(2) assume that the call means the pointers are non-nullptr. So it may delete non-nullptr checks, leading to a dereference of invalid data. By introducing a `size > 0` check before `memcmp`, we disallow it to propagate the invariant. Future C versions may hopefully relax the `memcmp` requirement. > [!NOTE] > AI disclaimer: I used AI to track down the exact specs of `memcmp` (yielding §7.24.1(2)) and the `-fdelete-null-pointer-checks` optimization. I checked the specs and implemented the fix myself.

title: Fix Viewport path update error when dock is reparented during tree
body: Fixes 115512. Guard _update_viewport_path() against calling get_path_to() when the ViewportTexture's local scene is not yet inside the scene tree. When an EditorDock is moved to closed_dock_parent (e.g. when switching scene tabs with a MultiplayerSynchronizer selected), the dock is reparented mid-tree-propagation. This triggers NOTIFICATION_ENTER_TREE on any Viewport inside the dock, which calls _update_viewport_path(). At that moment the ViewportTexture's local scene may not be inside the tree yet, causing get_path_to() to fail with: ERROR: Parameter "common_parent" is null. The path will be correctly updated on the next ENTER_TREE once the node is fully propagated into the tree.

title: Use SplitContainer for Node3D Editor viewports
body: The previous approach for the Node3D Editor's viewport container had some issues: - It used SplitContainer's `separation` but grabbing did not account for `minimum_grab_thickness`. This made it more difficult to grab. - The splitter icons had rendering issues when dragging (it flickers / doesn't show when dragging in some cases). - No touch dragger support. - No accessibility support. - At compact spacing preset, there was no space between them and it was impossible to drag them. These could be fixed on their own, but it is better to just use SplitContainer to handle all of it. I changed it to use 3 SplitContainers, one main one and two children. I change their vertical flags when needed to fit the different layouts. I also made sure that the horizontal/vertical positions would remain the same when switching layouts like before. These use the new drag nested intersections SplitContainer option so you can still adjust multiple at once. Also you can drag the intersection between one of these and the docks now. related https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/14300 The four-way works by disabling the first child split and using the second child's drag margin to expand it over the first, and setting the first child split offset to follow. I set minimum size of viewports to 39. This is the value I got when I minimized them on master, not sure why that value or where it is from, but it keeps the behavior the same. Removed the 3-way and 4-way grabber icons. Drag nested intersections handles it by showing both regular grabber icons instead. The 3-way icons had another issue too, sometimes it was not aligned correctly. Related https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/74691 The exact viewport that is used for certain positions in some layouts have changed a bit, but I don't think it is that important to keep the same. 2 viewports (including alt) now use indexes 0 and 1 instead of 0 and 2, and 3 viewports alt now uses 0,1,2 instead of 0,2,3. I also fixed an issue in SplitContainer where enabling the touch dragger didn't hide it if dragging was disabled until it was sorted, it now updates immediately. Otherwise in the four way, it would show incorrectly for the first split until it gets updated.

title: Profiler: Fix for tree view becoming empty when you unpause the game
body: This is a fix for the profiler tree becoming empty when you un-pause the game. This fixes part of 76146 The issue was that when you pause and then un-pause the game, a single profiler data frame is not sent to the profiler. All the code in the editor_profiler.cpp relies on continuous sequential frames to do the math required to display the graph and tree. Rather than a high impact fix where I changed a ton of the code to compensate for this missing frame, I instead insert one empty frame to fill the gap in the internal data structure that holds the frames. The math to display the tree view was incorrect when it encountered a gap of 1 frame in the profiler data. Because of that the tree view would cease to display anything. Also when you first start the profiler the tree view is updated continuously as new data comes in. Once you pause and un-pause, that state where the tree is continuously updated is impossible to return to it. This PR also fixes that, so when you un-pause the tree returns to it's continuously updated state. An addition of 1 small block of contiguous code in 1 file. With this fix the tree view always displays data as you pause and un-pause the game. ******************************* Disclosure and License -As a software engineer I use many systems to ensure code quality, and that includes AI. I use the AI to: do detailed code analysis, be a sounding board for my different ideas, look for bugs, find unexpected behaviors, identify performance hot spots, expensive CPU calls, race conditions, poorly placed variable initializations, possible optimizations, and many more things. I do this during development and before I push code to the repo. It lists items that are potential problems and I decide what I think is important and make those changes. -For a descriptive example of my AI usage on a particular PR please read this https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117066issuecomment-4061773098 -Any and all code attached to this PR falls under the coverage of the MIT License.

title: Editor: Fix Bone2D creation to form proper bone chains from selected Node2D hierarchies
body: When using "Make Custom Bone2D(s) from Node(s)" with a selection that includes both a parent and its child Node2D, the resulting Bone2D nodes were previously inserted at the same level in the scene tree — the parent/child relationship between the original nodes was not reflected in the bone hierarchy at all. This commit fixes that by: - Sorting selected nodes by depth (shallowest first) before processing, so a parent's bone is always created before its child's bone. - Tracking a node→bone mapping so that each new Bone2D is reparented under the Bone2D created for its Node2D's parent (when that parent was also selected), producing a proper bone chain rather than a flat list. Several correctness issues in the undo/redo logic are also fixed: - A `memdelete` was added for the allocated Bone2D when the node has no parent, fixing a pre-existing memory leak. - An early return is added when the selection contains no Node2D instances (e.g. only Control nodes), preventing an empty no-op undo action from being pushed onto the stack. - All `_set_owner_for_node_and_children` undo calls are deferred to after the structural undo operations (remove/add child) for all nodes have been registered. This prevents the recursive ownership walk from descending into intermediate Bone2D nodes that are still attached to the tree mid-undo in scenes with non-selected nodes between two selected ancestors. - The original transform is now captured into a local variable before the Bone2D is constructed, making the intent of the undo transform restore explicit rather than relying on `new_bone->get_transform()` being equal to the captured value. - Depths are precomputed into a HashMap before sorting, reducing the sort from O(n·d·log n) to O(n·d + n·log n) by avoiding repeated ancestor traversals per comparison.

title: Fix keyboard nav in translation preview submenu
body: Fixes a minor bug I noticed that no one would bother to report. In these kinds of menus, keyboard navigation means that when you do ui_right, you open the submenu and focus its first element. Focusing the first element shouldn't happen for mouse hover. I noticed that this doesn't happen for the translation preview submenu, because it's generated on the about_to_popup signal. So I either had to either: a) Change EditorTranslationPreviewMenu to be generated earlier, or b) Change PopupMenu itself. I decided to do the latter, as I don't see why the keyboard focus for submenus wouldn't happen only after it's been popped up. This might fix other similar bugs I don't know about and I can't think of any way it would cause regressions.

title: Fix editor infinite loop and crash in sub-inspector auto-open
body: Fixes Fixes 116523 Description Unfolding a resource that automatically opens a bottom panel plugin triggered a synchronous inspector rebuild. If a custom EditorPlugin unconditionally returns true for _handles(), this resulted in a Use-After-Free crash or an infinite loop that locked the UI, as the inspector was being destroyed while iterating over its own properties. This PR fixes the issue by decoupling the bottom panel open logic from the manual fold toggle and tying it directly to the sub-inspector's state lifecycle in update_property(). When the edited object changes or is initialized from a previous state, it checks a new _has_external_editor() helper. If a plugin handles the resource, the _open_resource_editor call is deferred to the end of the frame. Steps to test Download and extract the official MRP provided in issue 116523: editorplugin_issue_mrp.zip (also attached below). Open the project in the compiled Godot Editor with this PR applied. Verify that the included custom EditorPlugin (which globally intercepts resources) is enabled. In the Scene tree, select the MeshInstance3D node. In the Inspector dock, locate either the shadow_mesh or material property. Click on the resource slot for shadow_mesh or material to unfold it in the inspector. Expected: The resource sub-inspector unfolds successfully. The Editor UI completes its layout update and remains completely stable. Regression: The Editor immediately crashes (Use-After-Free) or locks up in an infinite UI rebuild loop. Attachments editorplugin_issue_mrp(1).zip

title: Allow CoreAudio to sleep and avoid busy work when idling.
body: Audio: let CoreAudio go to sleep when nothing is playing and reduce busy work when idling. This changes the macOS audio path so we stop the CoreAudio output unit when the engine has been idle for a short time, instead of keeping it running. I added a small output sleep/wake capability to AudioDriver (optional by backend), then wired it up in CoreAudio with AudioOutputUnitStop/Start. On the AudioServer side, we now keep an atomic count of active stream playbacks, update it when playbacks are added/removed, and use it to detect true stream idleness. After 3 seconds with no active streams, we ask the driver to sleep. As soon as playback starts again, we wake it immediately. I also added a removal guard on playback nodes to avoid double-removal races, and adjusted CoreAudio device switching so output/input device changes still apply even while output is asleep. This builds on previous work and previous efforts: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/45948 https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/51296 Also fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/28039 This is part of the ongoing saga to reduce the busy work while idling: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116845 By keeping the audio going, Godot prevents MacOS from sleeping, with this change, your machine can actually go to sleep.

title: Remove deleted nodes track in _post_fix_animations
body: <!-- Please target the `master` branch. We will take care of backporting relevant fixes to older versions. Before submitting, please read our checklist for new contributors: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/introduction.htmlchecklist-for-new-contributors --> Fixes : 117150 Issue : Nodes with "Skip Import" on are being deleted in _post_fix_node. But animation tracks are being created before _post_fix_node, which means we also create tracks for "Skip Import" nodes. So we end up with tracks that points to nodes that no longer exist. Creating an error in _post_fix_animations when we try to use get_node using the track's path, we also ended up with theses useless tracks in tracks_to_keep. Fix : We use get_node_or_null instead and delete the tracks that point to deleted nodes Note : I felt that deleting theses tracks in _post_fix_animations made the most sense, but deleting them in _post_fix_node right before deleting the node is also an option

title: Accessibility warnings on controls are misleading
body: Tested versions - Reproducible since 4.5 (accesskit integration) System information Godot v4.6.stable - Linux Mint 22.3 (Zena) on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 3 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1050 (nvidia; 535.288.01) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-8300H CPU @ 2.30GHz (8 threads) - 23.19 GiB memory Issue description In the editor, there is a setting to turn on accessibility warnings that show up on controls that are missing a text accessibility_name. I think this is a useful feature! If it was just applied to nodes that are relevant. I do want to know if a button is missing it. Having it show up on every container with the text "Accessibility name must not be empty" is incorrect and might have developers implement accessibility incorrectly (like me!). You should not be encouraged to add an accessibility name on every container. Most screen readers will probably skip it because it's not an interactive element, but some may not. And some elements are "interactive" like the ScrollContainer where you probably don't want the screen reader to speak its accessibility name. There are controls that clearly need it, like a button. And there are controls that clearly don't need it, like a vbox. Then there are a few controls that are up for debate: ScrollContainer, and Custom nodes to name two. I think the best choice here would be to display a warning on those, and change the tooltip to "Accessibility name is empty. Consider adding the name and description if this control should be announced by the screen reader." Steps to reproduce N/A Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

title: Fix deadlock during shutdown when threaded RenderingServer is still compiling shaders
body: Fix for 117064. The deadlock is happening because the threaded RenderServer (Worker Thread 0) is trying to post new tasks (shader compilation), while the WorkerThreadPool runlevel is already at `RUNLEVEL_EXIT_LANGUAGES`. The proposed fix is to change worker threads posting new tasks after language shutdown to run the task directly. External threads are still blocked, only worker threads themselves are unblocked. As this is happening after `RUNLEVEL_PRE_EXIT_LANGUAGES`, the only active worker threads should be long running pump tasks.

title: Fix exposure not working with canvas background on Compatibility.
body: Fixes half of 110902. Alternative to 113863. Compatibility applies exposure in the scene shader. Since it's not done in post, this means canvas items do not get affected by it since they don't care about the exposure at all. As a solution, when the exposure is non 1.0 and the background mode is canvas, we can force the internal intermediary buffer to exist and apply exposure to it during the copy operation. This matches the behavior on Forward+/Mobile. I tried applying the exposure in the canvas shader instead, but that requires passing down the exposure to the canvas renderer, doing linear/sRGB conversions and also does not work with the background color.

title: AudioStreamInteractive: Remove transitions for deleted clips
body: Previously, if a transition was defined for a clip index that was subsequently removed (by reducing clip_count), an error would be logged when loading the AudioStreamInteractive: ERROR: Condition "p_to_clip < CLIP_ANY || p_to_clip >= clip_count" is true. at: add_transition (modules/interactive_music/audio_stream_interactive.cpp:258) This was because the transition table was not fully updated when reducing clip_count. Previously any references to the deleted clips as filler clips were removed, but transitions from or to the deleted clips were not. Remove any transition table entries that are from or to deleted clips. Resolves https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/95742

title: Reload resources in editor_resource_preview when import settings change
body: Issue Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/33745 Motivation If you change import settings that impact texture ("Invert Color" in my case) the preview is not updated in the file system. You can work around by clicking "reimport" twice. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f23f479c-545e-4c35-b934-d8621fd7800f Solution EditorResourcePreview maintains an in-memory cache of resources. When the resource file is updated, we check the last modified time and MD5 hash and perform our cache/load operations as needed. However, if the **import** file changes, those md5 and last modified values of the **resource** file remain the same. The import change does not get picked up in iterate() and is not reflected when generating the preview/thumbnail. To address this, I added a check in iterate() to see if the import file last_modtime is more recent than the cached last_modtime. If so, we know the cached data is stale, which we can fix by reloading from disk. Note 1: Why does the following code in the conditional block not fix this issue? When `cache_valid == false`, we go into the _generate_preview() function. This function also loads the resource with ResourceLoader. However this load uses the CACHE_MODE_REUSE. Meaning we are just loading the stale data and using that to display our preview. This is why I added `ResourceLoader::load(item.path, "", ResourceFormatLoader::CACHE_MODE_REPLACE);` in the new conditional block. CACHE_MODE_REPLACE causes the loader to update the cache entry with the data loaded from disk. Now our resource cache is up-to-date, and _generate_preview()'s load will use the correct (not stale) data. Note 2: Wait! In the existing code, why does last_md5 != md5 evaluate to true if only the import was updated? Since we are comparing the source md5 with the cached md5, and the source is the same, shouldn't the hashes be equal? Yes. I believe this is actually another currently existing bug. Earlier during _read_preview_cache(), we already iterated through the entire cache file. Therefore in the following code, we are reading EOF and `last_md5` gets set to `""`. So this conditional ALWAYS resolves to true. If this bug were not present, the workaround I mention above (clicking reimport twice) would no longer apply. I went ahead and fixed this as well since it's related and in the same code path. I am willing to move this change out into a separate PR (after this one lands) though since it does not really cause or resolve the bug at hand. Testing Manually tested with following project: MRP.zip. See video: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5f43292c-22a9-48f3-bbbd-22c75e02c34b

title: Set accessibility name on Tree inline cell editor when editing
body: Summary Fixes 110417. When editing a `Tree` item inline — e.g. renaming a node in the Scene dock via **F2** — a `LineEdit` (or `TextEdit` for multiline cells) appears inside a `Popup`. This editor had no `accessibility_name` set, so screen readers had nothing to announce when it received focus. For comparison, the search box in the **Add Node** dialog works correctly because it has a `placeholder_text` which `LineEdit` uses as its accessible name (see `NOTIFICATION_ACCESSIBILITY_UPDATE` in `line_edit.cpp`). **Fix:** in `Tree::edit_selected()`, set the accessibility name on `line_editor` / `text_editor` before `grab_focus()`: - Use the column's translated title (`xl_title`) if one is set. - Fall back to the item's current text (e.g. the node name) so the screen reader announces context like *"Node2D, edit text"*. Both the single-line (`CELL_MODE_STRING`) and multiline cases are fixed. Notes - The column title fallback to item text is intentional: the Scene dock tree has no column title, so using the node name gives the user clear context about what they are editing. - Tested that the fix compiles cleanly. Runtime verification with a screen reader (NVDA/Narrator) is needed to confirm the announcement behavior.

title: i18n: Extract Control accessibility name/description in POT generator
body: Summary Fixes 115366. Control nodes gained `accessibility_name` and `accessibility_description` properties in Godot 4.5 (shown in the **Accessibility** tab of the Inspector). These strings are passed through `tr()` at runtime, making them translatable — but `PackedSceneEditorTranslationParserPlugin` was never updated to extract them, so they were silently omitted from generated `.pot` files. **Fix:** add both property names to the `lookup_properties` set in `PackedSceneEditorTranslationParserPlugin`'s constructor, alongside the existing translatable properties (`text`, `title`, etc.). Validation Verified with a headless GDScript test that `SceneState` exposes these properties as plain strings — the same API the C++ parser uses internally: No new logic is introduced; the change is equivalent to the existing entries in `lookup_properties`. Note: `accessibility_name` and `accessibility_description` do **not** need to be added to `exception_list` — unlike `LineEdit::text` or `TextEdit::text`, these fields are author-defined labels (not user-entered content), so extracting them for translation is always correct.

title: Debugger Evaluator: Evaluate button starts clickable (not greyed out) even when expression field is empty
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: v4.6.stable.official [89cea1439] System information Godot v4.6.stable - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6636) - 12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-12900K (24 threads) - 63.75 GiB memory Issue description In the Debugger > Evaluator panel, the Evaluate button starts clickable = not greyed out when you show the panel for the first time during a given breakpoint hit, although the expression field may be empty. Clicking on it would do nothing. Typing some expression and delete it will force refresh and grey out the button. Submitting an expression to Evaluate via Enter or by clicking Evaluate button with some expression written will also force refresh and make it greyed out. **More context** I like the new Evaluator for quick expression evaluation, but it is quite confusing to use as a watcher. After evaluating a few expressions, I understood that those expressions were not updated on next breakpoint hit. At first I thought the Evaluate button was meant to refresh their values, but clicking it did nothing. Then I realised that clicking Evaluate button was actually the same as pressing Enter after entering an expression in the expression field, and that it was a bug that caused it not to be greyed out. As a matter of fact, to refresh a value you need to type it again and Evaluate it again, which is not very useful since at that point the history will show the same value again anyway. But that's another issue. In fact, I will probably open more issues and proposals about readability, automatic and active value refresh. title: Fix ScriptCreateDialog exclusive child error when adding Global Autoload
body: Fixes 117027 Enables transient_to_focused for FileSystemDock's ScriptCreateDialog. The error was caused by the ProjectSettingsEditor being set to the main window's exclusive transient child upon opening, which is then overwritten when the ScriptCreateDialog is opened from the Globals -> Autoload menu's "Add" button. The ScriptCreateDialog object is being retrieved from the FileSystemDock, where it is usually opened from the main window and does not cause the error. When transient_to_focused is enabled, the ScriptCreateDialog correctly sets either the main window's or the project settings window's exclusive transient child when opened from each, respectively. This change also has the effect of disallowing interaction with the settings menu until the script creation dialog is closed. Before this change, the following interaction would also cause one or more **CanvasItem RID leaks** notified after closing the editor: - Open script create dialog via Globals->Autoload->"Add" - Return to project settings dialog press "Add" again (May leak multiple RID's if changing the autoload name each time, or if pressing "Add" multiple times) - Close the script creation dialog (in any way) - Close the editor (with or without saving) I could not find any instances of similar behavior for other dialogs, but it may be worth investigating the above RID leaks if there are dialogs which can be opened from non-main sub-windows which do not enable transient_to_focused and can be reopened multiple times.

title: Profiler: Fix for graph panning with scrollbar (panning does not work on touchpad)
body: I decided the best way to fix this bug was to remove panning and add a scrollbar instead. No one really knew panning was available in this graph and panning did not give the user any indication where in the graph they had moved to. The scrollbar solves both of these problems because the scrollbar itself let's them know they can move around and it gives the user visual feedback where in the graph they are currently looking. Fixes 71112 Partially address 108840 There are also some bug that indicate the user was confused when they zoomed in on the graph and then cleared the data which causes the graph to remove it's lines. So they are zoomed in on a black area that is empty and they think the profiler is not working but it is. The scrollbar will fix this by making it obvious where in the graph they are. Some added and some removed lines of code in two files. No messy blocks of code with intermixed changes. ******************************* Disclosure and License -As a software engineer I use many systems to ensure code quality, and that includes AI. title: Running a compute shader with a texture that was shared with `RenderingDevice.texture_create_from_extension()` crashes the engine when using the D3D12 driver
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: v4.7.dev2, v4.6.1.stable System information Godot v4.7.dev2 - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Direct3D 12 (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 5700 XT (AMD; tested with both 25.10.2 and 26.2.2) - AMD Ryzen 5 3600 (12 threads) - 31.90 GiB memory Issue description Running a compute shader on a local rendering device with a texture that was shared from the main rendering device using `RenderingDevice.texture_create_from_extension()` causes a series of errors in the rendering backend, ultimately crashing the engine. This only occurs when using the D3D12 driver. When using the Vulkan driver, the compute shader executes without any errors and produces the expected result. The first error is thrown when the compute pipeline is submitted and stems from `RenderingDeviceDriverD3D12::command_buffer_end()` where `cmd_buf_info->cmd_list->Close()` returns `E_INVALIDARG`. Steps to reproduce 1. Use the D3D12 driver 2. Create a texture on the main rendering device 3. Share the texture with a local rendering device using `RenderingDevice.texture_create_from_extension()` 4. Use the texture in a compute list on the local rendering device Minimal reproduction project (MRP) MRP.zip
