title: Skeleton2D nodes with a non-empty modification_stack cannot be instantiated multiple times in the same scene
body: Tested versions v4.6.1.stable.mono.official.14d19694e System information Winwos11 - Godot v4.6.1.stable.mono.official.14d19694e - Vulkan 1.4.312 (Forward+) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 Laptop GPU Issue description When a scene containing a Skeleton2D child node, whose Skeleton2D node's modification_stack property is not empty, is instantiated multiple times in other scenes, a large number of duplicate error messages will be output, all stating "ERROR: Drawing is only allowed inside this node's _draw(), functions connected to its draw signal, or when it receives NOTIFICATION_DRAW." At this time, the editor becomes abnormally laggy, the 2D skeletal animation exhibits many anomalies, and sometimes the editor even crashes directly and becomes unresponsive. But if you set the `resource_local_to_scene` property of the SkeletonModificationStack2D resource to true, this problem will not occur. Steps to reproduce 1. **Create a Skeleton2D scene** - Create a new scene with a Node2D as root - Add a Skeleton2D node as child - Add a SkeletonModificationStack2D resource to the Skeleton2D node's `modification_stack` property - Add at least one modification (e.g., SkeletonModification2DLookAt) - Save this scene as `skeleton_scene.tscn` 2. **Create a test scene** - Create a new scene with a Node2D as root - Instance `skeleton_scene.tscn` multiple times (at least 2-3 instances) as children 3. **Reproduce the issue** - Run the test scene - Observe the console output - The following error will spam repeatedly: - The editor will become extremely laggy - 2D skeletal animations will display abnormally - In severe cases, the editor may crash or become unresponsive 4. **Expected behavior:** - No error messages - Smooth editor performance - Normal skeletal animation display 5. **Actual behavior:** - Multiple instances of the same scene cause continuous error spam - Editor performance degrades significantly - Skeletal animations fail to render correctly - Editor may crash when multiple instances are active - Setting `resource_local_to_scene = true` on the SkeletonModificationStack2D resource resolves the issue Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Test4.6.zip

title: Profiler: Fix for graph disappears when user stops and starts profiler while zoomed in
body: This is a bug where the user pauses the profiler and zooms into the graph. They then stop the profiler and restart it. Because the zoom did not get reset the user sees no profiler graph and thinks it's not working. Is it in fact working but the graph is way to the left because of the previous zoom state. Another way to reproduce this bug. Start profiling, zoom in to a section of the graph way to the right, press the "Clear" button in the profiler. The graph will disappear. This confuses the user who see an empty graph and thinks it's not working. It is working, it's just all the way to the left where they can't see it because of the zoom. This PR fixes the issue by moving (resetting the variable) the graph to where the user can see it. 1 line of code added to 1 file. That 1 line sets 1 variable to it's original initialized state when the user clears the data from the profiler graph. With this fix the user will no longer think the profiler graph is broken when they stop and start (or Clear) it while zoomed in. ******************************* Disclosure and License -As a software engineer I use many systems to ensure code quality, and that includes AI. title: Profiler: Fix for graph stopping when excessive zooming consumes the CPU
body: This is a bug where the user can overload the CPU and the profiler by continuous zooming in or out. The Godot editor app will slow way down and the profiler graph will stop updating. It looks like everything is stuck to the user. The issue was that the zoom was causing a re-draw of the profiler graph very quickly even if the graph was already at max zoom or min zoom. 8 line code change in 1 file. The new code stops the graph re-drawing after you have reached the min or max zoom. The Godot editor app will no longer temporary lock up. ******************************* Disclosure and License -As a software engineer I use many systems to ensure code quality, and that includes AI. title: `DPITexture` has incorrect alpha borders
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in v4.7.dev2.official [778cf54da] System information Godot v4.7.dev2 - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 3 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1070 Ti (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.8228) - AMD Ryzen 5 1500X Quad-Core Processor (8 threads) - 15.92 GiB memory Issue description "DPITexture" import does not have the "Fix Alpha Border" option, which means that in many circumstances it'll make these outlines (left). The "Texture2D" import (right) doesn't have this problem as it has "Fix Alpha Border" enabled by default. By manually disabling it you can get results that are practically identical in some circumstances. I deliberated for a while over whether this was technically a proposal but I eventually decided that it felt unintended, since "Fix Alpha Border" usually defaults to being enabled. Steps to reproduce 1. Import a `DPITexture` (with non-black borders), and place it onto a `Sprite2D`. - To better see the issue, place it on a background of a similar color (e.g. a `ColorRect` or even a copy of the same `Sprite2D`). 2. Adjust the `Sprite2D` in a way that it doesn't perfectly snap to the viewport. It's easiest to either: a. Zoom the editor viewport such that it is not a perfect multiple of the default b. Adjust the position, offset, rotation, or scale with non-integer values 3. There should be a fairly clearly visible black border. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) issue.zip

title: Inferred return values are breaking existing code
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in 4.7.dev2 System information Godot v4.7.dev2 - macOS Tahoe (26.3.0) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Metal (Forward+) - integrated Apple M1 Pro (Apple7) - Apple M1 Pro (10 threads) - 16.00 GiB memory Issue description It appears that Godot is assuming the return types of functions that do not have a return type specified. This is causing a lot of `Cannot return value of type "X" because the function return type is "Y"` errors in my plugin (GUT). I turned all "Error" to "Ignore" and the issue still happens. One simple example is I have a method that the user is expected to override if they want to make GUT skip a test script while running tests. GUT will skip the script if the user returns `true` or a `String`. All other values returned are treated as `false`. The `String` is displayed in the logs if it is returned. Here are two example implementations given in the wiki: In the editor it marks the string return as an error because it thinks that the return value is a `bool`. There are zero declarations of `should_skip_script` in the GUT project that have a declared return type. I attempted to declare a return type of `Variant`, but that causes parsing errors on the `DisplayServer` example, since not all paths return a value. This would be a feasible workaround, but it isn't a great solution. There could be many users that are using this example in CICD pipelines. This also appears to be affecting my dynamic Script doubling logic as there are many tests failing due to `Cannot return value of type ...` errors, or `A void function cannot return a value.` errors. I have not looked in that yet though, since the above case is way less complicated. I imagine it has to do with the fact that return types weren't supplied in the metadata, so all my wrappers `return super()` by default. Steps to reproduce Successful reproduction * clone https://github.com/bitwes/Gut * Open the editor * Click the "GUT" bottom panel * Click "Run All" Failed to reproduce I was unable to reproduce the issue with a simple project that had two inner classes in the same file that replicated the definitions of `should_skip_script`. I was also unable to replicate it with two files with `class_name` that replicated the definitions of `should_skip_script`. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) clone https://github.com/bitwes/Gut

title: Soften duplicate signal registration error in JNISingleton

This PR checks `ClassDB::has_signal` before calling `ADD_SIGNAL` and skips the duplicate registration with a warning instead of erroring out. title: NavMesh implementations finding bad paths due to faulty neighbour node updates
body: Tested versions Found while using 4.6.stable, has existed for some time, relevant code has been moved around / refactored so didn't dig too far back. System information Linux, Godot V4.6.stable + master Issue description Hi. I was making a demo app for a devlog to demonstrate why a nav mesh was not going to be suitable for my project, and after bolting on a 2D visibility graph and adding a few procedurally generated 'maps' and code to visualise things, I noticed some rather odd detours being taken in nav-mesh paths. While this was kind of great for my purposes, it felt a little OTT; there seemed to be clearly better corridors it should have taken, using polygons that would surely have been expanded. I was looking at something like the following, but I'm not going to remove the things I've since added, so spoiler alert ;P The orange path is the current implementation (the green is drawn over the top of yellow and orange at start and end). I believe the error is in updating previously visited nodes, specifically this line: modules/navigation_2d/2d/nav_mesh_queries_2d.cpp:299 `if (new_traveled_distance < neighbor_poly.traveled_distance) {` While this is perfectly normal looking for an A* implementation, it is making one very important assumption: that the heuristic does not change. In this not really A* we are doing that does not hold, with the poly position shifting depending on where we are coming from. The update condition needs to be made consistent with the priority queue. The 'tight' check with the !is_equal_approx() is required to avoid nodes constantly re-opening each other (inf loop), not common but if the geometry & start/end positions are just so... it happens; another consequence of the not really A* we are doing and the fact we have no concept of closed nodes here, and must always re-evaluate and potentially re-open previously visited nodes. How exactly the existing version checking only the travel_cost was avoiding this remains a mystery to me. Some more examples: These are mostly ones that are flattering to the fix of course. They often all find the same path (far less often on bigger meshes though), but that is a boring case to show; and the 'fixed' version sometimes finds a worse path, particularly on very small meshes (one included above, if I uploaded what I intended), but usually better or the same. To get a better/bigger picture, I collected stats from 10000 random pathing runs per map (and three seeds each for the procedural maps), I counted the number of times each achieved a 1st place (shortest path), 2nd, or 3rd (including ties for 1st and 2nd), and reported the percentage of times it achieved that 'rank', and then also the sum of all path distances and a percentage longer than optimal that is. The percentages of 1st and 3rd rankings are probably most interesting, and show the the fixed version generally performs better on all but the tiny toy map. Map1 is a tiny toy map, made in editor. Map2 is procedurally generated, 'buildings' roughly on a grid, fairly small Map3 extends Map2, offsets buildings from the grid a bit more and is much bigger Map4 is procedurally generated, maze-like, but not overly complicated, smallish Map5 is Map4 but bigger Map6 is large (area, not so much verts), editor made, intended to be an RTS like map This was all done in 2D, but the code for 3D is identical and will almost certainly have the same bug, I have not got to testing that yet. More testing with different maps/map-types would obviously be nice, but I've spent too long down this rabbit hole already and will be attending to other things for a while :-) I will get the demo project on github in the next day or two, it is obviously using a modified engine that can run the old code and the proposed fix at the same time. I will get a write up on what I did there and then drop an update here when both are done. Steps to reproduce Path on a (reasonably sized) mesh, observe odd detours from the ideal path. Modify code with proposed fix and try again :-) Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Hard to gauge what exactly is required for a minimal setup, but will be uploading demo soon (which is far from minimal, but is mostly visualisation and mesh generation code + a visibility graph in an extension)

title: Profiler: Fix for graph not displaying performance spikes at different zoom levels
body: Profiler: graph was not displaying performance spikes at different zoom levels. NOTE: BRAND NEW CODE AS OF 3/22/2026 While zooming in and out on the profiler graph the performance spikes would vanish. This was because the sampling method was skipping spike data as it picked data to display. The new method captures min and max over the entire sampling window so no high or low points are missed - no matter what the zoom scale is. Additionally before this fix the graph would flicker and jitter as the sample points were changing. Users have assumed something was broken with the graph and comment things like this in their bugs: "instead the graph starts to flicker or something." This is from 76146 *********** The old code would not draw the graph correctly. The new code does draw it correctly and has a touch better performance. Speed before code fix to display performance spikes correctly: _update_plot 100 runs total: 168466 usec, average: 1684 usec per call - graph is starting to fill up _update_plot 100 runs total: 357756 usec, average: 3577 usec per call _update_plot 100 runs total: 537164 usec, average: 5371 usec per call - graph is full from here on _update_plot 100 runs total: 566930 usec, average: 5669 usec per call _update_plot 100 runs total: 584532 usec, average: 5845 usec per call _update_plot 100 runs total: 586033 usec, average: 5860 usec per call _update_plot 100 runs total: 592993 usec, average: 5929 usec per call Speed after code fix to display performance spikes correctly: _update_plot 100 runs total: 167741 usec, average: 1677 usec per call - graph is starting to fill up _update_plot 100 runs total: 340753 usec, average: 3407 usec per call _update_plot 100 runs total: 488717 usec, average: 4887 usec per call - graph is full from here on _update_plot 100 runs total: 521295 usec, average: 5212 usec per call _update_plot 100 runs total: 519643 usec, average: 5196 usec per call _update_plot 100 runs total: 532377 usec, average: 5323 usec per call _update_plot 100 runs total: 546293 usec, average: 5462 usec per call *********** NOTE: BRAND NEW MOVIES AS OF 3/22/2026 before fix simple.webm after fix simple.webm Here are a before the fix video and an after the fix video. Both these video show the profiler just running - no zoom, no panning, no user input. You can see the frame count incrementing in the upper right corner so data is coming in and the graph is moving to the left as that new data comes in. In the before my code fix video the graph is very jittery and the performance spikes appear and disappear. In the after my code fix video the graph motion is smooth and the performance spikes do NOT disappear. I'd rate this as a high importance fix because the point of the profiler graph is to find the performance spikes. *********** All code changes are in 1 method only. The graph will now never hide performance issues from the user. ******************************* Disclosure and License -As a software engineer I use many systems to ensure code quality, and that includes AI. I use the AI to: do detailed code analysis, be a sounding board for my different ideas, look for bugs, find unexpected behaviors, identify performance hot spots, expensive CPU calls, race conditions, poorly placed variable initializations, possible optimizations, and many more things. I do this during development and before I push code to the repo. It lists items that are potential problems and I decide what I think is important and make those changes. -For a descriptive example of my AI usage on a particular PR please read this https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117066issuecomment-4061773098 -Any and all code attached to this PR falls under the coverage of the MIT License.

title: Deadlock when quitting during pipeline compilation
body: Tested versions - Reproduciable in v4.6.1 System information Godot v4.6.1.stable.mono - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 3 monitors - Direct3D 12 (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2070 SUPER (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.8180) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-14900K (32 threads) - 31.80 GiB memory Issue description When using the multi threaded rendering, `WorkerThreadPool` might deadlock, if you call `GetTree().Quit(1);` while it is compiling a shader pipeline. Order of events causing deadlock: 1. `GetTree().Quit(1);` is called. 2. Main thread enters `WorkerThreadPool::exit_languages_threads()`. 3. All threads enter/report back to `RUNLEVEL_PRE_EXIT_LANGUAGES`. 4. Worker thread 0 (RenderingServer Thread) starts new flush/draw (see call stack below). 5. Main thread moves forward and switches runlevel to `RUNLEVEL_EXIT_LANGUAGES` and enters the next loop to wait for all threads to detach from scripting language. 6. Worker thread 0 (RenderingServer Thread) enters `PipelineHashMapRD::compile_pipeline`, calls `WorkerThreadPool::add_template_task`, which in turn ends up in `WorkerThreadPool::_post_tasks`. It finally reaches the `while (runlevel == RUNLEVEL_EXIT_LANGUAGES)` loop. 7. Now the main thread is indefinetly waiting for the remaining thread to exit, while the remaining worker thread 0 is indefinetly waiting for the runlevel to change. It appears the underlying issue is, that the rendering thread is still able to post a new task while run level is already at `RUNLEVEL_PRE_EXIT_LANGUAGES` or `RUNLEVEL_EXIT_LANGUAGES`. Call stack of Main thread: Call stack of Worker thread 0 (RenderServer thread): Steps to reproduce 1. Enable multi threaded rendering. 2. `GetTree().Quit(1);` right in the first frame 3. Have enough nodes in the scene to get the timing right 🫠 Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Here's a repro project. To enforce the bug you have to change the engine by adding a `OS::get_singleton()->delay_usec(500'000);` at this line worker_thread_pool.cppL851 deadlock-bug.zip

title: GDScript: Fix and simplify coroutine stack clearing
body: * Fixes 117049. * Fixes 117164. * Regression from 116711. This PR simplifies coroutine stack clearing. When a coroutine resumed, the stack was previously stored in multiple `GDScriptFunctionState`s (via the `Ref<GDScriptFunctionState> first_state;` field). Now, previous `GDScriptFunctionState`s are no longer held in memory. After a suspended function resumes, responsibility for the stack passes to `GDScriptFunction::call()`, which cleans it up at the end as usual. The `first_state` field was introduced in 19828. I made sure that this change doesn't reintroduce 19823: Before, it was `previous_state`, introduced in 17291. I made sure that this change doesn't reintroduce 17280:

title: Fix stale texture preview in inspector
body: Motivation Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117048 In a project I'm working on, I have a custom resource which contains a Texture2D. When replacing one of the textures on disk and allowing Godot to reimport it, the preview thumbnail shown in the EditorResourcePicker continued displaying the old image. The only way to have the preview reflect the new image was to close the scene and reopen. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a167bd6c-5baf-4c0b-bcb4-20023c013ed0 Background: EditorResourcePreview maintains an in-memory cache of generated preview thumbnails. It uses two kinds of keys: path-based (ex: `res://rock.png`) and instance-ID-based (ex: `ID:123`). Path entries are requested via queue_resource_preview and are used by things like the filesystem dock. ID-based entries are requested via queue_edited_resource_preview and are used by EditorResourcePicker in the inspector. For ID-based lookups, the cache is considered valid as long as hash_edited_version_for_preview() matches the stored hash. Entries are invalidated via check_for_invalidation, which compares the file's current modification time against the cached value and erases the entry if they differ. Changes editor/inspector/editor_resource_preview.cpp - check_for_invalidation now evicts the ID-based preview cache entry for the affected resource in addition to the path-based one. editor/inspector/editor_resource_picker.cpp - EditorResourcePicker now listens to the preview_invalidated signal, reloads the in-memory resource via reload_from_file(), and re-requests the preview Testing Manually tested, see video: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7baf450e-4410-4e6a-970c-aeb5c0b1c2b0

title: Fix script editor export drop modifier drop line
body: - related https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/95914 - related https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109448 Dropping nodes, files, or resources with export drop modifier now puts them onto a new line instead of in the middle of a line. Dropping multiple files with export drop modifier now puts each of them on a new line instead of being comma-separated if not on empty line. This does not apply to the preload modifier, since that can be used within a line (like when making an array). Dropping nodes with either the member modifier or export modifier now put it on a new line if the line was indented, even if it was otherwise empty. This is because they don't work when they are indented. If you dropped into a function, it will still have errors but at the end of a function is now fine. Dropping on empty line behavior is now unified, the empty line will be preserved when dropping anything on an empty line. So it now includes properties and nodes without modifiers (and nodes with export modifier), everything else already does it. This fixes dropping a resource not indenting its new empty line to match the current indentation.

title: Fix unknown identifier `source_texture` in `texture_blit` visual shader
body: Fixes 117019 This PR corrects the naming of `source_texture` inputs for `Shader::MODE_TEXTURE_BLIT` in visual shaders. Previously, the inputs had an inconsistent naming scheme (e.g., `source_texture`, `source_texture2`). This resulted in `unknown identifier "source_texture"` when sampling from one of the source textures. The naming of the uniform declarations has been copied (`source_texture0`, `source_texture1`, `source_texture2`, `source_texture3`), resolving those errors.

title: [Buildsystem] Remove "-s" flag from Emscripten when building without debug symbols
body: As far as I found "-s" flag only applies to GCC, but if Clang worked fine before I decided to not touch it and only remove it from Emscripten. Emscripten can use "-s" flag with space to read build options, which is not something that we want here. I think release link flags went something like "-s -Os", so I guess if it meets another flag beginning with "-" it parses the next flag instead, but it shouldn't be passed to emscripten without build option in the first place.

title: Use IDs instead of hashes for TrackCache keys
body: Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116231 Hello, I've been tracking this issue since `4.6.0-stable` made it apparent. This is a solution that implements re-hashing on hash collisions for `track_cache`. As far as I'm aware, using `StringName` for hash was the latest solution proposed here (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110439) by @beicause for hash collisions. I pulled the last commit there and tested it, and found it decreasing the performance a bit. `uint32_t` comparisons are faster so I wanted to keep them. In order to both fix the collision issue and keep using `uint32_t`, I made 3 implementations and tested them: 1. Dynamic double hash (Using double `AHashMap`) - `track_cache` holds `(TypeHash, HashBucket *)` pairs where `HashBucket` contains a `union` that is either a `TrackCache *` or another `AHashMap` 2. Adding a linked list in `TrackCache` struct - the `struct` simply holds `next` and `prev` pointers - adds to the list in case of collisions and iterates the whole list per lookup 3. Re-hashing the hash that collides with a `uint32_t probe` value until it no longer collides within the `track_cache` - Nothing changes, just the `thash` value is probed when a collision is detected - All hashes are inserted into a static `AHashMap` and that map is checked during insertion These solutions are either proposed or mentioned in other hash related PR discussions: (the one referenced above and https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110400)(https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110439). I tried not to touch other parts of the code and only focus on hash. My benchmark results for the provided MRP in (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110474) with my system (Ryzen5 1600, RTX2060) is as follows: | Stable | Dynamic Double Hash | Rehash | Linked List | StringName -- | -- | -- | -- | -- | -- Avg FPS | 127.72 | 116.38 | 127.5 | 124.84 | 110.04 Avg MSPF | 7.83 | 8.59 | 7.84 | 8.01 | 9.08 Since that mrp does not contain any hash collisions, these can be considered as amortized performance. All except rehashing, requires additional checks to see if the hash key maps to a unique track or a bucket; and another lookup after that in case of collisions. So as expected, their runtime performances are worse (more than I expected). Also, collisions occur very rarely, so making additional checks that costs a few frames for a very rare case made no sense to me. Therefore, moving the cost to track creation time seemed like the best option. My implementation is not exactly what @TokageItLab proposed (with deferred calls and all) because I didn't understand how to do that :laughing:. I tested this PR with mrp-rehash.zip . It contains 3 tracks in animation player that generates the same hash in v4.6. Adding/removing, undo/redo, works fine in my tests. I tested these only in 2D.

title: Fix quick load for resources imported by editor import plugins
body: Fixes 72639 This PR fixes script classes not being recognised in imported resources (such as from custom EditorImportPlugin scripts) which is the root cause of them not showing up in the editor's quick load resource picker. Previously the script class was never registered in the editor file system on first import (in `EditorFileSystem::_reimport_file`). It also wouldn't get set correctly when refreshed (e.g. from `EditorFileSystem::update_file`) without the `ResourceFormatImporter::get_resource_script_class` method override to remap the original file path to its saved imported resource path.

title: Playing audio with AudioStreamPlayer on web by any means other than play() method ignores volume_db and pitch_scale
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: - 4.7.dev1, 4.6.1-stable, 4.6-stable (all tests except for play() ignore pitch_scale on web. AnimationPlayer ignores volume_db too) - 4.5.1-stable, 4.4.1-stable (same as above, but AnimationPlayer also ignores volume_db and pitch_scale on desktop) - 4.3-stable (same as above, but AnimationPlayer ignores volume_db and pitch_scale on web, and only pitch_scale on desktop) - Not reproducible in: 4.2.2-stable (before the Playback Type option was implemented) System information Godot v4.6.1.stable - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3050 Laptop GPU (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.9174) - AMD Ryzen 7 7435HS (16 threads) - 15.82 GiB memory Issue description On a web export, when playing a sound with an AudioStreamPlayer by any means other than directly using the play() method, the audio ignores the `volume_db` and `pitch_scale` properties. This includes: - Autoplay - Looping (in the MRP it starts ignoring after 3 loops, but I've seen it ignore after the first loop in other projects with longer sounds) - AnimationPlayer audio track The intended behavior is for the audio to always play with the `volume_db` and `pitch_scale` settings set by the AudioStreamPlayer. This issue can be avoided by changing the `playback_type` on the AudioStreamPlayer to "Stream", which is not the default on web. I'm aware that the default Sample playback type has some limitations, however, the inconsistencies between Godot versions and ways of playing sound leads me to believe this is a bug rather than intended behavior. This is also not mentioned as a limitation in the documentation. Live demo here: https://files.fupi.cat/fupicat/etc/web-audio-bugs/index.html Tested on both Firefox and Chrome, same issue on both. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8705ab38-af02-469e-a9ab-edabeb0be204 Steps to reproduce Minimal reproduction (autoplay) - Create an AudioStreamPlayer and load a stream. - Set `autoplay` to `true`. - Export to web and play the scene. title: Add explicit OptionButton fallback state in ScriptCreateDialog
body: This PR adds explicit defensive state handling to the `Language` OptionButton in `ScriptCreateDialog`. **The Context:** While investigating and fixing a compilation bug related to `module_gdscript_enabled=no` (see PR 117013), I discovered that `ScriptCreateDialog` relies on implicit UI behavior when GDScript is missing. **The Current Behavior:** Currently, if a user builds with `module_gdscript_enabled=no` but keeps another language active (like Cvia `module_mono_enabled=yes`), the `default_language` index remains `-1`. The `OptionButton` only avoids a broken blank state because the Godot UI framework implicitly auto-selects index `0` when an item is added to an empty dropdown. Furthermore, if zero languages are registered, the dialog has no internal fallback state (relying entirely on an outer-wall check from `SceneTreeDock` to prevent it from opening). **The Fix:** This PR makes the dialog's state handling explicit and resilient: 1. If zero languages are registered, the `OptionButton` is explicitly disabled with a fallback warning text. 2. If languages exist but GDScript is disabled, it explicitly calls `select(0)` to default to the first available language, rather than relying on the UI node's implicit auto-selection. This future-proofs the dialog against potential changes to `OptionButton`'s default behavior and provides a hard internal fail-safe for the zero-language edge case. Fixes 116873. - Supersedes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110532 - Supersedes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/98805

title: Fix compilation error with `module_gdscript_enabled=no` by adding explicit `EditorSyntaxHighlighter` include
body: Fixes a module dependency logic error in `editor/doc/editor_help.cpp` by explicitly including the header file `editor/script/syntax_highlighters.h`. Currently, `EditorSyntaxHighlighter` is being transitively accessed via GDScript dependencies. When compiling the engine with GDScript disabled (`scons module_gdscript_enabled=no`), the build fails with an "invalid use of incomplete type" error because the class blueprint is missing. This PR makes the inclusion explicit, allowing the engine to compile successfully when `module_gdscript_enabled=no` (such as when building a C-only engine with `module_mono_enabled=yes`).

title: TileMapLayer CPU performance degrades on repeat runs in Editor
body: Tested versions Tested in Godot Engine v4.6.stable.official, on my 2021 M1 MacBook Pro. Encountered this when measuring performance of my MultiRect PR, but determined the issue exists outside of my changes. System information Godot v4.6.stable - macOS Tahoe (26.2.0) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Metal (Forward+) - integrated Apple M1 Max (Apple7) - Apple M1 Max (10 threads) - 64.00 GiB memory Issue description In editor, running a scene with a performance intensive large TileMapLayer performs worse on repeated launches. Forward + Renderer, on my target scene: * Initial run from Editor averages 10 CPU ms per frame. * Repeated runs from editor average 14 CPU ms per frame. Compatibility Renderer, on my target scene: * Initial run from Editor averages 26 CPU ms per frame. * Repeated runs from editor average 37 CPU ms per frame. In both renderers, running the scene for the first time from a fresh restart of the editor performs better than subsequent runs, and every run that is not the first seems to take the same performance hit. Something must be 'persisting' across started/stopped scenes that makes every repeat perform worse than the first. Steps to reproduce 1. Launch Editor 2. Run Current Scene with a CPU intensive tilemap Scene 3. Observe CPU Frame time with Profiler 4. Stop running current scene 5. Run current Scene again 6. Observe degraded CPU Frame Time performance. 7. Stop running current scene 8. Restart the editor 9. Run Current Scene 10. Observe fresh restart produces better performance. title: GDScript: Track columns by raw string offsets
body: GDScript keeps track of the position of tokens/nodes/errors/..., however at the moment the column of this position counts tab characters not as one character but by the currently configured tab width. This PR changes this tracking to always count tabs as a single character in position information. With this PR GDScript still tracks positions one-based while other systems use zero-based positions. It's not worth changing that, the conversion isn't complex and there is a lot more stuff which relies on line numbers. This PR also includes a fix to the functionality from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/75216 to actually get the links to jump to the right positions. Motivation This current position format is pretty unusual and requires conversions in adjacent systems. This change in particular is motivated by the language server. In order to convert positions from the parser to something we can send to external editors we always need to be aware of the associated source code. Without going into detail, this isn't exactly great if we want to share more code and utilities between the builtin editor and the LSP implementation. _Triage edit: Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117546_

Workarounds: Before the issue occurs: - remember to `shift + tab` to the replace input box before pressing `esc` After the issue has occurred: - mouse click on the script editor to be able to continue typing - restore focus from the keyboard with: - `ctrl + F1` switch to the 2d view - `ctrl + F3` switch to the script editor Where the issue doesn't occur: The script editor becomes focused when the find or replace input boxes are focused and `esc` is pressed. Steps to reproduce - open the project: issue-script-editor-loses-focus-on-esc.zip - open `test.gd` - press: `ctrl + f` - type: `abc` - press: `ctrl + r` - press: `tab` to focus the button: Replace - press: `esc` - the find and replace bar closes, - issue, the script editor isn't focused. Expected: Focus the script editor when the find and replace bar is closed with `esc`. This already happens when the find or replace input boxes are focused and `esc` is pressed. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) issue-script-editor-loses-focus-on-esc.zip

title: Find previous, skips occurrences

An even number of occurrences skips the odd occurrences, and an odd number of occurrences skips the even occurrences. Steps to reproduce - open the project: issue-find-previous-skips-occurrences.zip - open `test1.gd` - press: `ctrl + f` - type: `abc` - press: `enter` a couple of times to see that it cycles through the occurrences as expected - hold: `shift` and press: `enter` a couple of times - issue, the find highlight stops on the second occurrence, even though the matches counter shows all numbers: 2 of 2 matches 1 of 2 matches - open `test2.gd` here find previous (shift + enter), skips the second occurrence The next two examples add one more line each `test3.gd` and `test4.gd` These examples show that an even number of occurrences skips the odd occurrences, and an odd number of occurrences skips the even occurrences. Where the issue doesn't happen: `test5.gd` doesn't skip any occurrences. It doesn't reassign any variables, it just creates 5 different variables. title: UI flickering in Hyper-V GPU-PV environments when sub-windows open (OpenGL Compatibility renderer)
body: Tested versions -Reproducible in v4.7.dev (master, commit 1aaea38e7f) System information Windows 11 in Hyper-V VM with GPU-PV (paravirtualized NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090), OpenGL API 3.3.0 NVIDIA 591.86 - Compatibility renderer Issue description Severe UI flickering occurs in the Godot editor when running in a Hyper-V VM with GPU paravirtualization (GPU-PV). The main editor window renders fine, but as soon as any sub-window opens (e.g., Create Project dialog, popups, file dialogs), all windows begin flickering/drawing black intermittently. In this environment, Vulkan device creation fails entirely (VK_ERROR_INITIALIZATION_FAILED), so the engine falls back to OpenGL Compatibility. With OpenGL, all windows share a single GL rendering context and wglMakeCurrent() switches between window HDCs. The GPU-PV driver struggles with this context switching, causing the flickering. Workaround: Running with --single-window eliminates the flickering completely, since all sub-windows are embedded in the main window and no wglMakeCurrent switching is needed. Other approaches tested (none resolved it): - DwmFlush() per-window and end-of-frame — no effect - Deferred SwapBuffers (render all viewports, then swap all) — no effect - Disabling vsync — no effect - Suppressing WM_ERASEBKGND — minimal improvement - Vulkan extension stripping/retry on vkCreateDevice — driver fails regardless of extensions Suggestion: Auto-detect GPU-PV environments (hypervisor detected + OpenGL active + Vulkan failed) and automatically enable single-window/embedded sub-windows mode. Detection could use IsProcessorFeaturePresent(PF_VIRT_FIRMWARE_ENABLED) on Windows. Steps to reproduce: 1. Set up a Hyper-V VM with GPU-PV (paravirtualized GPU sharing) 2. Install Godot editor 3. Launch the editor — main window renders fine 4. Click "Create New Project" or open any dialog 5. Observe severe flickering across all windows Steps to reproduce 1. Set up a Hyper-V VM with GPU-PV (paravirtualized GPU sharing) 2. Install Godot editor 3. Launch the editor — main window renders fine 4. Click "Create New Project" or open any dialog 5. Observe severe flickering across all windows Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Minimal reproduction project is N/A — requires Hyper-V GPU-PV hardware environment.

title: GDScript: Disallow implicit use of packed arrays as constant expressions
body: * Closes 67873. * Closes 88753. * Closes 100458. * See also 116947. * See also the discussion in the Contributors Chat. We can't implement the functionality required for immutable packed arrays in the core (the `make_read_only()` method, as is done for `Array` and `Dictionary`) because it introduces unnecessary overhead. Packed arrays (and the `Vector<T>` template, widely used in the engine) are designed for performance, even at the cost of convenience. Alternatively, we could fix the crash for constant packed arrays but not implement immutability. This would mean that the contents of constant packed arrays would be mutable, like `Array` and `Dictionary` in 3.x. However, this option would be inconsistent with the behavior of `Array` and `Dictionary` in 4.x. Furthermore, there could be issues with constant dictionaries (if you use packed arrays as keys). Also, in some places, the compiler might rely on the immutability of constant expressions.

title: Crash, sticky keys, shift then esc, filter by events, editor shortcuts
body: Tested versions Reproducible in: - v4.6.1.stable.official [14d19694e] - v4.7.dev1.official [bf95b6258] System information Godot v4.6.1.stable - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - GeForce GTX 550 Ti - Intel(R) Core(TM) i3-2100 CPU @ 3.10GHz (4 threads) - 11.95 GiB memory Issue description Godot crashes when win 11 sticky keys is enabled, and the following keys are pressed in: editor settings > shortcuts > filter by events - press and release: `shift` - press and release: `esc` Godot freezes for about three seconds, then it closes. Starting Godot in the Windows Terminal shows: Sticky keys don't have to be enabled, it just makes the crash easy to reproduce, because Godot can also crash when sticky keys is disabled, by pressing and releasing both, `shift` and `esc` at the same time, but it usually takes a couple of tries. When sticky keys is enabled, then Godot crashes every time `shift` is pressed followed by `esc`. Godot does not crash when holding `shift` and pressing `esc`, when sticky keys is either enabled or disabled. This might not be related, but: Pressing `shift + esc` in Notepad++ shows an `ESC` character, when enabling: View > Show Symbols > Show Control Characters & Unicode EOL Steps to reproduce - open: win 11, settings, accessibility, keyboard - enable: sticky keys - start Godot - create a new project - in the top menu choose: editor > editor settings... - at the top of the editor settings window, click on the tab: shortcuts - at the top right click in the input box: Filter by Event - press and release: `shift` (shift remains pressed because of sticky keys) - press `esc` - Godot freezes for about three seconds, and sometimes the mouse pointer shows an hourglass - Godot closes It's also possible to crash Godot when sticky keys is disabled, by pressing and releasing both, `shift` and `esc` at the same time, but it usually takes a couple of tries. When sticky keys is enabled, then Godot crashes every time `shift` is pressed followed by `esc`. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A. It happens with a new empty project.

title: Fix iOS simulator linking for x86_64 and EditorLog URL crash
body: When exporting an iOS project, the generated `.xcarchive` stayed in the export directory, which poisoned Xcode's linker search paths for simulator builds with arm64 device dylibs. This commit moves the .xcarchive straight to the standard macOS Archives directory `(~/Library/Developer/Xcode/Archives/YYYY-MM-DD/)` so it fixes the simulator linking issue and allows the build to instantly appear inside Xcode Organizer. Additionally, this adds explicit URI schema checks (`file://`, `http://`) to `EditorLog::_meta_clicked`, preventing a 64-bit integer overflow crash caused by attempting to parse system file paths with colons as script line numbers. --- This bug reproduces in: - Godot 4.6.1 stable and 4.7 dev/master branches. - Mac Intel x86_64 - Xcode Version 26.2 (17C52) Steps to reproduce: - Export to iOS normally - On Xcode, build to simulator Bug: Changes in export:

title: Fix crackle when changing volume of audio bus
body: Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/32882 Just adding another interpolation step later on. Probably is not the correct approach since `AudioServer::_mix_step_for_channel` SHOULD already do this, but for some reason doesn't seem to work properly. Still wanted to open PR for visibility and in case anybody wants to use this fix for their projects. title: 4.6 Regression: Crash on saving w/ custom classes
body: Tested versions Reproducible in 4.6-stable, 4.6.1 Not in 4.5.1-stable, not in c5e6fde0c7e25608e8866abde2a72dc903048bf2 System information Godot v4.6.1.stable - Devuan GNU/Linux 6 (excalibur) 6 on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 SUPER (nvidia) - AMD Ryzen 5 2600 Six-Core Processor (12 threads) - 15.55 GiB memory Issue description @Dj1nus discovered that PR 109458 `Add indicator to linked resources` introduced code that causes a crash in Terrain3D, and presumably any class that implements _get_property_list() and _get() in the way we have. https://github.com/godotengine/godot-cpp/pull/1904 Did help improve this and fix my crashing on windows. However DJ is still crashing on linux. These crashes begin in our _get_property_list() or _get(), but end often in the STL or cowdata. Here are several call stacks, which are all very different Callstacks.txt, though the code and process is the same. Here's our _get_property_list(), and our _get(). Steps to reproduce * Create a new project with 4.6+ * Download this build https://github.com/TokisanGames/Terrain3D/actions/runs/22576817642 * Unzip it in your project folder * Open the demo * Select the Terrain3D node, and repeatedly save. No need to change anything. It might take up to 60 saves. This build uses the godot-cpp 4.5 branch, dcfab8de26e62c17b0f06c796125a63b1e437569, and includes the godot-cpp fix https://github.com/godotengine/godot-cpp/pull/1904. I can't reproduce it on windows anymore. I used to be able to before the godot-cpp fix, as shown below. DJ can still produce it on demand in Linux, after the offending commit 804188d320a1d6405d8f42c6277b511b51bd005e, but not in the previous c5e6fde0c7e25608e8866abde2a72dc903048bf2. There are more details on our downstream issue https://github.com/TokisanGames/Terrain3D/issues/945. ---- Archived Both of us have independently confirmed that the crash occurs after that PR in 804188d320a1d6405d8f42c6277b511b51bd005e, and not in the previous commit c5e6fde0c7e25608e8866abde2a72dc903048bf2. It occurs on windows or linux. The error messages, crashes and call stacks are very different each time. Often but not always crashing in the STL, or cowdata or memory.cpp. For instance here are callstacks from four crashes errors.txt, and here's from another. There are more details on our downstream issue https://github.com/TokisanGames/Terrain3D/issues/945. Here is our implementation of _get_property_list() v1.0.1 for the version in the MRP below. However if we need to redesign our function for the new version of Godot, it will be based on this implementation of _get_property_list() v1.1-dev. Here is a build of our `main` branch that matches this code. Steps to reproduce * Create a new project * Download Terrain3D from the asset library * Open the demo * Select the Terrain3D node * Save repeatedly. It should crash in 1-50 saves in 4.6, but not 4.5.

title: Overhaul scene loading and edit state management
body: This PR makes a major overhaul on how scenes are loaded in the editor and how their edit state is handled. - `load_scene()` was renamed to `open_scene()` - Added _new_ `load_scene()` method that only loads a scene. The scene is not even added to the scene tree. - `open_scene()` calls `load_method()` and then switches to the loaded scene. - When a scene is loaded (using the new method), it's edit state is loaded into EditorData, instead of directly into plugins - When a scene is loaded, the selected nodes are loaded into EditorData, instead of directly into EditorSelection - When scenes are being restored on load, they are only loaded using the new `load_scene()`. - Together with the simplified initialization, this should make scene restoring faster and more stable. - When a scene is closed, its edit state is saved to a file. - Example fixed bug: Move 2D editor view, close the scene and open it again. The camera was being reset. - Selected nodes are now stored as paths relative to root, instead of absolute paths. - This makes Node selection stored in `editstate` files resilient to editor structure changes - However it will cause one-time errors when loading old projects in 4.7 (I think?) - Removed various deferred calls. Scenes now open completely, without running extra logic "sometime later" - This also allowed to simplify some code, like `auto_select_current_scene_file` setting - Reloading a scene using a path that does not match any open scene now results in an error. - ~~Seems like this made CI fail xd~~ Fixes 116867 Fixes 116865 Might fix some other issues.

title: [BUG] bone2d polygon assignment does not persist after edit
body: Tested versions Noticed in 4.6.1 stable on debian. System information Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie) x86_64 Issue description issue When assigning bones to polygons in the bone2d polygon editor the assignments do not persist, leading to incorrect bone structure. The user has no way to correct this issue making the skeleton2d feature unusable in 4.6.1. Additionally, the design requiring a "sync bones" button to be manually pressed by the user each time they view this editor for each polygon is not desirable and could also lead to incorrect bone structure even if this bug were not present. desired behavior A fix should make it so the users assignment of polygon to bone persists even after the user moves focus on to a different edit menu. The users assignment should persist even if the user changes the skeletal structure—adding or removing unrelated bones. (ie. if the assigned bone has been deleted then it makes sense that its polygon assignment also should be deleted. however, if an unrelated bone has been deleted, it should not affect the polygon assignment for the other bones) Additionally, a fix should reconsider the design of the editor in such a way as to not force the user to visit each polygon and manually sync the polygon to the skeleton. Steps to reproduce Steps to reproduce: - create a tree of body part polygons a-la the tutorial (arms belong to torso node, head belongs to torso, forearm belongs to arms, etc...) - move all nodes so it looks like the character is standing in a natural position - edit the pivot points of each polygon so the pivot lies at the joint - use the "make skeleton 2d" command to generate bones. This command will generate an incorrect skeleton structure (separate issue) - fix the mess the "make skeleton 2d" command made by re-assigning the parentage of all bones so they resemble the hierarchy you originally built with the polygon nodes - re-parent the whole tree to a skeleton2d node and set its rest position. There should now be no warnings in the scene tree. - Select each polygon and assign the skeleton node to the skeleton property - For each polygon, go to the bone2d editor in the bottom panel and "sync" the bones - For each polygon, go to the bone2d editor and assign the correct bone for each polygon (arm polygon maps to arm bone, etc...) - go back to the first polygon, notice how your edited bone assignment is now incorrect! https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8501156c-6b48-4113-aa0a-e58aaa2b125b Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Please ignore my silly puppet character that has my face on it. (*facepalm*) Its for another project (lol) skeleton-2d-polygon-assignment-bug.zip

title: Fix direct physics states observing outdated data when using separate physics thread
body: Fixes 113270 I tried the reported issue on 4.4, and there I was getting raycast hits ~90% of the frames. On the latest versions, raycast misses 100% of the frames (except for the first frame). I then tried bisecting it and arrived at pull request 109591. After a bit more digging, the bug unfolds like this: - Physics iteration begins - Physics server is sync'ed and transforms are read back into nodes via `flush_queries` - During readback, the `Area3D` gets a notification that its global transform has changed, and it sets the new transform to the server. - The transform changes because the `Area3D` is parented to a `RigidBody3D` - One of the following then happens: 1. Without threading, the new transform immediately propagates and updates the physics engine's query tree 2. With threading, on latest versions, the command to update is queued up, but nothing happens as the server is paused 3. With threading, on older versions, the command to update is queued up and starts executing at some point - The `physics_process` callback is invoked and a raycast is performed 1. Without threading, the raycast succeeds as the query tree is up to date 2. With threading, on latest versions, the raycast misses as the query tree is out of date 3. With threading, on older versions, the raycast sometimes misses and sometimes hits, based on how fast the physics thread is The proposed change flushes any pending commands when retrieving direct state access. This is the same behavior as calling any other server function that returns a value. With the change, spatial queries and other direct state functions are able to operate on latest data. It's still possible to perform a spatial query on an outdated tree by: 1. Getting a direct space state 2. Setting some transform to the server 3. Performing the spatial query But this change at least covers the bug that occurs without using any low-level access.

title: Fix project save crash
body: Fixes 116431 **Description:** When creating or opening a project in a directory protected by OS-level security (such as Windows Defender Controlled Folder Access), `ProjectSettings::get_singleton()->save()` returns an `ERR_UNAUTHORIZED` (or similar file I/O error). Previously, during `NOTIFICATION_READY` in `editor_node.cpp`, this return value was ignored. The engine would swallow the error and continue the boot sequence without a valid `project.godot` file or accessible `.godot/` cache directory. This resulted in a static editor state, leading to a massive cascade of file-access errors and eventual segfaults when trying to write filesystem caches or script documentation upon exit. **The Fix:** Added a check to capture the `Error` returned by the initial `ProjectSettings::get_singleton()->save()`. If the save fails (`!= OK`), the engine now intercepts the failure, triggers a native `OS::get_singleton()->alert()` to explicitly warn the user about folder permissions/antivirus blocking, and immediately calls `get_tree()->quit()` to cleanly abort the initialization before the editor can enter a corrupted state. **Testing:** - Enabled Windows Defender Controlled Folder Access. - Attempted to open a project in a protected directory. - Verified that the editor correctly halts, displays the native OS error dialog, and exits cleanly with code 0 upon acknowledgment, preventing any cascading cache errors.

title: Performance: Godot Android Configuration Plugin burns CPU
body: Tested versions The Godot Android configuration changes from 14e6696c8e3f79db64c37eff8bcdebfa2f99bc57 introduced a constant polling on every frame for changes to the export configuration of Android, and this shows up when Godot is idling and is a major source of battery drain - for a longer discussion see https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116845 What this does is to call EditorExport::update_export_presets on every single frame being rendered. Ideally, we should have these plugins raise an even that the EditorNode can respond to, or post a notification about a configuration change taking place, rather than having this busy looping happening. I am opening this bug, so we can have a focused discussion on what needs to be solved here. System information Any system Issue description Major battery drain in the editor when idling. Steps to reproduce Open any Godot project, let it sit without doing anything, attach a profiler, watch it burn battery. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Any project will do.

title: NavigationServer3D.AgentGetPosition does not report the correct position in the RVO backend
body: Tested versions Is reproducible in godot 4.5.1 and based on the underlying cause can traced back at least to commit a6ac305 (69988). System information Godot v4.5.1.stable.mono - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.9186) - AMD Ryzen 7 5800X 8-Core Processor (16 threads) - 63.90 GiB memory Issue description There is an issue involving a difference between the position of an RVO agent (in the library code) and the godot agent (using its own position). I would expect the library to always provide such velocity, where if used, collision should be impossible. Whether that happens correctly or not is not exactly the point of this bug report. From the observation that either does not happen or I am doing something wrong (once again not the main point). The problem which arises is that in such circumstances (and I did not check the RVO lib code thoroughly to really pinpoint what is happening), when collision happens or I guess is inevitable, it moves one or possibly both of the agents out of the collision. Whether this is correct behavior or not I will leave up to you. The problem is, that the changed position is not reflected in the value which _NavigationServer3D.AgentGetPosition_ provides, as it is the same value that _/modules/navigation_3d/3d/godot_navigation_server_3d.h_ returns. And as is clear from _/modules/navigation_3d/3d/godot_navigation_server_3d.cpp_ and _/modules/navigation_3d/3d/nav_map_3d.cpp_, this value is only updated from the setters (and never from the rvo simulation). This would be fine if the calculation of the 3rd party library did not move the objects. Since the position between these classes gets desynced, it can produce odd results. The only way to really see the RVO position is to do what I did in the video and "go around" the agents. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1548ce29-2a62-4440-b00f-ebde28891b65 Of course, It is possible to work around this, for example, I could set the position of the agent at every physics update, but that does not really solve the issue, merely hides it. title: Fix different behavior between debug and release exports for invalid `await` on runtime null
body: - Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/105153 Result **DEBUG** Build: **RELEASE** Build: <details> <summary>GDScript code example</summary> </details> It's issue not about `await`. GDScript virtual machine behaves differently in `debug` and `release` exports. `Debug` stops code execution after error, but `release` sometimes doesn't check errors (and not stop). Solution is add checks for operations and terminate execution if an error occurs for `release` export too. However, `release` export don't generate `SCRIPT ERROR`. Although, in my opinion, it would be nice to see `SCRIPT ERROR` in `release` export too. But I decided to maintain previous behavior.

title: Create script dialogue crashes if gdscript module is disabled
body: Tested versions - v4.6.stable.mono.conservatory_edition [f4cbe9b46] System information Godot v4.6.stable.mono (f4cbe9b46) - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 3 monitors - Direct3D 12 (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 Ti (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.8108) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-10900K CPU @ 3.70GHz (20 threads) - 31.85 GiB memory Issue description **This issue is not that big a deal.** It cannot occur in a stock build of the engine, which 99% of people are going to be using. And even still, 99% of the 1% that *do* have a custom fork aren't going around disabling GDScript. So ymmv. 😛 Anyway... `create_script_dialogue.cpp` sets its `default_language` property based on if it can find `"GDScript"`. When it does, it sets the `default_language` property to the proper index and also updates the `OptionButton` to select that option (see L900, L901). However, if GDScript is disabled, this check never succeeds, and so the value is left at its default of `-1` which causes a crash in `script_language.cpp` when calling `ScriptServer::get_language(int p_idx)` in the dialogue, because `p_idx` is `-1`. I was able to resolve this by modifying the create script dialogue logic to just set the default index and the selected dropdown option to `0` if `"GDScript"` is not found, just an else block on top of the existing statement shortly after L900. Not a great fix because it fails in the hypothetical scenario where no script languages are available at all. Steps to reproduce 1. Create a custom build of the engine and compile with `module_gdscript_enabled = "no"` 2. Create a new project. 3. In the FileSystem panel, right click, go to Create > Script. This will crash the engine. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

title: More fault-tolerant DLL copies on Windows
body: - Failing to copy a DLL in `open_dynamic_library` now results in loading without copying rather than failing the extension load. This will prevent hot-reload, but is better than outright failure. - Try to copy DLLs to a couple of numbered paths before giving up. - Use is_extension_reloading_enabled() to control generate_temp_files rather than is_editor_hint() - don't call set_extension_reloading_enabled(true) when run with --script parameter. Scripts often spawn processes quickly and can trigger the file locking issue. Also they're not normally interactive so enabling hot-reloading support by default is of dubious value. Fixes 107089

title: C#: Deal with invalid script in cache
body: Fixes 109711 This addresses a potential race condition when a `CSharpScript` is simultaneously being released on the .NET Finalizer thread, and accessed on the main thread. If the main thread tries to access the partially-released value first before the `CSharpScript`'s destructor removes itself from the script cache table, it retrieves an invalid reference. This resolution has the main thread pre-emptively lock and remove the invalid reference when it is found, and proceed as if it was not found in the table.

title: [TextEdit] Fix clipping of last character due to right margin rounding
body: This fixes an off-by-one clipping issue introduced in commit 1794098. Using ceil() on the right margin reduces the visible text area by up to 1 px, causing the last character of long lines to be clipped. This restores the previous behavior by using floor() for SIDE_RIGHT while keeping ceil() for SIDE_LEFT. - Fixes 110577. See also: - 114221 - https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110791 This pull request resolves the issue without affecting other behavior: it only adjusts the one extra pixel needed at the end of `TextEdit` on custom scales so the last character is visible again. Probably using floats is a proper solution (114221), but if that is not planned in the near future, this is a simple, low-risk improvement to the currently severely impacted usability.

title: Fix `reload_scene_from_path` may crash
body: Fixes 116788, also removes a bit of redundant code. - The crash is caused by accessing a dangling pointer (`selected_node`) https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/15a4311583fbaad2eee5d2ecda5437eb9fd68c63/editor/editor_node.cppL4560-L4564 - It is dangling, because `reload_scene_from_path()` removes and frees the old scene without directly calling the `set_selected` counterpart. `set_selected` is called in a lot of places, so I'm not sure exactly when it's properly updated, but `EditorSelection::_emit_change()` seems like the main driver. Anyway, it's not called at the proper time. https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/15a4311583fbaad2eee5d2ecda5437eb9fd68c63/editor/editor_node.cppL7019-L7021 - It also has a chance of not crashing, because immediately afterwards the same scene is loaded back, sometimes reusing the same memory. As a result `selected_node` may points to some valid instance, just not the intended one. <details> <summary>Backtrace</summary> The top few frames may vary because invalid memory address is involved </details> <details> <summary> Call stack when memory is reused</summary> This is captured by breakpoint on write to `SceneTreeDock::get_singleton()->get_tree_editor()->get_selected()->_instance_id` </details>

title: Godot Editor: while idling, it does so much wasteful work that it skips rendering frame and drains the battery.
body: Tested versions Several System information MacOS, iOS, but the problems are cross-platform. Issue description When the editor is idling, it is doing a lot of low-intensity work. On iPads and phones this manifests as a warm device, and a lower battery life. On the desktop, on a Mac hosting Godot with libgodot, it manifests itself as reliably missing a rendering frame when idling, because there is so much work going on there: This shows a one second interval in Godot, where Instruments recorded 21 "hitches" (meaning that there were no updates ready to give to the system compositor) The main culprits are an accumulation of small decisions over the years, and they include: * Extensive network polling from assorted sources (debugger, language server), rather than using notifications ("You have a connection", "A client disconnected") * Bug in LineEdit blinking support * Editor Selection tracking (it really wants to keep that selection in sync at every frame) * AnimationPlayer polling, even when there are no animations. Maybe it just needs company. * Export dialog refreshing its data list, certainly stresses the importance of exporting a game, as no expense will be spared in the pursuit of this noble goal. * Assorted settings are loaded during notification_process, to check if something is active, but that really should be instead a subscription to ProjectSettings changes, and a local variable. The call to EDITOR_GET in these busy loops can consume 1% of the frame processing time. A more pernicious and hidden cost is Node::can_process(), that takes a whooping 16% of time, and I am not sure it can be eliminated easily given the editors' current design. I have noticed that Godot's Editor uses an idiom where every almost every UI element is created, hooked, configured at startup and very little is left to dynamism. It is both time consuming to setup up-front (allocations, layout, font measurements for 99% of things that are never used), but it also bloats the tree. I have been on a quest to eliminate those from the iPad port little by little, to get my startup performance back, but it is generally a slog and a maintenance challenge. Too many repeated transient dialogs that are created over and over (like file pickers) that are just sitting there eating ram, resources and startup time. Generally, I do not think Godot desktop folks care much about this, as their systems are probably more plentiful than an iPad, but the 16% of CPU time on every frame burned on this hurt me right in the feels. I am going to start fixing these for Xogot, and will be sharing my patches as PRs, hoping that they will be useful for the desktop edition as well. Steps to reproduce Run Godot under instruments, using the "Hangs", "Hitches" and "Time Profiler" instruments, launch, wait and let it idle. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) No project needed. Anything will do. Additional bugs filed after further profiling: * https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116894 * https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116893 * https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116895 Not yet filed due to research pending: * SceneMultiplayer::poll -> 2.6% of the cycles (this is an empty project) * JoypadSDL::process_events() -> 4.2% of the cycles (no joypads plugged in) - specifically SDL_WaitEventTimeoutNS taking up 3.3% PRs and Updates - LineEdit: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116853 - Poll avoidance: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116866 - SceneTreeDock Polling: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116864 - Hoist another batch of expensive EDITOR_GET from the 3D editor: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116872 - Avoid process_internal in various components when a perfect alternative exists: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117024 - Prevent audio stack from churning when there is nothing to do while idling, and makes it so Godot does not prevent MacOS from sleeping: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117162

title: Fix Dictionary editor losing selection when changing types in Remote inspector
body: Fixes 116748 **Description:** In the Remote inspector, the `EditorPropertyDictionary` continuously polls and calls `update_property()` to sync with the running game state. If a user attempts to change the type of a dictionary key or value using the `EditorVariantTypePopupMenu` (the pencil icon), the asynchronous polling loop evaluates the `changing_type_index == slot.index` condition. Prior to this fix, the poll would prematurely reset `changing_type_index` to `EditorPropertyDictionaryObject::NOT_CHANGING_TYPE` and steal focus while the popup menu was still actively open. When the user finally made a selection, the callback would fire against a cleared index, throwing the error: `Tried to change the type of a dict key or value, but nothing was selected.` **The Fix:** Added a visibility state guard (`&& (!change_type || !change_type->is_visible())`) to the focus-grabbing and state-reset block at the end of `update_property()`. This prevents the polling loop from resetting the target index while the type-selection popup is actively open, safely preserving the state until the user completes their selection and the menu closes. **Testing:** - Verified on `master` that changing dictionary key/value types via the pencil icon in the Remote inspector no longer throws an error and successfully applies the newly selected type. - Verified that local inspector behavior remains unaffected and correctly grabs focus after a type change.

title: Add support for `--ignore-error-breaks` to `LocalDebugger`
body: Fixes 116822. As mentioned in 116822, the `--ignore-error-breaks` argument that was added in 77015 only did so for `RemoteDebugger` and not `LocalDebugger`, so doesn't work in the context of `--debug`. This adds it for `LocalDebugger` as well, by basically just copying this from `RemoteDebugger::debug`: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/5cba18ff3bbe5adc9b4a8d7e970084979014b5f4/core/debugger/remote_debugger.cppL404-L406

title: `--ignore-error-breaks` does nothing with `--debug`
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.7.dev [5cba18ff3bbe5adc9b4a8d7e970084979014b5f4], 4.6.1.stable, 4.5.2.stable System information Godot v4.7.dev (5cba18ff3) - Fedora Linux 43 (Forty Three) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT (RADV GFX1201) - AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D 16-Core Processor (32 threads) - 60.39 GiB memory Issue description It might be a bit cheeky of me to frame this as a bug, but it seems `--ignore-error-breaks` (as introduced in 77015) was only implemented for `RemoteDebugger` and not `LocalDebugger`, which means that when you use `--debug` you will always end up hitting a debugger break on any GDScript error, which might not be desirable if you're just hoping to hit a particular breakpoint. Not having `--ignore-error-breaks` available in `--debug` also creates problems for certain niche CI use-cases, as `--debug` is currently the only way to have GDScript warnings actually be logged as part of automated tests and whatnot. Steps to reproduce 1. Run the MRP with `/path/to/godot --debug --ignore-error-breaks --breakpoints res://main.gd:8`. 2. Note how the debugger stops at the error in `_do_something_silly` rather than at the breakpoint. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) no-local-ignore-error-breaks.zip

title: Fix Warp mouse on Tablet/Trackpad 3D Navigation Scheme
body: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5c8c19a7-3826-4cb4-b2b3-ba1866cf1e0c **(the lag in the video is not reflective of how the engine is running, it's just the windows snipping tool doing some weird cuts on the video).** This fixes 116177 where emulated 3-button navigation (e.g. Alt/Shift + trackpad drag) stopped when the pointer left the 3D viewport. Instead of relying on mouse capture (which hides the cursor), this PR adds an emulated-nav continuation path in **Node3DEditorViewport:** - tracks whether emulated navigation is active, - computes active emulated nav mode from current modifier shortcuts, - temporarily enables global input() processing while emulated nav is active, - applies nav updates from outside-viewport motion there, - disables that path when modifiers/nav mode are no longer active. Inside-viewport motion still uses the existing _sinput() flow, so behavior remains consistent and avoids double-processing. **Changed files** - node_3d_editor_plugin.h - node_3d_editor_plugin.cpp - view_3d_controller.cpp **Testing** - Emulated 3-button trackpad nav continues when crossing viewport edges. - Cursor remains visible. - Standard navigation and freelook behavior remain unchanged.

title: Fix `GridMap` editor pasting when orbiting using the "Alt" key
body: Fixes 116730. This bug happens because when orbiting with the Maya/Modo navigations styles, the code only partially cancels the operation, leaving it in an incorrect state. This PR makes so that instead of trying to cancel it, it ignores the mouse press until it's not orbiting anymore.

title: Autocompletion: Don't hide certain setters and getters
body: Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116717 Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/114832 Autocompletion filters out getters and setters to not clutter the list. However the filtering applied hides some important methods: indexed props are not easily accessible in other ways and so their getters and setter should be suggest. Also if a property is internal, we really should not hide its public setters and getters. Sanity check: Is this the right place for fixing this, it's `core/` after all? `p_methods` is only used together with `p_exclude_from_properties`. `p_exclude_from_properties` is not exposed and only used in two places: autocompletion and listing methods for some picker dialog in the editor. So yeah this is a good place to fix this. Due to the very limited audience of the parameter there really is no need for any more finegrained control as suggested in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116717issuecomment-3958407007 <sub>@wjt I added you as co-author, since half of this PR is basically your suggested patch</sub>

title: Gridmap "Mesh Cast Shadows" off, Lightmap Baking bug
body: Tested versions 4.6.1 System information Ubuntu Studio Linux 24.04 Issue description I have a Gridmap Meshlibrary. It's import settings are for Static Lightmaps, on all meshes (this is my default, confirmed after reimporting etc). I have windows, where I've set their "Mesh Cast Shadow" to "Off" This works correctly, i make a level, and shadows pass through the window as expected. Strangely though, when i click on the LightmapGI and bake lightmaps, it ignores that setting, and bakes as if the mesh were opaque. And strangely, light will continue to be ignored passing through these windows until i go back inthe gridmap and re-add one window pane fresh, then suddenly they all refresh and respect mesh not casting shadow again. Likely something related to breaking the lightmap for their mesh or something? I'm unsure Image Steps to reproduce I've tested the most basic setup like this: - Create a new scene. - Create a pair of Meshinstance3Ds, - Unwrap their UV2 for lightbaking - Set one object's geometry settings Cast Shadows = off - Export as a gridmap meshlibrary Now make a new test scene: - Add a directional light, set it to bake static - Add an object to act as a floor to catch shadow. - Add a gridmap node with your new library - Add the shapes you created, and see how one casts a shadow and one does not. - Add a LightmapGI, and bake. Observe how the non-shadow casting object, suddenly casts shadows. - In your gridmap, add that non-shadow object again, and see how suddenly the shadows work again. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) MinimalScene.zip i didn't create a whole project for it, this is just the essentially scene and meshlibrary ready for the test Image

title: Unused allocations for `MultiMesh` interpolation data
body: Tested versions Present since Godot 4.4 with the addition of Fixed Timestep Interpolation for `MultiMesh` in August 2024. See PR and commit. System information Irrelevant Issue description See `RenderMeshStorage::multimesh_allocate_data` in `servers/rendering/storage/mesh_storage.cpp`. The function sets the `MultiMeshInterpolator`'s data, before calling `_multimesh_allocate_data`. Three `PackedFloat32Array`s used for interpolation are resized: `_data_curr`, `_data_prev` and `_data_interpolated`. Each has a size of `p_instances * mmi->_stride`, which, with an instance count in the hundreds of thousands, can scale up to multiple megabytes of memory. From what I've gathered, these 3 arrays are only accessed if the `MultiMeshInterpolator` has `interpolated` set to true. Furthermore, `interpolated` can only be true if `multimesh_set_physics_interpolated` has been called previously, which overrites all 3 arrays with the current multimesh data. I believe that the last 4 lines in the `if (mmi)` block should be removed. `_data_curr`, `_data_prev` and `_data_interpolated` should not be resized in `multimesh_allocate_data`. The data they allocate doesn't seem to be used before being freed or replaced. For multimeshes that aren't interpolated, these 3 arrays will take up memory and cause reallocations but won't ever be used. Steps to reproduce None Minimal reproduction project (MRP) None
