title: Crash with obscure error messages when adding non-ascii characters to Compatibility scene shader
body: Tested versions 4.7.dev System information GLES3, Direct3D12, Windows Issue description Before anything else, I would like to mention that the process of writing and debugging scene shaders is really not great, and the rendering team would greatly benefit from better tools (Better error messages, a setting for recompiling shaders at engine startup rather than engine build time, rudimentary compile errors within the IDE). Now to the issue: When you write any non-ascii character in GLSL shader for compatibility (`drivers\gles3\shaders\scene.glsl`), you get an infuriatingly unhelpful compilation error message: `error C0000: syntax error, unexpected $end at token "<EOF>"` This even happens when the respective character is part of a comment, which does not help when you try to fix the error by commenting out lines (happened to me when I was trying to refer to an algorithm by somebody named Ureña, and I swear, I wasted hours on this). If you run the engine from the console, you get slightly more helpful errors: `Unicode parsing error, some characters were replaced with � (U+FFFD): Invalid unicode codepoint (ffffffb1), cannot represent as UTF-8`, but it still does not say the line, the file, the particular character (ffffffb1 means nothing), or that the characters should all be ASCII, rather than UTF-8. Potentially related: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/61756 Steps to reproduce Add this comment to `drivers\gles3\shaders\scene.glsl`: `// ñ` recompile and run in compatibility rendering mode. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

title: WebKit (Safari on iOS) page crash when playing audio during gameplay
body:
Tested versions Tested on iPhone 12, iOS 26.3 using official web exports from Godot 4.5.1.stable. System information iPhone 12 - iOS 26.3 - Godot 4.5.1.stable Issue description Playing a project with continiously called `AudioStreamPlayer.play()` in the code for 10-30 minutes causes a page crash in iOS Safari. Tested on iPhone 12, iOS 26.3 using web exports from Godot 4.5.1.stable. This was present in some form in Godot 4.3 and seems at least partially related to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/107390 and https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/107948 (which are fixed and present in 4.5.1). As part of the fixes above, sample playback was renitroduced in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/91382. While playing with the default value of `audio/general/default_playback_type.web == 1` (Sample) the game crashed for me. But switching to Stream avoids the crash entirely. I haven't noticed any severe audio distrotions either, but it might be due to the audio simplicity in our project (as well as MRP). While profiling my iPhone using a Virtual Machine running MacOS Sequoia, I noticed something that appears to be a memory leak in the `Timelines -> Memory` tab, buuut it doesn't seem definitive: I've had some tests (with Streams) that climbed to the same amount of max memory, got garbage collected and didn't crash. Is it possible, that since mixing is happening on-device (rather than in-Godot), there is some sort of internal leak caused by sending too many samples to the device? Steps to reproduce 1. Unarchive and launch the `audio_safari_page_crash_mrp.zip` project in Godot 4.5.1 play mode. 2. Toggle Debug Monitors for objects, resources, memory. 3. Click `x2` button until the counter at the bottom shows `1024.00` (this is the current value of `Engine.time_scale`). 4. Wait for 100-200 seconds. 5. Observe no apparent memory leak in Debug Monitors.
1. Set up a remote server (dedicated or VPS). (I was able to more reliably reproduce this issue on on actual remote server, possibly due to CORS thing of some sort). 2. Upload and unarchieve both `web-sample.zip` and `web-stream.zip` somewhere on the server. 3. In `./web-sample` run `python serve.py` (requires Python 3.11+ and `pip install cryptography`). Take note of the port (8060 by default). 4. Connect to the served page via iPhone's Safari. 5. Click `x2` button until the counter at the bottom shows `1024.00`. 6. Wait for 100-200 seconds. 7. Observe a page crash with briefly displayed error `This web page was reloaded because a problem occured`. 8. Repeat steps 3-6 using the `./web-stream` directory. 9. Observe no crash. Note: the only difference `web-sample.zip` and `web-stream.zip` have is being exported with corresponding `ProjectSettings` value for `audio/general/default_playback_type.web`. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) audio_safari_page_crash_mrp.zip web-sample.zip web-stream.zip

title: Fix drop preview not working when dragging tabs between windows
body: Show tab drop preview across native windows during tab drag Fixes 116577 When dragging a TabContainer/TabBar tab between non-embedded windows, the target TabBar could fail to show the drop marker because preview activation depended on local mouse motion input only. By updating TabBar drag preview state on drag and hover lifecycle notifications (NOTIFICATION_DRAG_BEGIN, NOTIFICATION_MOUSE_ENTER, NOTIFICATION_MOUSE_EXIT), this change ensures the drop indicator appears reliably for cross-window tab drags.

title: Visual shader Texture2DParameter default Type "Data" results in washed out colors in Mobile/Forward+
body: Tested versions 4.6.1.stable System information Godot v4.6.1.stable - macOS Sequoia (15.7.4) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Metal (Mobile) - integrated Apple M2 Pro (Apple8) - Apple M2 Pro (10 threads) - 16.00 GiB memory Issue description In the visual shader editor when adding a Texture2DParameter node this has the default Type set to "Data". Using this as is in a sampler to set a shader albedo color works as expected in Compatibility renderer (see below). Image However, if we change to Mobile/Forward+ renderer, this results in very washed out colors: Image I couldn't find any documentation anywhere what this "Type" is supposed to be for, but the issue appears to be the "Data" type setting. Changing this to "Color" instead makes the texture colors correct again on Mobile/Forward+ as well (also works on Compatibility). Image I'm not sure what the Data type is supposed to do, or if this is the expected behavior of it, but at the very least it introduces some hard to figure out lighting/color issues that could easily be avoided by just making Color the default type instead for new Texture2DParameter nodes. And if the issue is caused by this Data type not being supported in either Compatibility or Mobile/Forward+ perhaps a warning should be issued to help users understand why the shader now produces a very different result after switching renderer. Steps to reproduce - Create a new Compatibility mode project - Make a simple 3d scene with a camera, a light and a mesh instance to apply our material to. - Create a new ShaderMaterial and a Visual Shader for this shader material - In the visual shader add a Texture2D sampler connected to the fragment albedo color - In the visual shader add a Texture2DParameter and connect this to the Texture2D sampler (observe that it is default Type:Data) - On the ShaderMaterial resource assign the default icon as the Texture2DParameter texture - Observe the camper preview to see the mesh with the assigned material shows the icon texture as expected. - Switch the project over to Mobile renderer - Observer the material now renders a lot lighter. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) shader_texture.zip

title: macOS: Crash of embedded game also crashed the editor
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: Custom fork of 4.6.stable, with a few modifications System information MacBook Pro M1 Max, macOS 15.5 Issue description This issue description will be a bit vague, as I am not able to reproduce this myself, but it seems likely there is (or was?) some issue with the game embedding on macOS, as we had this editor crash reported to us, which happened right as the embedded game itself crashed: Note that this is from a custom fork of 4.6-stable with a few changes here and there, so the line numbers might not perfectly match `master`, but as far as I know none of the more relevant classes here have had any changes done to them. Judging by the call stack and the `KERN_INVALID_ADDRESS at 0x00000000000009d0`, it looks like it's crashing here in `LayerHost::gui_input`, due to `script_debugger` being `nullptr`: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/15a4311583fbaad2eee5d2ecda5437eb9fd68c63/platform/macos/editor/embedded_process_macos.mmL348-L352 My best guess is that `EmbeddedProcessMacOS::set_script_debugger` was called with `nullptr` from `GameView::_detach_script_debugger` after the game process crashed, and at the next flushing of input events it ends up erroneously making its way to this `send_message` call in `LayerHost::gui_input`, but I've tried to simulate this by pushing input events every frame (in both a game and tool context) and manually killing the game process, but it seems to handle it gracefully every time. Steps to reproduce None yet, unfortunately. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) None yet, unfortunately.

title: Fix Reinhard and AgX tonemapper in Mobile renderer when using subpass.
body: - Fixes 116643 When 110077 was merged, 94496 needed to be rebased. During that rebase, one function where the `output_max_value` needed to be set was missed. This caused Reinhard and AgX tonemappers to break with the mobile renderer when using that subpass function.

title: `AudioStreamWAV`: Check for `eof_reached` when reading LIST INFO tags
body: Fixes 116099. Supersedes 116112 which touches code beyond the issue. By analyzing the file, it seems to have a `LIST` chunk that not only is placed after `data` (which is usually the last), but also informs a size of 3,225,026,560 bytes, which is far beyond the end of the file. This is an unusual case, but it can be solved by simply adding an `eof_reached` check that breaks the `INFO` loop (which honestly, should have been there long ago). It will print an error in the editor, but it's reasonable for a file with invalid chunks.

title: Remove unnecessary mutex locks in RD & fix a few race conditions.
body: Removes unnecessary mutex locks from functions where they are not required. I figure most of these are remnants from when `RID_Owner` was not thread safe. I also fixed a few race conditions that I noticed: - Draw list can read framebuffer formats map while it is getting written in a different thread. I don't practically see this happening since framebuffers always get created on the render thread, but it is technically allowed. - Pipeline creation and draw list can read vertex formats map while it is getting written in a different thread. This realistically CAN happen. Eventually I'd like to completely get rid of `_THREAD_SAFE_METHOD_` and use multiple mutexes. This would allow `vertex_array_create`/`index_array_create`/`uniform_set_create` functions to run in parallel and not throttle unrelated rendering functions. However, it is much more complicated than it sounds due to how draw trackers are managed.

title: Wayland protocol error due to buffer size not divisible by 2
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.6.1 and a3e84cc2af14aa4cffbefd8e13492e59567a64e3 (master at time of writing) - May be reproducible in older versions System information Godot v4.7.dev (a3e84cc2a) - Arch Linux 1 ZEN SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Mon, 09 Feb 2026 17:45:06 +0000 on Wayland - Wayland display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - AMD Radeon 890M Graphics (radeonsi, gfx1150, LLVM 21.1.6, DRM 3.64, 6.18.9-zen1-2-zen) - AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 w/ Radeon 890M (24 threads) - 46.33 GiB memory Issue description Wayland buffers must have a size divisible by their scale. The constraint is being violated intermittently on Sway using a scale=2 output. It appears to be a race condition, where the scale is updated after the correct size has been enqueued (`WindowRectMessage`) but before that message is processed. in that gap the new scale is committed along with an incorrectly sized buffer and Godot crashes with a Wayland protocol error. This commit seems to fix my issue by always using the fractional scaling code path when the viewporter protocol is available. However this doesn't solve the underlying issue, so it could still be hit with compositors that don't support the protocol. Also it's claudeslop, so it could be wrong in other subtle ways. Happy to open a PR with it, or to human-write a better solution with guidance on how to go about that (I'm experienced with Wayland, less so with Godot's windowing code and thread safety model). Here are logs with `WAYLAND_DEBUG=1` and print statements added to `WaylandThread::window_state_update_size()`, `WaylandThread::_frame_wl_callback_on_done()` and `DisplayServerWayland::_update_window_rect()`: Steps to reproduce Use Sway 1.11 with a display scale of 2.0 1. Open project selector 2. Click create project 3. Click browse 4. Esc to exit popup 5. Repeat until crash (usually first try) 6. If no crash, restart Godot and try again Alternative: 1. Open project 2. Open project settings 3. Repeat until crash (usually first try) These may depend on screen size or some other variable Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

In skeleton_3d_editor_plugin.cpp, drag-and-drop is implemented only for the bone tree UI (joint_tree), not for scene nodes in general. While there is some validation in place, there are two scenarios that do not seem to be covered and could explain the crash: 1. p_data["type"] is not validated 2. that the dragged source is a valid bone type. Given that Godot has type annotations for the DnD sources, rather than the ad-hoc validation done with the metadata containing `bones/`, wouldn't it be better to use a dedicated DnD type, instead of `nodes`? title: GDScript: Fix async call without await check
body: Fixes 95210 The runtime check for an async function called without `await` was done by assuming all calls that return a `GDScriptFunctionState` instance were invalid (when the return value is actually used and not validated, i. e. `call-ret`). This assumption doesn't always hold, as there are valid ways of accessing such instances, for example a `get_object()` call on a `Callable` from a `Signal` connection that has a function awaiting it. To fix this, I added a new `bool` field on the `GDScriptFunctionState` class (present only in debug builds), to mark if each instance was properly "handled", and only throwing the runtime error when the returned instance doesn't have this flag set.

title: Fix re-importing scene file breaking refs in an `Array[Node]`
body: Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116654 Most probably a similar fix would need to be applied for `Dictionary`s, but I didn't want to implement before this solution is accepted code-wise. Essentially the issue is that `EditorNode::get_modified_properties_reference_to_nodes` only stores properties that directly reference `Node`, which `Array` and `Dictionary` of course do it "indirectly". My solution is to transform the `Array[Node]` into `Array[NodePath]`, store it, and then transform it back into `Array[Node]` in `EditorNode::update_node_from_node_modification_entry`.

title: "When a coroutine is deleted, the destructor of local variables (class instances) is not called."
body: Tested versions Test version godot 4.2 - 4.61 maybe 4.X all version System information windows11 , CPU AMD Ryzen 7900 , RAM 48GB, 4060ti 16GB, dx12(vulcan) Issue description I created a manager that handles creating and deleting coroutines. However, when a coroutine is deleted while it is awaiting, the destructor of its local class instance variables is not called. The destructor is only invoked if the coroutine itself explicitly returns from within its function. Intuitively, deleting a coroutine should also clean up the local variables used inside it, and their destructors should be called. This behavior was natural when I coded with Lua scripts. But in GDScript, unless the coroutine terminates itself, the destructors of local variables are not triggered. I worked around this by using an if await return approach, but the more intuitive behavior would be that deleting a coroutine automatically cleans up its local variables and calls their destructors. Even without my custom coroutine manager, it seems that in GDScript coroutines can be deleted at any time. So why does this bug exist? Steps to reproduce ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- example: Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Test_Util.zip

title: Fix clipboard history not updating on subsequent copies in Wayland
body: <!-- Please target the `master` branch in priority. Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers. To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html --> Fixes 116522 When setting the clipboard, the data source was reused after the first copy and only the in-memory buffer was updated, so the compositor was never notified of subsequent clipboard changes. The change makes it destroy and recreate the `wl_data_source` when setting the clipboard selection, so that `wl_data_device_set_selection` is called each time. Clipboard managers (e.g. KDE Klipper) detect new entries by receiving a new selection event from the compositor, which only fires when set_selection is called with a new data source. This PR also applies the same fix to the primary clipboard.

title: iOS: Propagate VC UI preferences to SwiftUI hosting controller
body: Fixes 116632 Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115552 The SwiftUI lifecycle migration (109974) wraps `GDTViewController` inside a `UIViewControllerRepresentable`. The hosting controller becomes the window's root view controller, but it doesn't delegate `preferredScreenEdgesDeferringSystemGestures`, `prefersHomeIndicatorAutoHidden`, or `prefersStatusBarHidden` to child VCs. Since iOS queries the root VC for these, the corresponding Godot project settings (`suppress_ui_gesture`, `hide_home_indicator`, `hide_status_bar`) are silently ignored. This adds `propagateUIPreferencesToRootViewController` which copies `GDTViewController`'s existing method implementations onto the hosting controller's class using `class_addMethod` / `method_setImplementation`, called from `viewDidAppear:` when the window hierarchy is established. Guarded with `ifdef IOS_ENABLED` since visionOS doesn't use these APIs. <!-- Please target the `master` branch in priority. Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers. To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html -->

title: iOS: suppress_ui_gesture, hide_status_bar, and hide_home_indicator project settings have no effect since SwiftUI lifecycle migration
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.6.1-stable - Not reproducible in: 4.4.1-stable System information iOS 26.2.1, iPhone 16 Pro Issue description The project settings `display/window/ios/suppress_ui_gesture`, `display/window/ios/hide_status_bar`, and `display/window/ios/hide_home_indicator` are silently ignored on iOS. The SwiftUI lifecycle migration (109974) wraps `GDTViewController` inside a `UIViewControllerRepresentable`. The hosting controller becomes the window's root view controller, but it doesn't delegate `preferredScreenEdgesDeferringSystemGestures`, `prefersHomeIndicatorAutoHidden`, or `prefersStatusBarHidden` to child VCs. Since iOS queries the root VC for these, the Godot project settings have no effect. Steps to reproduce 1. Enable `display/window/ios/suppress_ui_gesture` in Project Settings (and ensure `hide_home_indicator` is disabled — the two interfere with each other due to an Apple API conflict, see 104411) 2. Export to iOS and run on a physical device 3. Swipe up from the bottom edge — the app backgrounds immediately instead of requiring two swipes Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Any project with `suppress_ui_gesture` enabled, exported to iOS with 4.6.1.

title: GDScript Not Producing Compile Error On Instantiating Godot-Cpp Defined Abstract Class
body: Tested versions v4.6.1.stable.arch_linux [14d19694e] System information Godot v4.6.1.stable (14d19694e) - Arch Linux Issue description When a C++ declared abstract class is defined in code, and registered to Godot, GDScript does not produce an error when trying to instantiate the class. This does not match the behavior when a GDScript class is defined as abstract, which correctly produces an error. The editor should notify the user, exactly like the behavior from GDScript defined abstract classes. This is important especially if C++ GDExtension assets are shared with other users unfamiliar with a codebase and its nuances. When the scene is played, it will produce an error trying to instantiate an object from a nullptr constructor function. The value of `ClassDB.can_instantiate("MyAbstractClass")` returns false, implying ClassDB has the information to produce an error when a user tries to instantiate the class. Error Log from IDE: Error log from GDScript editor: Instantiating a C++ abstract class and a GDScript abstract class. The C++ class is correctly registered with Godot. The godot-cpp function that appears responsible for correctly defining the creation function nullptr if it is abstract. Steps to reproduce 1) Fresh install of Godot from Arch pacman `sudo pacman -Syu godot` 2) Follow the example of setting up godot-cpp GDExtension (godot-cpp version used is 4.5) 3) Create the C++ abstract class and register it with Godot C++ Abstract Class: C++ Class Registration: 4) Create a GDScript script and attach it to the scene GDScript Function: 5) An error for the GDScript defined abstract class will be produced but there will be no error for the C++ version Minimal reproduction project (MRP) gh_issue.zip src_gh_issue.zip

title: `ResourceSaver::set_uid` does not check path is recognized
body: Tested versions Godot 4.7, 4.6, and 4.6.1 System information Fedora 42 Issue description Methods in both `ResourceSaver` and `ResourceLoader` validate that, when iterating the savers or loaders, respectively, the implementation is called by checking whether it either handles the `Resource` or recognizes the path. However, `ResourceSaver::set_uid` does not perform these checks and instead delegates this detail to the implementation, which is out of alignment with the other methods. Because this isn't documented, it can lead to errors or editor crashes. `ResoureSaver::set_uid` should behave like its sibling methods, and validate the path is recognized before calling the saver implementation. I realize this is currently an API limitation because the `recognize_path` method takes both a resource and path; however, perhaps it would be advantageous to introduce a new API to align the behavior expectations. The `ResourceLoader` implementation has such methods, at least. Steps to reproduce n/a Minimal reproduction project (MRP) n/a

title: Android: Virtual keyboard height is not updated correctly when the keyboard resizes
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in Godot v4.6.stable.official [89cea1439] System information Android 12 Issue description When a virtual keyboard is displayed, `DisplayServer.virtual_keyboard_get_height()` correctly returns the initial size of the keyboard. However, if the keyboard size changes while it is already open (e.g. when the emoji menu is toggled), the method continues to return the old height value. The updated height is only reflected after the keyboard is closed and reopened. **Investigation** I traced the bug to the JNI function `Java_org_godotengine_godot_GodotLib_setVirtualKeyboardHeight` in `platform/android/java_godot_lib_jni.cpp`. This led me to the Kotlin implementation in `platform/android/java/lib/src/main/java/org/godotengine/godot/Godot.kt`, specifically the call to `GodotLib.setVirtualKeyboardHeight(finalHeight)`. **My fix** I am not an Android/Kotlin developer, but I consulted an AI tool for a potential solution. It suggested adding the following: In the `ViewCompat.setOnApplyWindowInsetsListener(rootView)` This worked for me, but I don't trust AI generated slop and belive there is a better solution. Steps to reproduce 1. Create a scene with a `LineEdit` control and a `Label` control. 2. Write a script to assigned the value of `DisplayServer.virtual_keyboard_get_height()` to the `Label` text (e.g., inside `_process` or via a timer). 3. Export and install the project on an **Android** device. 4. Tap the `LineEdit` to open the virtual keyboard and note the height shown on the label. 5. Change the keyboard size while it is still open (e.g., by opening the emoji menu). 6. **Observation**: Notice that the `Label` text does not update to reflect the new height. 7. Close the keyboard and reopen it. Observe that the height now reflects the emoji menu size rather than the standard keyboard size. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Virtual keyboard bug repo

title: `_GuiInput` can still be called after a `Control` has been `QueueFree`d
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in `v4.4.1.stable.mono.official [49a5bc7b6]` - Reproducible in `v4.6.1.stable.mono.official [14d19694e]` System information macOS Sequoia 15.6.1 Issue description I don't know for sure that this is a bug, but it was unexpected when I encountered it (and it took a _long_ time for me to encounter it in my game 😳). After a `Control` has been `QueueFree`'d, its `_GuiInput` can still trigger. I would expect the engine to check `IsQueuedForDeletion` rather than requiring my code to do that (for anyone finding this from an internet search, the trivially easy workaround is indeed to check `IsQueuedForDeletion` before acting in `_GuiInput`). The tricky part is that the `Free` doesn't happen until the end of the current frame, so if your framerate is low (e.g. it's set to 1 FPS in the attached `.zip`) or if you can click really fast, then this is relatively easy to trigger. I think the reason this took so long for me to discover is because I test with a mouse on a 60-FPS device; it wasn't until someone tested on a Legion Go with a touch screen that this happened. I assume it's because it's easy to accidentally produce too many touch events with your finger. Steps to reproduce From a `Control`: Minimal reproduction project (MRP) GuiInputWhenQueueFreedRepro.zip

title: `ResourceLoader::load` behaves inconsistent for builtin resources
body: Tested versions v4.7.dev.custom_build [c4a893e98] v4.6.1.stable.flathub [14d19694e] System information Godot v4.7.dev (c4a893e98) - Fedora Linux 43 (Workstation Edition) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor Issue description Packed scenes can have builtin resources. Those have a well defined path e.g. `res://scene.tscn::CanvasItemMaterial_drvgu`. `ResourceLoader::load` loads resources by a given path however when given such paths the method behaves inconsistent, depending on the current state of the cache. `ResourceLoader::load("res://scene.tscn::CanvasItemMaterial_drvgu")` works and returns the correct resource if the outer scene is currently loaded somewhere (so it is present in the cache). When calling `ResourceLoader::load("res://scene.tscn::CanvasItemMaterial_drvgu")` without prior loading of `scene.tscn` it will fail. This is bad, because users are not supposed to care about the resource cache when writing normal code. The presence of something in the cache can make users believe that their code is working correctly, but under certain edge cases it might fail due to something getting unreferenced. I'd expect to either get a consistent descriptive error. Or preferably to be able to "cold load" builtin resources. Steps to reproduce - Open the MRP - Run the editor script - modify whether to first load the scene - see how it influences the result of the method Minimal reproduction project (MRP) load_builtin.zip

title: Fix symbol jump history navigation issue.
body: - Fix 92420 - Related 97031 - Related 108057 This PR aims to make the history navigation behave like it does in VS Code/Visual Studio. There are several main issues in the original code: 1. Each time a script is opened (jump) from other script, its old state will be saved to history, which is unnecessary. 2. title: Robust `safe_normalize` routine (32 bit)
body: This function guarantees unit length output in 32 bit and 64 bit real_t builds down to a minimum epsilon, and returns the length of the input vector, or 0 on failure. Just `Vector2` for now, to make bikeshedding / changes easier, although similar may be applicable in `Vector3` and other classes. Supersedes 74912 Helps address 74852 TLDR This version of `safe_normalize` uses a trick boost to the input vector before normalization in order to guarantee a unit length result. It should be faster than an alternative approach of conversion to `double` for the normalization (which is done in alternative PR 116666). Which one to go for might possibly be different in Godot 3.x and 4.x ... possibly doubles in 4.x and 32 bit approach in 3.x (partly because of support for 32 bit mobile which may emulate 64 bit math / or be slow at it). Explanation Since I first used Godot I noticed that the normalize functions checked against zero, rather than an epsilon. All previous engines I had encountered and written used an epsilon here. I filed it under something to investigate later in my _memory palace_ :grin: . | Engine | Epsilon by default | Epsilon | Returns if unsafe | | ----- | ----- | ----- | ---- | | Godot | no | 0 | zero | | Bevy | no | 0 | zero / panic | | Unity | yes | 1e-5f | zero | | Unreal | yes (user definable) | 1e-8f (squared) | zero or unchanged | | CryEngine | yes | unknown |unit vector | | Gamebryo | yes | unknown | zero | While having a special case for zero is mathematically correct, in practice we are using floating point, and the behaviour on normalization of small vectors breaks down, resulting in non-unit vector output, and possibly overflow (or other unpredictable behaviour depending on CPU and rounding mode). In Godot this can exhibit as failing later math checks for `is_normalized()` (which itself is very conservative in non-precise mode). But non-unit length results can also lead to subtle bugs throughout geometry processing. This can be acceptable in some applications (e.g. normalize on GPU) but is probably not a good trade off in others (especially non-bottleneck code). This led to some investigation as this is an interesting problem. How would you choose an epsilon? * Something that reliably produces a "reasonable" unit vector output (that passes `is_normalized()` in our case) * The epsilon can (in theory) be lower in 64 bit than in 32 bit (to produce the same accuracy in result) Most engines just use 32 bit, however we have the choice of builds. However I would personally go with using the same constants in 32 bit and 64 bit, i.e. tuning for the lowest common denominator, as changing these can lead to subtle and hard to track bugs between the builds (this is the approach Godot generally takes afaik). **Note that the epsilons chosen are designed to pass `is_normalized()` with the more stringent precise math checks, rather than the rather looser defaults. If we only need to pass defaults, the epsilons could likely be relaxed a little.** Can we do better? Inspired by our particular problem (passing math checks) we realize we can **do better** and increase the unit length accuracy of the result at low inputs (at low performance cost). Instead of giving up and returning zero at the point where the result length would deviate significantly from 1.0, we can extend the range quite a bit by adding a second branch to "rescue" the result and recalibrate it to be closer to length 1.0. We evaluated some rescue approaches: 1) Apply a fixed multiplication "boost" to small vectors before normalization, then remove this boost from returned lengths (see 74912) 2) Essentially apply normalization "twice" (the approach in this PR) 3) Convert to `double` in the rescue branch, then convert back to `float` for the result Currently the (2) and (3) are winning on advice from Grok, as the accuracy gained seems to outweigh any small performance costs that occur when this branch is taken. (3) seems to offer even greater returned length accuracy (more than required?) but is likely slower due to 32-64 bit conversions, and slower SIMD with 64 bit. The choice between (2) and (3) may also depend on CPU with (2) being likely more friendly on lower mobile hardware (maybe 3.x versus 4.x? not sure). Of course this only matters when the rescue branch is taken. I'm happy to do a version with double for comparison, or swap this PR to doubles if we are confident it will be better. For reference, double rescue branch for `Vector2` would be something like this: _(and probably omitting this and just changing the threshold to the lower one for the 64 bit Godot builds)_ Some other more complex approaches Grok suggested include: * high-precision accumulation (kahan / kilisch style) * posit numbers / tapered precision formats * fast approx + Newton refinement (1-2 iterations) Although these may be straying into overkill for general purpose game engine. Should we apply the rescue branch at all? This is debatable. The obvious argument against would be that input vectors small enough to not produce reliable unit vector output are not accurate enough to give a decent direction. This can be shown to be false however. The minimum threshold lengths involved (before squaring) are actually _quite large_, numerically speaking. We are talking lengths of around 0.002. For comparison, `FLT_MIN` (32 bit) is 1.175494351e-38F, way way smaller. It's clear that a direction can be encoded quite accurately for quite a way below the threshold chosen for a unit length result, even taking into account multiple operations and numerical degradation. So I would argue that, especially in our case (where math checks are used), we should apply the second rescue branch. There don't seem to be any obvious downsides, especially given the current situation in the codebase (they will currently work, but produce output that varies significantly from unit length). That is, we are likely to see _more_ regressions if we don't use a rescue branch (although see the later note on the distinction between _regressions_ and _misuse_). That's the version I've gone with here. It's fairly easy to remove / ifdef out the rescue branch. How to apply If we decide to add a new safe function, the question arises, should we apply this technique for the existing bound normalization functions, or should we have engine / games explicitly call a "safe_normalize"? Keeping the existing normalization functions the same has both pros and cons: * pro - no risk of regressions * cons - bugs (possibly not yet identified) are not automatically fixed Given that the majority of other engines use an epsilon, I would tend in the long term towards making the change and using the new approach by default. I suspect that logic will show that any _regressions_ that show up as a result are actually **unidentified bugs**. i.e. any code that is currently relying on near zero length normalization is currently bugged and unsafe. However in the interests of getting things moving, I'm just going with the safer approach of just adding the new function for now and not changing any existing normalization routines. Why does `safe_normalize` return length at all? Length is calculated for free in normalization (in all cases except one extra divide in the rescue branch). The return value should get optimized out where unused. In fact the divide would likely be optimized out in many cases (especially if we placed this in the header after finalization). In many cases the length of a vector is useful information as well as needing to normalize, so this avoids an extra step. An example in AI is calculating the direction and distance from a player to a waypoint or another player. Another example is `Plane` normalization. It's actually just _coincidence_ that I was working on improving the normalize at the same time as adding a version that returns length, it made sense to combine these two problems. ToDo - [x] Grok numerical tests - indicates it should pass `is_normalized()` in all cases except failure - [ ] independent numerical testing - [ ] other classes (possibly follow up PR) Changes This is a result of iterating on 74912 with grok. As such some testing with numerical precision is needed, in case of hallucinations (in my earlier PRs I did this testing and found 32 bit SSE pretty accurate to pretty small lengths, however Grok seems to err on the side of caution with epsilons). Notes * On suggestion from Grok, I've gone for one of the other options, "double normalization", on the rescue branch rather than the multiplication boost we discussed when brainstorming. It seems like the accuracy increase from this option can be significant, and the cost is relatively low versus multiplication boost. * I've forgone the bool, as in most cases just returning the length directly can be used to test failure almost as efficiently as bool. * Epsilons are custom chosen (stored in mathdefs) and tuned for 32 bit. <!-- Please target the `master` branch in priority. Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers. To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html -->

title: Fix game speed UI not resetting when game is restarted (from editor)
body: `_reset_time_scales()` has a visibility guard that was originally needed to prevent resets on session start. After 4e06e305bf2311abb1818085f0022b1e65eda442 scoped the call to only run when all sessions end, the guard is unnecessary and prevents the speed state from being properly reset. Fixes 115400. Previously, if you started a game from the editor, set the speed to 2x or something in the pop-up game window toolbar, then closed out using the normal x in the title, and then restart, the UI would still show 2x, but the game itself would still be running at 1x. A minor annoyance. You could still just click 2x again and get the expected behavior. Now, with this fix, both the actual speed and the displayed icon match the 1x expectation on launch. I'm not familiar enough with the codebase to know if this alone is a sufficient test case. Fixed: Screencast from 2026-02-20 19-32-22.webm Tested on Ubuntu + Godot 4.6.1 (and tip of master), Cversion. I believe it was also happening in 4.6.0. <!-- Please target the `master` branch in priority. Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers. To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html --> --- _Disclaimer: this problem was identified and solution verified by me, but the root cause analysis and implementation was performed by Claude Code w/ Opus 4.6._

title: "Allow hiDPI" setting ignored in Game View / embedded game window on macOS
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in 4.6.1 stable - Reproducible in 4.7 dev 1 - Likely reproducible in every version that has the embedded game window System information Godot v4.6.1.stable - macOS Sequoia (15.7.4) - Multi-window, 3 monitors - Metal (Forward+) - integrated Apple M3 Max (Apple9) - Apple M3 Max (16 threads) - 64.00 GiB memory Issue description When using the embedded Game view (either the floating window, or the docked game view tab in the Godot editor), incorrect scaling is applied to the game. I suspect this is related to this issue: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116120 But this report is specifically about the fact that the 'Allow hiDPI' setting specifically is totally ignored by the embedded game window / view. It appears to always behave as if the setting is turned on - the rendered view appears half the size it should be, and everything is scaled down accordingly. Steps to reproduce 1. Create a default project in any affected Godot version. 2. Enter Project Settings > Display > Window, and turn **OFF** the "Allow hiDPI" setting. 3. Create a new scene with a Control node as the root. 4. Add a GraphEdit node, Label node, or any other Control-derived node with which the scaling issue can be seen. 5. Save the scene, and Run Project using this new scene as the startup scene (or "Run Current Scene"). 6. Observe the rendered game is much smaller than it appears in the editor, and any nodes with text / other graphics are drawing at half the expected size. 7. Optionally, turn "Allow hiDPI" back **ON**, and try running the game embedded again - the size and render scale are unchanged. 8. If the "Embed Game on Next Play" setting is disabled, and the project is run again, the 'Allow hiDPI' setting being on or off will affect the size of that un-embedded window. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

title: Another resource is loaded from path (possible cyclic resource inclusion).
body: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bfdbe2ec-fffe-4b50-b2fe-49519a6bfa6b https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/573ee3a6-cb37-405f-acdf-6be6d5efbc78 https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5cb40d91-a5dc-4aaa-a55e-331dfbc695d3 Tested versions Was able to reproduce on 4.6.1, 4.6, 4.5, and 4.5.beta1. Not observed in 4.5.dev5 or lower (yet.) Seems more prevalent on recent versions. System information Godot v4.6.stable - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Ti (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.9186) - AMD Ryzen 9 7950X 16-Core Processor (32 threads) - 63.14 GiB memory Issue description Godot produces: ERROR: Another resource is loaded from path 'res://main.tscn::h2yge' (possible cyclic resource inclusion). In a simple scene with one exported Curve resource. I've tried a couple other resource types such as JSON and was able to replicate the error with little effort. So it does not seem unique to only the Curve resource. I first observed the error with a custom resource type. Was able to reproduce on 4.6.1, 4.6, 4.5, and 4.5.beta1. Not observed in 4.5.dev5 or lower (yet.) Seems more prevalent on recent versions. Average save / load attempts required to observe error: Godot 4.7.dev1 -- 7 save/load attempts Godot 4.6.1.stable -- 7 save/load attempts Godot 4.6.stable -- 7 save/load attempts Godot 4.5.stable -- 14 save/load attempts Godot 4.5.beta1 -- 14 save/load attempts; also sometimes get error: ERROR: Task 'generate_thumbnails' already exists. Godot 4.5.dev5 -- N/A (tried 20+ save/load attempts and did not see any errors.) Godot 4.5.dev4 -- N/A (tried 20+ save/load attempts and did not see any errors.) Godot 4.5.dev1 -- N/A (tried 20+ save/load attempts and did not see any errors.) Godot 4.4.1stable -- N/A (tried 20+ save/load attempts and did not see any errors.) Godot 4.4.stable -- N/A (tried 20+ save/load attempts and did not see any errors.) Godot 4.3.stable -- N/A (tried 20+ save/load attempts and did not see any errors.) Steps to reproduce Open the example project in engine of choice (4.6, 4.5, 4.4.) Repeatedly create and remove the exported resource, saving the scene intermittently. (I am not sure if the bug will occur without saving the scene.) So far I've only seen this triggered by manually using the mouse to clear and add resource. Using Undo/Redo commands has not triggered it for me. Check console output logs -- sometimes the error is difficult to reproduce. After anywhere from 5 to 20 tries you should see the error. Once the error occurs once, it is more likely to repeat until the resource is cleared, the scene is saved, and the project/scene is reloaded. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) cyclic-resource-error.zip

body: Initial batch of cherry-picks for 4.6.2, focused on lingering regressions and simple bugfixes

title: Fix undoing `GridMap` move operations resulting in deleted cells
body: When moving cells to a different location, the editor stores the position of the currently present cells so they can be re-added if the operation is undone. The problem comes when that location overlaps with the original location of the moved cells, because when fetching the present cells, it found nothing, because they were removed to be shown in the cell preview. This PR makes so that if no cell was found, it will go through the clipboard to see if any cell there matches the location, and used that instead for the undo operation.

title: Crash when closing detached Script Editor window instead of re-attaching to main editor

title: Fix incorrect index usage in path string construction in `PostImportPluginSkeletonRestFixer`

title: OpenXR: Fix OpenGL ES driver checks
body: The `OpenXROpenGLExtension` class handles both OpenGL and OpenGL ES but currently there is no code path to activate the extension when the "opengl3_es" rendering driver is requested explicitly. A similar issue exists in `OpenXRFBUpdateSwapchainExtension`. With this commit both strings are checked.

title: Input: Fix action matching for projects saved before device ID change
body: Summary Some existing projects are broken with 116274 due to them serialising events with device_id = 0. An example would be the Global Illumination project. Solution Normalize legacy device=0 on keyboard/mouse events when loading input actions from project settings. Before 116274, these events defaulted to device=0; now they use DEVICE_ID_KEYBOARD (16) and DEVICE_ID_MOUSE (32), causing _find_event() mismatches. <!-- Please target the `master` branch in priority. Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers. To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html -->

title: Clipboard history only logs the first copy attempt from Godot in Wayland
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in 4.6 and 833889aec024b792b1c5ce5089eaebc5eb45692f System information Godot v4.6.stable unknown - Arch Linux 1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Mon, 09 Feb 2026 17:16:33 +0000 on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - KDE Plasma Version: 6.5.5, KDE Frameworks Version: 6.23.0, Qt Version: 6.10.2, Graphics Platform: Wayland Issue description When using native Wayland with KDE (Klipper as clipboard manager), copying text from the editor or in-game works for the first attempt; however, subsequent copies are not logged in the clipboard history until you copy text from another application and then copy text in Godot again. Example scenario : 1. Copy text "A" inside the Godot editor 2. Check the clipboard history, initially showing "A" in the list 3. Copy text "B" inside the Godot editor 4. Check the clipboard history, still shows "A" in the list 5. Copy text "C" outside Godot 6. Check the clipboard history, now shows "C", "A" in the list (ordered chronologically) 7. Copy text "D" inside the Godot editor 8. Check the clipboard history, now shows "D", "C", "A" in the list 9. Copy text "E" inside the Godot editor 10. Check the clipboard history, shows "D", "C", "A" in the list Despite not being logged, pasting still works properly (according to the example scenario, it will paste "E") Using X11 (set `run/platforms/linuxbsd/prefer_wayland` to false) does not seem to have this issue. Not sure if this is reproducible in other clipboard managers or DEs Steps to reproduce 1. Launch the editor with native Wayland 2. Copy any text in the editor 3. Open the clipboard history and observe that the copied text is logged 4. Copy different text in the editor again 5. Open the clipboard history again, and observe that the second copy is not logged. title: Wayland: Scale pan delta
body: The documentation says that `wl_pointer::axis` uses surface-local units, i.e. unscaled units, so we need to scale them. --- NOTE: This is under the assumption that the expected unit for gestures is the pixel. See: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116499issuecomment-3930881219

title: Fix EditorSettings error due to `android_sdk_path` when exporting a project
body: This editor setting is queried once every 3 seconds, as it is used to check whether devices have been (dis)connected for one-click deploy. This method may be called early on depending on initialization order, which has led to occasional error messages when exporting a project. The method now returns early if EditorSettings isn't ready yet (it will be called again soon after anyway). This should fix spurious CI failures that have occurred since https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109146 was merged. PS: It seems we're performing the `adb devices` polling even when the editor isn't currently focused. This wastes CPU, as the user can't interact with the one-click deploy dropdown if they aren't currently focused on the editor. We should probably early return if we are currently not focused, but I figure I'd leave this to a future PR as to avoid any possible regression here.

title: Tests: Tidy up compiled tests
body: - Follow-up to 112041 - Follow-up to 116486 Now that all the non-module tests are migrated to implementation files, it's now feasible to make slightly more involved changes: 1. Only define `TESTS_ENABLED` where it's absolutely necessary. With slightly more generous forward-declares, the only remaining spots in the repo that use this define are implementation files in `main` and `modules/gdscript`. Previously we were passing this to *literally every source file*, which was a bit excessive 2. Declare doctest config defines in `test_macros.h`. This is cleaner than trying to pass them as compilation arguments, as this file is acting as a wrapper for `doctest.h` already. As such, we don't need to declare these in individual `SCsub` files and hope that every module is perfectly synced with our main testing `SCsub` 3. Created dedicated header/impl files for `SignalWatcher`; moved the relevant macros to that header. 4. Moved the relevant `DisplayServerMock` macros to `display_server_mock.h`, and migrated even more functions to the implementation file. 5. Slimmed down includes in `test_macros.h`, and consequently added the relevant includes to individual files when relevant. Made this an IWYU pass as well, with unnecessary includes being removed alongside an occasional `IWYU pragma:`. 6. Fixed typo (`WEB_EMABLED` → `WEB_ENABLED`) 😅

title: Fix scroll bar unable to use full area with custom stylebox types
body: Fixes 116508 Simply changes scrollbar's area size along the orientation to be based on the `StyleBox` margins instead of min size. This allows grabber size/positioning to be controlled by the properties in the Content Margins group which is independent from min size for custom `StyleBox` types. The `StyleBox` types in the engine in will behave the same since their min sizes are the margins. https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/897172fa25699455f0a537f054654068b3dda5d5/scene/resources/style_box.cppL35-L48 From what I could tell, the intention was that the scroll bar area was constrained by the margins since these lines had been this way since the first commit and, at that point, min size was always based on margins. However, min size was made independent for custom types as of 71686 , so this change should get it closer to how it worked before. <!-- Please target the `master` branch in priority. Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers. To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html -->

title: Unable to use full area of ScrollBar with custom StyleBox type that has a min size
body: Tested versions Reproducable: - v4.5.1.stable.mono.official [f62fdbde1] - v4.7.dev.mono.custom_build [bf95b6258] System information Godot v4.5.1.stable.mono - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Mobile) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2060 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.5599) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-10750H CPU @ 2.60GHz (12 threads) - 15.76 GiB memory Issue description When writing custom `StyleBox` types, scrollbars use the background's min size to determine the grabber's size instead of the content margins. This reduces control over grabber positioning/sizing since is always at least background min size smaller than the background and cannot cover the entire background when content margins are set to 0. A more concrete thing this issue prevents is making a modified version of `StyleBoxTexture` since a user either gets to define the min size based on texture margins or allow the grabber to cover the background, but not both. In the picture, the scrollbar is set to max value, but can only get to 100 from the bottom of the background since the min height is 100. Steps to reproduce - Make a custom `StyleBox` type - Have it override `_get_minimum_size()` to be something non-zero - Notice when the custom type is used in a `ScrollBar`'s `scroll` theme property, the grabber is more contrained than when using `StyleBoxTexture` with the same min size Minimal reproduction project (MRP) scrollbar with custom stylebox.zip -Scroll the scrollbar or change the value property in editor and notice how the grabber cannot reach the bottom content margin as long as the custom style box's minimum size is larger.

title: Fix TabContainer tab icon, disabled, and hidden not saved before ready
body: Fixes 116358 `TabContainer` stores tab properties (icon, disabled, hidden) in a temporary buffer `pending_tabs` before the node receives `NOTIFICATION_READY`, then flushes them into the internal `tab_bar`, the setters (`set_tab_icon`, `set_tab_disabled`, `set_tab_hidden`) correctly wrote to `pending_tabs`, but the getters (`get_tab_icon`, `is_tab_disabled`, `is_tab_hidden`) always read from `tab_bar`, which is empty before ready. since `PropertyListHelper` calls the getter during serialization and skips saving any property that matches its default value, these properties were never written to the scene file and lost on reload. this PR makes the three getters check `!is_ready()` first, if the node isn't ready and the index is within `pending_tabs` bounds, they return the buffered value instead of reading from `tab_bar`.

title: OpenXR depth submission broken when using MSAA with Forward+
body: Tested versions - Reproduceable in all versions, including 4.6 System information Godot v4.6.1.mrlink3.mono (3993b4005) - Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Direct3D 12 (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.9186) - 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-13900K (32 threads) - 127.7 GiB memory Issue description There is currently a bug when using OpenXR with "Submit Depth Buffer" enabled while MSAA is enabled with the Forward+ renderer. The issue is that RendererViewport will use `texture_storage->render_target_set_override` to set overrides for the color and depth buffers for the renderer to render into. Without MSAA, this is fine because the color/depth textures are created externally by the OpenXR runtime and they have the correct usage flags to render into. But with MSAA enabled, Forward+ uses special compute resolve shaders and thus required `STORAGE` usage which the OpenXR module does not enable. The OpenXR module doesn't know whether to create a depth swapchain for `STORAGE` or `DEPTH_STENCIL` because it doesn't know whether the override will result in being resolved into (requiring `STORAGE`) or rendered directly into (requiring `DEPTH_STENCIL`). I have investigated a number of solutions: 1. Modify the OpenXR module to create depth textures with the `COMPUTE` `usage_flag` base on if the viewport has MSAA enabled for 3D. This would also be specific for Forward+ since the mobile renderer has worked around this problem (for Vulkan only) by using a Vulkan resolve feature that allows it to natively resolve depth textures. I've identified two ways this can be done: 1. Simple way: Key off the project settings to determine if MSAA is enabled. The flaw is this breaks if the application toggles MSAA dynamically (my product built on Godot will do this because we will have different rendering quality settings). 2. Complicated way: Look up the current MSAA setting for the viewport or render target and recreate `XrSwapchains` as needed with compatible usage flags. The OpenXR module is passed the `render_target` `RID`, so additional plumbing is needed so that `RenderTarget` can expose the MSAA 3D setting (it already exposes the MSAA 2D setting). 1. My prototype implementation is here: https://github.com/brycehutchings/godot/pull/6 2. Create raster shader equivalents of the depth resolve compute shaders and pick based on the render target. There actually are already raster equivalent shaders for depth resolve that are available and currently going unused. The problem is the `VOXEL_GI_RESOLVE` variant which would have to be reworked as a raster shader, and I haven't looked into if that is feasible. I presume compute shaders are used for a reason though, otherwise if Forward+ could migrate to raster shaders then that also solves the problem and avoids forked logic. 3. Add support for other graphics APIs to support depth resolve (like D3D12). I have actually implemented this as well, and found it does not work because D3D12 is stricter than Vulkan and doesn't like resolving from a depth buffer with a stencil aspect to a depth buffer without stencil. 4. Have Forward+ ignore the depth override if it is incompatible and do a blit at the end. Presumably this has some extra overhead though. I have prototypes of 1a, 1b, 2 (without support for GI resolve though), and 3 (which ended up not working). For my team's product, 1b is what I plan to use because we need the dynamic support and it brings parity to Unity which we were using before, but I can contribute any of 1a/1b/2 if any of them are in the direction that the Godot maintainers would prefer. Steps to reproduce Using a minimal XR project on PC with Forward+ renderer, enable "Submit Depth Buffer" and use a runtime that supports it. Observe that the submitted texture is always 0'd. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) xr-msaa.zip This will use GDScript to toggle MSAA 3D on/off every 2 seconds. It has OpenXR enabled, Depth Submission enabled, and uses Forward+.

title: Extract DocumentList from ScriptEditor
body: Extract DocumentList from ScriptEditor - Built on https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111309 ( I wouldn't refactor without this due to risk of infinite `tree_changed` signals.) - Similar to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/115346 Fixed display full paths mode not showing unsaved status. Fixed Online Docs button text not updating when using history arrows Should work the same otherwise, and should be much easier to work with in the future. Editor Settings are now cached instead of gotten each time it updates (sometimes multiple times). Moved some file menu handling since it was needlessly duplicated. Removed the "nodes" drop type, it is unused, it is not possible to drag the EditorHelp or ScriptEditorBase directly (since always I think). I left the rest of the drag and drop logic unchanged, it can be improved later. There are some pre-existing minor issues like dropping files never goes to the end of the list or sometimes not selecting the right child after dropping. Moved the `(*)` unsaved suffix from `get_name` to `update_list`. It had to be trimmed off in most cases, and the other uses are just messages where it isn't needed. Removed the `grab_focus_block`, it was not functional and seemed to be hacky. It was probably to prevent the TextEdit from taking the focus if using the keyboard on the script list. If it is wanted, it can be re-implemented later. Moved various updating logic to `ScriptEditor::ensure_select_current` or `_update_filenames` to have it unified rather than scattered around where sometimes things aren't updated. Now other things aren't updated from the document list update function.

title: OpenXR Action Map binding cannot be removed when the action is renamed
body: Tested versions Godot 4.6.stable System information Godot v4.6.stable unknown - CachyOS Linux 1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Mon, 16 Feb 2026 20:41:55 +0000 on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Mobile) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3080 (nvidia; 580.126.18) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-10700K CPU @ 3.80GHz (16 threads) - 31.19 GiB memory Issue description Pressing the garbage can icon next to a binding to remove it, will not work, with the error if the internal name for the corresponding action has been renamed. The error comes from where it tries to look up the action in the action map based on the name. There are several work-arounds: - Saving the OpenXR action map propagates the rename - Adding a new binding also propagates the rename - Manually renaming the binding also allows deleting the binding again. It's not a significant bug, but I figured it'd be good to at least have the issue and its work-arounds documented here, as it caught me off-guard. I think the fix would be to have the rename propagated as soon as the action is renamed, instead of requiring a save first. Steps to reproduce This can be done in an empty project 1. Enable OpenXR; Project > Project Settings > XR > OpenXR > Enabled checked. Save and Restart 2. In the OpenXR Action Map tab, Add action with the plus button. Give it some name 3. In profile (e.g.: Simple Controller), add the binding to one of the buttons (e.g.: Menu click) 4. Back in the Action Sets tab, rename the action to something else 5. Go back to the profile where you created the binding and try to press the garbage can button The binding remains and the Output log shows Minimal reproduction project (MRP) OpenXRActionRenameBugProjectFiles.zip An empty project with OpenXR enabled, and a minimal action map. Can also be created with the steps described above.

title: Editor: Only force CSV text for translation

title: Embed window `suspend/pause` doesn't stop awaited coroutines
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in v4.6.1.stable.mono.official [14d19694e] System information Windows 10 - Foward+ - Nividia 1070 Issue description The pause/suspend button of embed windows doesn't seem to affect **await coroutines.** Here is a basic example. After pausing the game, the coroutine keeps running and executing the code. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fdf12a6e-ebe9-4aed-871b-2b5e86df82b6 Here is a more real example of how bad this can become. The object stops but the camera keeps shaking even though the embed window is paused. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fdf2fd5d-4d7a-4e81-bc42-8a0fbac8f542 **Not sure if this is a limitation of the embed window or a bug that no one noticed yet.** But if your game depends on coroutines, the pause/step function doesn't quite work as a debug tool. As it'll give very inconsistent results and break a lot of things in the game when stepping frame by frame. **Note:** Using `await get_tree().create_timer(0.05).timeout` instead of `await get_tree().process_frame` would **kinda** work. It seems the suspend/pause stops the timer creation/update, so the code would stop when it encounters that. But would still execute until that point when paused. Steps to reproduce Steps: 1. Create a code with a long coroutine (multiple frames, eg: await get_tree().process_frame) 2. Execute the game with the embed menu 3. Pause/Suspend the embed game window while the coroutine is still running **Result:** Coroutine keeps running even tho embed window is paused **Expected Result:** Coroutine paused like process function Minimal reproduction project (MRP) MRP Steps: 1. Execute the project 2. Pause the game window **Results:** Camera keeps shaking **Expected**: Camera shake stops suspend embed.zip

title: X11 on Gnome issue with non-integer scaling causes main viewport render at higher resolution
body: Tested versions - Godot 4.6, Linux - Godot 4.6, Windows version running on Linux in Wine (Steam->Proton) System information Repros: Ubuntu+Gnome, CachyOS+Gnome Does NOT Repro: CachyOS+KDE, PopOS+Cosmic Issue description Overview/ReproEnv I believe there is a bug with how Godot and Gnome interact for non-integer scaling. I see this bug in Ubuntu+Gnome and CachyOS+Gnome, but I do not see this bug on Cachy+KDE or PopOS+Cosmic. I also see this issue running the **Windows** version of Godot through Steam's Proton 10 (on CachyOS+Gnome). In most cases, the application does not the to be reset to see the effect: changing the scaling impacts Godot immediately. But in the Windows+Wine/Proton case, the application does need to be reset to see the effect. The issue seems to be an Gnome+X11 issue. Forcing Wayland (with `display/display_server/driver.linuxbsd` in settings) on Gnome fixes the issue. But I still cannot repro on PopOS+Cosmic or Cachy+KDE by forcing X11. I believe Proton/Wine defaults to X11, but I did not verify that. I expect this is reading some environment variable, which would help explain why it is reproducible with the Windows version running in Wine. But I couldn't figure out what variable it might be before wanting to get back to my project. Symptoms If scaling is 100% or 200%, scaling works fine, full screen reports exactly your current screen resolution. Fullscreen (F11) should report your screen resolution. But 125% it will report 1.6x your resolution and 166% scaling it will report 1.2x resolution. Why is this important? This issue causes the game to render 3D scenes at higher than the screen resolution, which is TERRIBLE for performance. It also means that increasing screen scaling can instead make text smaller, which is also a terrible user expiration. Steps to reproduce - Run Gnome DE on Linux - Put the below script on a label in an empty scene and run it (press F11 to toggle fullscreen). - Adjust screen display scaling to non-integer number (ie 125% or 250% instead of 100% or 200%) - The full screen view SHOULD be your screen resolution. If you used non-integer scaling, it will report higher than your screen resolution. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) Add this script to a label on ANY project:

title: Fix GPUParticles3D aligning to imperceptible velocities
body: Fixes 95715, by altering the requirement of `PARTICLE_FLAG_ALIGN_Y_TO_VELOCITY` to not trigger on imperceptible movements. The rotation is caused by the `PARTICLE_FLAG_ALIGN_Y_TO_VELOCITY` flag. It rotates the particle in the direction of its current velocity. It applies this based on if velocity is larger than 0.0 When the particles appear stationary micro adjustments actually occur to keep their movement in the direction of the collision normal 0. These adjustment are small but not perfectly zero. This then triggers the Align to velocity portion of the particle code. The other solution would be to clamp velocity back to 0.0 but I believe this would cause the same issue that 87320 tried to solve. Before: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f4b14819-5fcd-44d4-a883-aa84482f61e2 After: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8dd6a121-0afb-4c1a-b2fe-389b41f8276f godot-particle-shimmer-mrp.zip

title: Fix GPUParticles2D trails not aligning to velocity
body: Fixes godotengine/godot89449 Hello! This is my first contribution to Godot, feedback is welcome. Problem When GPUParticles2D trails are used with radial/orbit/directional velocities, the trails don't align to the direction of motion. This was caused by the engine forcibly applying `PARTICLES_TRANSFORM_ALIGN_Y_TO_VELOCITY` when trails were enabled, which overwrote the process shader's velocity alignment with an incomplete cached velocity. As a result, the radial/orbit/directional velocity components were ignored when determining alignment. Solution Remove the forced `PARTICLES_TRANSFORM_ALIGN_Y_TO_VELOCITY` in `GPUParticles2D::set_trail_enabled`. Trails now respect the process shaders `Align Y` particle flag, making the trail behavior consistent with GPUParticles3D. Breaks compatibility Projects relying on automatic y-alignment for GPUParticles2D trails will need to enable the `Align Y` particle flag on their process shader when previously they didn't. Testing - Verified that 2D trails can now align with particles when they have animated velocities, and that toggling the flag turns alignment on/off for trails. - Here's how the fix behaves in my minimal repro project https://github.com/heyomidk/godot-minrepo-89449: fix-89449

title: CanvasItem::draw_ellipse() has mis-named 'major' and 'minor' parameters
body: Tested versions - Present in: 4.6.stable - Not Applicable to versions prior to 4.6 (method(s) in question introduced in 4.6) System information Godot v4.6.stable - macOS Sonoma (14.0.0) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Metal (Forward+) - integrated Apple M1 (Apple7) - Apple M1 (8 threads) - 16.00 GiB memory Issue description The `CanvasItem::draw_ellipse()` 2nd and 3rd parameters are named in the source code as `major` and `minor`, and described in the documentation as the "semi-major axis" and "semi-minor axis". However, how they are coded and how they behave are as the X-direction axis and the Y-direction axis. In geometry, the major axis of an ellipse is its longest diameter. Correspondingly, the semi-major axis is the longest semidiameter; and, the minor is the shortest. e.g., both `draw_ellipse(..., 20, 10, ...)` and `draw_ellipse(..., 10, 20, ...)` draw an ellipse with a semi-major axis of 20. The first has its major axis in the X-direction ("squashed") and the second has its major axis in the Y-direction ("tall"). Thus, the parameter names would be better as something like "x_axis" and "y_axis", or similar. Throughout its API, Godot (naturally) relies on knowledge, references, concepts, and metaphors from geometry. People approaching `draw_ellipse()` with a background in geometry will be misled by both the parameter names and how they are described in the API documentation. People approaching geometry with a background in working in Godot will be mistaken about what a 'major' and 'minor' axis of an ellipse are. (This same observation applies to `draw_ellipse_arc()`, and to corresponding methods in `RenderServer`, and anywhere else that is tightly associated with these `CanvasItem` methods.) Steps to reproduce On a 500x500 `Control`, issue the drawing commands: Observe that both ellipses have the same major- and minor-axis sizes. To check the definition of major and minor axis in this context, see Wikipedia Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A - This issue is not project-dependent. This is an issue with the parameter names as written into the source code, and how they are therefore described in the API documentation.

title: Wayland: Skip resize request when the size is the same
body: Fixes 116304. Follow up to 114082. `DisplayServer::window_set_size` is called lots of times in the code, with the assumption (I suppose) that it's going to be idempotent. We had checks in `_update_window_rect` but we still called `WaylandThread::window_set_size`, which did a lot of stuff. In particular, this caused issues with HiDPI as it "overrode" the window size before it had a time to figure out its scale. --- Tested on: - Fedora 43 VM with COSMIC 1.0.1, GNOME 49.2, KDE 6.5.4 - KISS Linux with labwc 0.9.3-49-g654da8c8 (built from git), sway 1.11. Both with and without 116308.

title: Fix Tool Script behaviors requiring restart.
body: Primarily authored by @Hilderin, superseding https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/92099 `@tool` Scripts have several commonly reported issues derived from only being soft-reloaded upon modification. * Adding `@tool` to a script already attached to a node will not add in-editor behavior until restart. * Modifying an existing tool script to behave differently will not change until restart. * Modifying an existing tool script's variables will cause a soft-reload that can result in bad memory access, including a crash which I suspect is under-reported. This PR resolves these issues by forcing hard-reloads of a script when `@tool` is added or removed, as well as when any existing tool script is modified. This is only a minor, 1-line adjustment to the original PR, adding the extra case of hard-reload upon modification, and is otherwise identical. I have rebased onto Master, and tested the fix, it appears to still behave correctly. Based on my testing, this should resolve: * Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/66381 * Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/114485 (POSSIBLE CRASH) * Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115187 * Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/103591 Should also revert the Docs warning about Issue 66381 : https://github.com/godotengine/godot-docs/pull/10544

title: Core: Add validation for property bindings
body: * Smaller version of 77618. * See also 116237. CC @ashwinvbs Godot uses template magic for method binding in most cases, eliminating unnecessary boilerplate. However, for property binding, you must specify the full `PropertyInfo`. This has led to a large accumulation of binding errors. In this PR, I've commented out enum/bitfield validation, as it requires a significant number of boilerplate changes. I've also removed helpers like `make_enum()`, which I proposed in 77618, and correspondingly removed stylistic changes. This PR only fixes the incorrect bindings. I think in the future we should make `ADD_PROPERTY` variadic and change the signature of `ClassDB::add_property()` to avoid boilerplate binding of properties. The main problem is that `PropertyInfo` combines information about the property's data type (which we can obtain from the return value of the getter and/or the setter parameter) and additional metadata for the editor. I would propose the following signature for `ADD_PROPERTY()`: Thus, `ClassDB::add_property()` combines `PropertyInfo` with property type information obtained from the setter/getter `MethodBind` and `PropertyInfo` with additional metadata specified manually. However, this is material for a separate PR, which will likely not be included in 4.7. The goal of this PR is to fix the most serious errors with property bindings within the existing system. <details> <summary>List of changes</summary> </details>

title: Reordering `AnimatedSprite2D` involuntarily changes `animation` to copy whatever `AnimatedSprite2D` is open in the inspector
body: Tested versions - Reproducible in v4.7.dev1.official [bf95b6258] IIRC this has been an issue for a quite a while though. System information Godot v4.7.dev1 - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 3 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1070 Ti (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.8228) - AMD Ryzen 5 1500X Quad-Core Processor (8 threads) - 15.92 GiB memory Issue description While reordering `AnimatedSprite2D` nodes in a scene that contains multiple of them, often I will find that doing so changes `animation` without my consent. This can even result in `animation` being assigned the name of a nonexistent animation. This additionally does not create an undo action, perhaps as an extension of how the SpriteFrames bottom panel doesn't seem to create undo actions at all (which might be worthy of its own issue if it doesn't already exist). Similarly, this doesn't happen when selecting multiple nodes or when either `AnimatedSprite2D` doesn't have a `SpriteFrames` resource, probably because the SpriteFrames bottom panel is disabled in both of those cases. The reordered `AnimatedSprite2D` copies the animation of whatever node is open in the inspector, if that node is also an `AnimatedSprite2D`. This usually occurs in the following two situations: - Dragging a node without first selecting it to focus it in the inspector. - Hovering a node while dragging long enough to focus it in the inspector (quite often happens immediately before dropping, if the mouse isn't moving at that point). Of note is that this specifically occurs when the `AnimatedSprite2D` is *reordered.* If it's just reparented without specifically moving it afterward, `animation` stays the same. Steps to reproduce 1. Add two `AnimatedSprite2D` nodes to the scene, ensuring that they both have a valid `sprite_frames`, and that their `animation` values are different. 2. Reorder one of the nodes in a way such that the other `AnimatedSprite2D` is open in the inspector when the first node is dropped. 3. The dropped `AnimatedSprite2D` should now have a different (possibly invalid) `animation`. Minimal reproduction project (MRP) issue.zip
